segments.s662-g353: وز (a poetic contraction equivalent to و + از) introduces a source/starting point; آنجا is the place adverb 'there'; بیامد is past 3rd‑singular 'came'; سوی + place (طیسفون) is a postverbal directional phrase 'toward Ctesiphon'. The clause is a simple past event with the direction following the verb, a common poetic word order.
segments.s662-g354: This is a verbless apposition or continuation describing the army: سپاهی ('an army' or 'troops') followed by the paired locative/adverbial words پس پشت و پیش 'rear and front' and the locative اندرون 'inside/within'. In Persian narrative such noun phrases can follow a clause to qualify or enumerate the state/location of the subject just mentioned.
segments.s662-g355: همه یکسر is an emphatic phrase 'all completely/entirely'; آباد here is predicative/adjectival 'prosperous' or 'adorned'; از introduces the material/means 'of/with' (saying they are of silver and gold). The phrase states the general appearance/material of the people/places just mentioned.
segments.s662-g356: به is used here as a preposition 'in/with' (repeated for parallelism); زرین is the adjective 'golden' modifying the two nouns that follow. ستام appears as a noun modified by 'زرین' (likely a garment or ornament in context); the second half repeats the pattern for کمر 'waist/girdle' (i.e. belts). This is a parallel descriptive construction listing items ornamented with gold.
segments.s662-g357: ز (here 'because of' / 'from') introduces the cause: بس (an intensifier 'so much/so many') + پرنیانی 'silken' qualifies درفش 'standard/banner'; سران 'of the chiefs' is the genitive/possessive. The phrase gives a causal ground for the following judgement.
segments.s662-g358: تو گفتی is the reporting clause 'you said' (تو = 'you' + گفتی = 'said'); هوا شد 'the air became' uses شد as the past copula 'became'; همه پرنیان is the predicate 'entirely/sheerly silken' (همه intensifies). Grammatically this is a reported clause: 'you said that the atmosphere had become all silk.'
segments.s662-g359: در و دشت ('court/palace and plain/field') is a conventional pair meaning 'in town and country'; گفتی 'you said' introduces the reported content; که is the subordinating 'that'; زرین شدست uses the archaic/poetic form شدست (equivalent to شده است) meaning 'has become/was made golden'. The clause reports the observation that places had grown golden.
segments.s662-g360: کمرها (plural 'the belts/girdles') is the subject; ز گوهر 'from/with jewels' indicates material or decoration; چو پروین 'like Parvin' is a simile (Parvin = the Pleiades, a shining star‑group) and شدست 'became' closes the clause. The structure says the belts, set with jewels, shone like Parvin.
segments.s662-g209: فرستاده گفت is a simple past-report clause: 'the messenger said' (subject + گفت, past). این ندارم is first-person present/near-present نَدارَم ('I do not have' / 'I will not accept' / 'I will not endure this'), with این ('this') as the direct object. به رنج is a prepositional phrase 'with/by hardship' (به + رنج) specifying the manner or cost the speaker would otherwise have to bear.
segments.s662-g210: که introduces a causal clause here meaning 'because/since'. کوتاه کردی is past tense (کردی, 'you made/you did') with کوتاه as adjective/verb complement 'short' → 'you shortened'. م + را should be read together as مرا (object pronoun 'me'); the segmentation in the edition splits it, but grammatically it is the accusative 'me'. راه گنج is a noun phrase 'the way/road to the treasure' (راه = path, گنج = treasure).
segments.s662-g211: بیامد is past tense 'he came'. بر here marks direction/target 'to' (بر + direct object), so بر مرد دانا به شب = 'he came to the wise man at night'. به شب is a temporal phrase 'at night' (به used as a temporal preposition).
segments.s662-g212: وزان is a connective 'and/from that' linking this action to the prior clause ('thereupon' / 'from that'). کفشگر نیز = 'the shoemaker, too' (کفشگر = shoemaker, نیز = also). بگشاد لب is a poetic past form: بَ‑ (verbal prefix) + گشاد (past stem of گشودن) meaning 'opened', and لب here means 'lip' → idiomatically 'opened his mouth' (i.e., began to speak).
segments.s662-g213: برشاه شد شاد is a compact past-clause: برشاه ('before/to the king' = in the king's presence), شد شاد = 'became glad' (شد, past 'became', + adjective شاد). بوزرجمهر is the subject (proper name) placed after the predicate for poetic order.
segments.s662-g214: بران 'thereupon' (a temporal/conclusive particle). خواسته شاه is a genitive construction 'the king's request' or 'at the king's bidding' (خواسته as noun/past participle = 'request/what was asked'). بگشاد is the past verbal form 'he opened/unveiled', and چهر here means 'face/appearance' → 'he uncovered his face' (i.e., revealed himself/removed a veil).
segments.s662-g215: چنین گفتن زان پس is a formulaic/poetic sequencing: چنین گفتن ('to say thus' / 'he said thus' — the infinitive used here in a narrative sense), زان پس 'thereafter'. که introduces the content clause 'that...' and یزدان سپاس is 'praise/thanks to God' (یزدان = God, سپاس = praise/thanks).
segments.s662-g216: مبادم is a negative optative 'may it not be / may I not be' (expressing a wish that something not happen), followed by مگر meaning 'unless'. پاک و یزدان‌شناس is the exception condition: پاک = 'pure' and یزدان‌شناس = 'one who knows/acknowledges God' (i.e., pious/God‑knowing). Together: 'May I not praise God unless I am pure and God‑knowing.'
segments.s662-g769: فراوان is an adverb here meaning 'in large numbers' (modifies the following clause). بران is a deictic prepositional/adverbial item 'on/over that' (pointing back to a previously mentioned letter) and combines with نامه ('letter') to mark what people wept about. هرکس is an indefinite noun phrase 'each person / everyone' functioning as the subject of گریست. گریست is the past form of گریستن ('to weep'); word order is adverb + adjunct + subject + verb (typical classical Persian).
segments.s662-g770: پس از is a prepositional expression meaning 'after' (پس + از); here it introduces a temporal phrase 'پس از عهد یک سال' = 'after the period of one year'. عهد is used in the temporal sense 'period/epoch' and سال specifies the length. دیگر here functions as 'again/another time' (classical use) and بزیست is the past form of زیستن with the archaic prefix ب- (classical narrative past) meaning 'he lived' or 'came to life' (i.e. lived again).
segments.s662-g771: برفت is a past-tense verb 'he went away/ departed' formed from رفتن with a prefixed ب- (common in classical Persian narrative); the provided lemma/senses list appears unrelated to this usage (so no matching sense). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. بماند is a present-subjunctive/preterite-stative form of ماندن here meaning 'remained' or 'was left' (it describes the continuing existence of what follows). این سخن is the demonstrative 'this saying/these words' (سخن = 'saying; speech; words') and یادگار is a noun 'memento / memorial' explaining what remained.
segments.s662-g772: تو is the vocative/subject 'you'. این یادگارش = 'this memorial of his / this memorial of it' (یادگار with the enclitic -ش = 'his/its'). بزنهار is an archaic/formal noun or verblike formula meaning roughly 'as a warning' or invoking 'znehâr' (warning); here it functions adverbially. دار is an imperative/inflected verb 'keep' or 'hold' in classical usage (here 'keep/hold this memorial as a warning for yourself'). The construction is an address: 'You, keep this memorial as a warning.' Note: some listed senses for دار in the wordlist are nominal and do not match the verbal imperative use here, so no direct sense-match for that sense list.
segments.s662-g773: کنون is an adverb 'now'. زین is a deictic/prepositional item 'from this / of this' in classical usage (the supplied senses list gives 'saddle/harness' which is a different lexical homograph; the meaning here is the demonstrative 'of this / from this point'). سپس is an adverb 'then/thereupon' that sequences the action. تاج هرمزد شاه is a noun phrase 'the crown of Hurmuzd (Ahura Mazda), O king' (تاج = crown; هرمزد names the deity; شاه is vocative or appositive 'king'). The sequence sets up the object to be acted on by the verb in the next segment.
segments.s662-g774: بیارایم is the first-person singular present/future-oriented form of آراستن with a prefixed بی- classical form meaning 'I will adorn / I will decorate'. و is 'and'. برنشانم is a prefixed classical verb (ب + نشانم) meaning 'I will set/seat (someone)' or 'I will place (it) on/into position' (object understood from context). بگاه is an adverbial noun meaning 'at the appropriate moment / at that time' and here modifies the verbal complex: 'I will adorn ... and I will seat/place (it) at the appointed time.'
segments.s662-g409: همان‌‌که (همان‌که) here introduces a causal/explanatory clause: “since / inasmuch as.” دل را is the definite object (دل + را) meaning “the heart” or “the courage/feeling,” followed by ندارم “I do not have.” The construction دل را ندارم به رنج literally places the object before the verb and the prepositional complement (به رنج, “to/for suffering”), giving “I do not have a heart for suffering” (i.e. I cannot endure suffering).
segments.s662-g410: اگر introduces a conditional (“if”). بگذرم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive form of گذر (بگذرم = “if I pass/leave” or “should I pass”); Persian often uses this subjunctive after اگر for a hypothetical. زین سرای سپنج is a poetic, compact phrase: زین (archaic/poetic = ‘from this’), سرای “house/dwelling,” سپنج “sponge” — a metaphorical compound meaning a fragile, absorbent or ephemeral dwelling (= this world / mortal house).
segments.s662-g411: چه is the interrogative “what.” گوید is the third‑person singular present/presentive verb ‘says’ (here rendered as “can say” in English); in the data list the verbal sense labels are missing, so the verb sense is flagged separately. کنون “now” is a temporal adverb; مرد روشن‌روان is the noun plus epithet (‘a man of bright/clear soul’). Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect (verb + time + subject).
segments.s662-g412: ز is the preposition ‘from / about’ linking the following noun as the thing being spoken of. رای here means “opinion / counsel,” and جهاندار “the sovereign / world‑ruler” is in genitive relation to رای. نوشین روان is a two‑word epithet (نوشین “pleasant/delighting” modifying روان “soul/spirit”) describing the sovereign; the whole phrase means “from/about the counsel of the sweet‑souled sovereign.”
segments.s662-g413: چو (here written چوسال) + سال اندر آمد is a temporal clause: “when years came in / when he reached the years …”. بهفتاد و چار is the numeral “to seventy and four” (= seventy‑four); the verb آمد is past “came,” so the clause means “when he reached seventy‑four years.” (The tokenization in the list merges parts of the numeral, so the precise numeral item has no matching sense and is flagged accordingly.)
segments.s662-g414: پراندیشه is a predicate adjective meaning “full of thought / pensive.” مرگ names the topic of that thought (“about death”), and شد is the past of شدن “became.” شهریار is the subject placed at the end for poetic word order: “the king became pensive about death.”
segments.s662-g415: جهان راهی (here written جهان راهمی) is an epithet meaning a worldly traveler or “one who wanders the world.” کدخدایی بجست is past tense: بجست = “sought” (بجست / جست = sought, sought office); کدخدایی is the noun “headmanship / the post of kadkhoda (village‑head),” so the clause says he sought that office. The compound is typical of Shahnameh’s compressed phrasing.
segments.s662-g416: که introduces a relative clause modifying the sought post (“who / such that …”). پیراهن داد is literally “gave a shirt/robe” (داد = gave), and پوشد نخست (پوشد = he put on / wore; نخست = first) completes the idea: the relative clause describes someone “who first put on the robe that was given” — the line is compact/elliptical and can be read as either “who, when given a robe, was the first to put it on” or “who was the first to don the robe (that he gave/received).”
segments.s662-g321: همه سر به سر is an idiomatic intensifier meaning “entirely / wholly.” سر به سر is a reduplicated phrase where سر (literally “head/top”) + به (preposition) + سر form an adverbial unit ‘completely.’ خاک رنج is a genitive-like noun phrase “the dust/earth of (your) suffering/hardship,” and توایم is a classical/poetic contraction meaning “I am yours” (here used as a devoted first-person statement). Word order is emphatic: the intensifier همه سر به سر fronted, then the noun phrase, then the predicate-possession token توایم.
segments.s662-g322: همه marks totality “all/everyone.” پاسبانان گنج is a noun phrase “guards/keepers of the treasure.” The final توایم repeats the classical possessed‑copula “I/we am/are yours,” so the whole clause states devotion/servitude: “we are all the treasure’s guardians.” Word order is noun phrase → predicate (possessive copula).
segments.s662-g323: چوخشنود is a fixed adjective/adverbial compound meaning “very/most pleased.” گردد is the classical third‑person subjunctive/jussive of گشتن/گردیدن meaning “may he/it become” (a polite optative here). ز ما governs the object of pleasure “with/us” (i.e. ‘pleased with us’). شهریار is the subject ‘the king.’ The clause therefore expresses a wish/request: “May the king be very pleased with us.”
segments.s662-g324: نباشیم is the negative subjunctive/optative first‑person plural of بودن (let us not be / may we not be). ناکام و بد روزگار is a compound predicate: ناکام “unsuccessful/thwarted” and بد روزگار “ill‑fated / in bad times.” The clause continues the wish from the previous segment: ‘may we not be thwarted and ill‑fated.’
segments.s662-g325: ز introduces the causal/source relation “because of / from.” رنجی is an indefinite/partitive form of رنج meaning “a hardship/suffering.” که is the relative pronoun linking the clause to شهنشاه; ایدر is the archaic/poetic adverb “here.” شهنشاه is the subject “the shahanshah / emperor.” برد is a classical past/pf. verb meaning “bore / endured / took” (here ‘endured’). The whole relative clause modifies رنجی: “the hardship that the shahanshah endured here.”
segments.s662-g326: همه at the start can be read as universal quantifier but here supports a negative sense in the clause that follows: همه رومیان آن ندارند خرد = “not all the Romans have that wisdom” (classical Persian often places همه with a following negated verb to mean ‘not all’). رومیان is the people “the Romans,” آن is the demonstrative “that,” ندارند is the present plural of داشتن “they do not have,” and خرد means “wisdom/understanding.”
segments.s662-g327: ز marks source/contents here: “(made) of / from dinars.” دینار is the coin; پرکرده is a participial adjective “filled” (i.e. stuffed full). ده (present in the line though not listed among the words) is the numeral “ten,” modifying چرم گاو “cowhides” (چرم گاو = cow leather/hides). The phrase thus describes ten cowhides filled with dinars. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: source → adjective → numeral + noun.
segments.s662-g328: به گنج آوریم uses آوریم (first‑person plural present/subjunctive) “we bring / let us bring” with به گنج “into the treasury” as the goal. از introduces the origin “from,” درباژ is a locative “the gate/entrance,” and وساو is a conjunction/particle meaning “and such / and the like.” The full clause says that these filled hides are brought into the treasury from the gate and similar places.
segments.s662-g433: "به بوزرجمهر آن زمان شاه گفت" places the indirect object first (به بوزرجمهر = 'to Bozorgmehr'), then a temporal phrase (آن زمان = 'at that time'), then the subject (شاه = 'the king') and the verb گفت ('said'). Note the old‑style word order: indirect object + time + subject + verb; گفت is the past reporting verb introducing the following clause.
segments.s662-g434: "که رازی همی‌داشتم در نهفت" is a subordinate content clause introduced by که ('that'). همی‌داشتم is an older imperfect/past habitual form meaning 'I had/kept' (first person), and در نهفت is a prepositional phrase 'hidden inside' modifying رازی ('a secret'). The clause as a whole reports what the king said.
segments.s662-g435: "ز هفتاد چون سالیان درگذشت" is a temporal clause: ز (poetic 'from/after') + هفتاد سالیان ('seventy years') with چون giving the sense 'when/after', and درگذشت used here in the sense of 'passed' (the passing of years). The cluster marks that a long period has elapsed before what follows.
segments.s662-g436: "سر و موی مشکین چو کافور گشت" has the coordinated subject 'سر و موی' ('head and hair'), مشکین ('dark/dusky') describing their prior colour, چو ('like/as') introducing the simile, کافور ('camphor') as the comparison, and گشت (past of گشتن) meaning 'became/turned'. Word order is subject → descriptive phrase → simile → verb.
segments.s662-g437: "چومن بگذرم زین سپنجی سرای" is elliptical/poetic: چو من ('when/as for me') + بگذرم (1st person subjunctive/finite 'I pass' or 'I shall pass') + زین ('from this') + سپنجی سرای ('spongy/ephemeral dwelling'). The phrase expresses 'when I leave this fragile house'. Note archaic compounding (چومن = چو من).
segments.s662-g438: "جهان رابباید یکی کدخدای" states the consequence: جهان ('the world') + رابباید (rā-bayād, 'must/needs to have') + یکی کدخدای ('one/a certain headman'). Word order is object/subject first then modal verb and complement: 'the world will need a single headman.'
segments.s662-g439: "که بخشایش آرد به درویش بر" is a relative clause beginning with که ('who/that'). بخشایش آرد literally 'brings forgiveness/generosity' where آرد is the present/subjunctive/optative form of آوردن used in narrative style ('may/does bring' or simply 'brings'), and به درویش بر ('toward the dervish') marks the beneficiary. The clause modifies کدخدای in the previous segment.
segments.s662-g440: "به بیگانه و مردم خویش بر" continues the list of beneficiaries: به بیگانه ('to/for the stranger') و مردم خویش ('and his own people') with خویش split as خوی + ش (kinship/root + third‑person possessive). The final بر repeats the directional/benefactive particle; together the segment completes the relative clause describing who the headman should be generous to.
segments.s662-g169: بدین شهرها = a prepositional/locative phrase 'in/around these towns' (بدین is an older/formal compound meaning 'in these' or 'to these'). گرد here denotes a person who goes about/round (a roaming/collecting agent) and ماهرکسست is a contracted poetic form of ماهر کس است = 'there is a skilful person'; the copula is fused into the compound, so the whole segment states the existence of a skilful roaming person in these towns.
segments.s662-g170: کسی کو = 'anyone who' (کسی = anyone; کو is an older/poetic relative/conditional marker here linking the relative clause). درم بیش دارد بدست = 'has more than a dirham in hand' with درم بیش = 'more than a dirham' and دارد = present 'has'; بدست = 'in (his) hand'. The clause modifies the preceding collector: he takes from anyone who has more than a dirham on hand.
segments.s662-g171: ز بازارگان و ز دهقان درم = 'from the merchants and from the peasants, (a) dirham' — a listing of sources (ز = poetic 'from'), بازارگان = merchants, دهقان = peasants/farmers, and درم names the small coin/amount. Word order is paratactic: two sources joined by و followed by the amount.
segments.s662-g172: اگر وام خواهی = 'if you want/ask for a loan' (اگر = if; وام = loan; خواهی = present/future 'you want/ask'). نگردد دژم = 'he/they do not become sullen' where ن- + گردد (negated form of شدن/گشتن here) means 'does not turn/become' and دژم = 'sullen/grim'; overall a conditional: if you seek a small loan, they will not respond with sullenness.
segments.s662-g173: بدین کار شد شاه همداستان = 'On this affair the king became/was of one mind' (بدین کار = 'concerning this affair'; شد = past 'became' or 'was made'; شاه = the king; همداستان = 'of the same mind/agreeing'). The sequence is topicalization of the matter followed by the copular verb and the subject.
segments.s662-g174: که دانای ایران بزد داستان = a subordinate clause introduced by که ('that/so that') meaning 'that the wise one of Iran told/recounted the tale'. دانای ایران = 'the wise (man) of Iran'; بزد داستان is a poetic phrasing meaning 'struck/told the tale' (here idiomatically 'recounted the story'). The clause gives what was agreed.
segments.s662-g175: فرستاده‌ای جست بوزرجمهر = 'he sent (a messenger) / he sent for Bozorgmehr' — فرستاده‌ای is a verbal/noun form indicating a sending or messenger and جست (past of جستن) = 'sought/asked for'; the personal name بوزرجمهر follows as the object. This is a concise poetic expression meaning 'he summoned Bozorgmehr'.
segments.s662-g176: خردمند و شادان دل و خوب‌چهر = three epithets in a row modifying Bozorgmehr: خردمند = 'wise', شادان دل = a two-word adjective 'joyful/cheerful of heart' (شادان modifying دل), and خوب‌چهر = 'fair‑faced/pleasant‑faced'. Such piled adjectives are typical in epic Persian description.
segments.s662-g329: بکمی وبیشیش is a paired adverbial phrase ('with a little and with much' / 'in small and great matters') placed before the predicate. فرمان رواست is a nominal predicate: فرمان (root 'command') + روا (an adjective/participle meaning 'fitting, ruling') and ست is the poetic contracted copula (classical/stylistic form of است) meaning 'is' — together functioning like فرمان‌رواست 'is a ruler' or 'rules'.
segments.s662-g330: پذیرد is third-person singular present (or predictive) 'accepts / will accept'. ز ما is the prepositional phrase 'from us' (ز is the poetic form of از). گرچه is the concessive conjunction 'although / though'. آن ناسزاست is a clause with آن 'that' + ناسزا 'insult/abusive word' + -ست (contracted است) 'is' — literally 'that is an insult.'
segments.s662-g331: چنین داد پرسخ (چنین داد پاسخ) = 'gave such an answer / answered thus' — چنین is an adverb 'thus', داد is the verb 'gave' and پاسخ is the object 'answer'. که introduces the content clause ('that...'). ازکار here is a fused prepositional expression 'از + کار' meaning 'by reason of / because of work' or 'earned through work'; گنج is the noun 'treasure' that is the topic of the subordinate clause.
segments.s662-g332: سزاوار is an adjective meaning 'deserving' or 'fit/appropriate (to)'; دستور is the noun 'order/arrangement' which is the thing deserved. باشد is the classical third-person subjunctive/optative or concessive form of 'to be' (here translated 'ought to be' / 'should be' / 'is to be'). به رنج is an adverbial phrase 'by/with toil' (به used here as 'by/with' + رنج 'hardship, toil'). Together: 'it deserves to be put in order by toil.'
segments.s662-g333: همه رومیان is the plural subject 'all/the Romans.' پیش موبد is a prepositional phrase 'before / in the presence of the mobed' (پیش here = 'in the presence of'). شدند is the past third-person plural of شدن 'to become / to go / to come' used here as a past action 'they went / came' (classical style).
segments.s662-g334: خروشان is a participial/adverbial form 'roaring / clamoring' describing how they came; و links the next phrase. با اختر بد is a prepositional phrase 'with an ill/unlucky star' (با = 'with', اختر = 'star/fortune', بد = 'bad/unfavorable'), i.e. 'under an unlucky omen.' شدند repeats the past verb and applies to the coordinated adverbial phrases (they came shouting and, under an ill star, were stricken/acted upon).
segments.s662-g335: فراوان is an adverb 'abundantly / plentifully' modifying the verb. ز هر در is a prepositional/idiomatic phrase 'from every door/gate' (ز = 'from', هر در = 'every door' — idiomatically 'from every quarter'). سخن راندند is a verbal construction where سخن 'word/speech' is the object and راندند (past of راندن) 'they drove' is used metaphorically = 'they uttered/spoke.'
segments.s662-g336: همه راز قیصر is a genitive phrase 'all the secrets of Caesar' (همه = 'all', راز = 'secret', قیصر = 'Caesar/emperor'). برو here is an adverbial/particle meaning 'away / off' (an older/poetic placement before the verb); راندند is the past 'they drove' so برو راندند = 'they drove away.' Overall: 'they drove away all of Caesar's secrets.'
segments.s662-g577: دگر آنک = “another, the one who” — دگر (another/other) introduces a different person; آنک is the relative/ demonstrative “that/ who” linking to the verb. پرسید is past tense “asked.” دشوار چیست is an inverted question: چی (what) + ست (is) yields “what is difficult?” — the copula ست follows the interrogative, a common classical/poetic order.
segments.s662-g578: بی‌آزار را دل = “the harmless one’s heart”: را here marks the preceding phrase as the topical/possessive nominal phrase (classical poetic use). پر آواز = a postpositive modifier: پر (full of) + آواز (voice/song) = “full of song/voice.” کیست = کی (who) + ست (is) = “who is it?” — the question asks for the identity of the harmless person whose heart is filled with song.
segments.s662-g579: چو here is the temporal/conditional binder “when” (or “as”). بد بود = “it/he was bad” (بد + بود, past). وبد ساز is a compound idea: وبد (and/bad) + ساز (maker/device) — in context a poetic agent meaning “an evil-maker” or “one who makes mischief.” با وی نشست = “sat with him” (با = with, وی = him/her, نشست = sat/settled).
segments.s662-g580: یکی زندگانی بود = “there was one/a certain life” where یکی functions as an indefinite “a certain” and بود is the past “was.” چون introduces a simile “like/as”; کبست is an archaic/obscure noun left untranslated here and used as the thing to which that life is compared (poetic vocabulary).
segments.s662-g581: دگر آنک = “another/the one who” as before. گوید is the (historic) present “says” (used in narration to report speech). گوا کیست راست is an interrogative: گوا (“gava” here = ‘witness’ or ‘testimony’) + کیست (“who is”) + راست (“true/real”) = “Who is a true witness?” — راست is a postpositive adjective meaning “true.”
segments.s662-g582: که introduces an explanatory clause “that/for.” جان وخرد = “life and wisdom” joined by the conjunction و. برگوا برگواست is a poetic/archaic phrase: برگوا appears to be a rare or corrupt/formulaic word repeated for effect and برگواست ends the clause with the copula -ست (“is”); the exact lexical sense is uncertain here, so the phrase is treated as an obscure poetic formula meaning something like ‘(is) ...’ repeated for emphasis.
segments.s662-g583: به از آزمایش = a comparative phrase “better than experience” (به از = better than; آزمایش = trial/experience). ندیدم is the first-person past negative “I have not seen / I saw not.” گوا here is the noun “witness/testimony” (the thing being compared). The whole line means “I have seen no witness better than experience.”
segments.s662-g584: گوای is the noun گوا with an -ی ending (appositive/attributive), introducing the apposition that follows. سخنگوی و فرمانروا = two nouns joined by و: سخنگوی (“spokesman/speaker”) and فرمانروا (“ruler/sovereign”). The phrase says that the witness (گوا) functions as both speaker and ruler — nouns in apposition following the head noun.
segments.s662-g737: This is an imperative instruction addressed to someone (a scribe or maker): نبشته is used as a verbal/nominal root meaning an inscription or 'write/inscribe', followed by the imperative verb برو 'go' — together functioning like 'go and inscribe'. بارگاه مرا is the direct object 'my palace' (بارگاه = palace/court); the enclitic/possessive element for 'my' is rendered in the line (archaic order), and the object marker را follows to mark the definite object. Word order here (verb phrase before object) is typical of older poetic Persian commands.
segments.s662-g738: A simple coordinated list of the things to be inscribed: بزرگی 'greatness/grandeur', و 'and', گنج 'treasure', و 'and', سپاه 'army/host'. The final grammatical markers (here shown separately in the input) indicate possession/definiteness tied to the previous noun phrase ('my ...'); the list is the object of the preceding command.
segments.s662-g739: فراوان is an adverb/adjective 'abundant(ly)'. ز (poetic variant of از) means 'of/from' or 'consisting of'. هر گونه is a bound phrase 'every kind / every sort'. افگندنی is a gerund/participle-form of افکندن 'to cast/ scatter' used here as a noun-like word meaning 'things to be scattered' or 'that which is scattered'; together the phrase says 'abundant of every kind to be scattered' or 'abundantly of every kind.'
segments.s662-g740: هم 'also/likewise' links this clause to the previous; از 'of/from' marks the materia or category; رنگ 'colour' (here meaning dyes/colours/visual ornament), و 'and', بوی 'scent/fragrance', و 'and', پراگندنی is related to پراگاندن/پراگنده — an adjectival/nominal form meaning 'scattered items' or 'dispersed adornments'. The whole segment lists additional sorts of items to be provided or scattered.
segments.s662-g741: بکافور is a prepositional/construct-like phrase 'with camphor' (ب + کافور). تن را 'the body' with the object marker را marks it as the definite object. توانگر 'wealthy/rich' here functions as predicate/adjectival goal and کنید is the polite/imparative plural 'make' (کنید = 'do/make'). The clause is an imperative: 'Make/ render the body wealthy (i.e. richly anointed) with camphor.'
segments.s662-g742: This second imperative clause uses زمشک (a fragrant plant/berry in the textual lexicon) with از بر 'from/on' (here 'from the surface of / upon') and ترگم (a small cloth/rag or shred) followed by افسر 'afsar' (a headpiece, helmet, or decorative crest) and again the imperative کنید 'make'. Literally: 'Make an afsar from zamshak on/over a rag.' In older Persian sequences, the material and the support are listed before the noun made and the verb 'make'.
segments.s662-g743: ز is the poetic 'from/of'. دیبای is brocade/silk cloth; زربفت is 'gold-woven' (a compound adjective modifying the cloth); پرمایه 'rich/substantial' further qualifies it. پنج here functions as a numeral 'five' modifying the garments/objects to be brought. The phrase therefore means 'bring five pieces of rich, gold-woven brocade (from...).'
segments.s662-g744: بیارید is an imperative 'bring' (contextually 'let/you bring'); ناکار here is an adjective that in the lexicon usually means 'ineffective/idle', but in this poetic context it likely modifies دیده (seen) to mean 'untried/unseen' or 'not used before' — the construction is elliptical/poetic. دیده is either the noun 'eye' or the past-participle 'seen'; here it functions as part of the phrase meaning 'that which the eye has not seen' (i.e. unseen wonders). ز 'from', گنج 'treasure/treasury'. The whole clause: 'bring (from the treasury) that which the eye has not seen.' Note: some individual lexeme-senses in the provided list don't exactly match this archaic/poetic usage, so some senses are marked as unavailable below.
segments.s662-g569: توانگر بود is a short protasis meaning 'if he is (or was) wealthy'—بود is past/old-fashioned copula used here in a conditional sense. چادر او بپوش is an imperative/jussive clause: چادر (tent/veil) + او (his) + بپوش (2nd-sing. jussive/imperative 'cover/put on'), literally 'cover his tent' or 'put his tent over (him)'. The verb بپوش is directed at the addressee and completes the advice begun by توانگر بود.
segments.s662-g570: چو introduces the alternative protasis 'when/if' (here conditional: 'if/when'). درویش باشد uses درویش (dervish/poor ascetic) plus باشد (subjunctive/present of 'to be') to state his condition. تو با او بکوش is an imperative addressed to 'you' (تو) with با او 'with him' and بکوش 'strive/endeavor'—i.e. 'make an effort with him' or 'befriend/help him'.
segments.s662-g571: کسی کو is an archaic relative/indefinite phrase roughly 'one who' or 'whoever' (کسی + the relative کو). فروتن‌تر و رادتر are comparative adjectives 'more humble' and 'more generous' modifying that person; the comparative -تر suffix indicates the comparison.
segments.s662-g572: دل دوستانش is a genitival phrase 'the heart(s) of his friends' (دل + دوستان + possessive suffix ش = 'his friends' hearts'). بدو is the clitic phrase 'to him/toward him' and شادتر is comparative 'happier/more joyful'; together they mean 'the hearts of his friends are the more joyful toward him.'
segments.s662-g573: دگر آنک پرسد is an introductory phrase: دگر 'another' + آنک (archaic relative/quotative 'who/that') + پرسد 'asks' (past/archaic form used narratively). که introduces the embedded question/relative clause. دشمن کراست is the question/identification 'who is the enemy' with کراست an archaic/copular/identifying verbal form (here marking 'is'), so the whole segment means 'another asks who the enemy is.'
segments.s662-g574: کزو is the relative adverb 'from whom' or 'from which' introducing the defining clause about that enemy. دل همیشه بدرد uses دل (heart) + همیشه (always) + بدرد (literally 'causes ache' / 'is in pain') meaning 'from whom the heart is always pained.' و بلاست adds that the same person is 'a calamity' or 'brings affliction' (بلاست = 'is disaster/affliction'), completing the description of the enemy.
segments.s662-g575: چوگستاخ is a fused adverbial 'when/if (he is) insolent' (چو = when/if, گستاخ = insolent). باشد is the subjunctive/present 'is,' and زبانش (زبان + ش) is 'his tongue/speech.' ببد is an archaic verbal form 'becomes/turns' (past/present-of-become), so the clause says 'when his tongue is insolent, his speech becomes bad.'
segments.s662-g576: ز (poetic 'from' or 'because of') + گفتار او ('his words/speech') introduces the cause. دشمن آید is a simple clause 'an enemy comes/arises' (آید = comes/happens). سزد is a judgmental predicate 'it is fitting/merited,' so the whole segment means 'from his words an enemy arises — which is fitting (i.e. he deserves it).'
segments.s662-g641: A vocative phrase addressing someone: توای is the poetic/vocative form ('تو ای' = 'O you'), followed by three adjectives پیر فرتوت بی‌توبه modifying مرد. Word order is noun (مرد) after its modifiers, a common poetic arrangement; overall: 'O you, old, decrepit, unrepentant man.'
segments.s662-g642: An imperative clause: خرد گیر is the verb phrase with خرد (object 'wisdom') placed before the imperative گیر ('take' / 'seize'). وز here introduces what to abandon (poetic variant of 'از'/'from' or a prepositional sense) followed by بزم و شادی ('revelry and joy'); بگرد is an imperative of گشتن meaning 'turn (away)'. The sequence is 'Take wisdom and turn away from feasting and joy.'
segments.s662-g643: A temporal clause: جهان تازه شد ('the world became new / was renewed') is the main clause; چون introduces the temporal clause 'when/whenas' (poetic usage) and is followed by قدح یافتی ('when you obtained/found the goblet'), where یافتی is 2nd-person past ('you found/obtained').
segments.s662-g644: A causal/result clause: روان را ('the soul', marked with the object clitic -را) + ز توبه تو ('because of your repentance') gives the circumstance, and برتافتی is a poetic past form meaning roughly 'you shone forth / brightened / made appear' — here used transitively/metaphorically of making the soul shine. Word order places the object (روان را) before the reason phrase and the verb.
segments.s662-g645: An interrogative clause with inverted poetic word order: چه گفت ('what said' = 'what did ... say') followed by the subject آن سراینده سالخورد ('that aged poet'). In classical/poetic Persian the verb can precede its subject as here.
segments.s662-g646: A temporal subordinate: چو ('when') introduces the clause اندرز نوشین روان یاد کرد. یاد کرد is the compound verb 'یاد کردن' (to remember) in past tense ('he remembered'), with اندرز نوشین ('sweet/delightful counsel') and روان ('the soul') arranged so that 'he remembered the soul's sweet counsel.'
segments.s662-g647: A temporal/relative clause: سخنهای هرمزد ('the words/sayings of Ahura Mazda') followed by چون شد ('when/when it came to be') and ببن (poetic form of 'ببین' = 'see/behold'). The clause means roughly 'when the words of Ahura Mazda were thus seen/beheld.'
segments.s662-g648: Main clause with a new actor: یکی نو ('a certain new one') + پی افگند ('then cast/uttered' — پی 'after/then' + افگند 'threw/cast') and موبد سخن ('the mobed's speech' or 'the priest a speech'). The verb is transitive/metaphorical: 'a certain new mobed then put forth a speech.' Word order places the subject phrase before the verb and its object follows.
segments.s662-g281: This is an inverted, poetic noun phrase giving the amount and source: ز (archaic/poetic از) = 'from', followed by دینار 'dinar(s)'. با هرکسی literally 'with/for each person' functions here to indicate 'from each person' (classical usage where با marks the party involved). The numeral سی هزار follows in fronted position for emphasis (= 'thirty thousand').
segments.s662-g282: نثار آوریده is a past participial construction meaning 'has/had brought (as an) offering'; آوریده is the past participle of آوردن with implicit subject. بر شهریار is the goal 'to/upon the king' (بر = to/toward here). Together the clause means 'brought as an offering to the king.'
segments.s662-g283: چو here is a temporal conjunction 'when'; دیدند is past tense 3rd-person plural 'they saw'. رنگ رخ is a compound 'color/complexion of the face' (رنگ = complexion/appearance; رخ = face) and شهریار is the possessor following the noun, so 'the king's countenance.'
segments.s662-g284: برفتند is past 3rd-plural 'they departed/they went away.' The following adjectives act adverbially to describe their manner: لرزان 'trembling', و 'and', پیچان 'winding/twisting', and چومار is a simile 'like a snake' (چو+مار). The sequence portrays them leaving in a trembling, sinuous fashion.
segments.s662-g285: شهنشاه is the subject 'the shah/emperor.' چون دید is a temporal/as concessive 'when/as he saw', and بنواختشان is a past-form verb with the 3rd-plural object suffix -شان: literally 'he played/struck for them' but in this context idiomatically means 'he entertained/received them' (navaxtan used figuratively).
segments.s662-g286: به آیین = 'according to ceremony/custom' (به + آیین). یکی جایگه is a nominal phrase 'one place/rank' (یکی = one; جایگه = place/rank). ساختشان is past 'he made/established them' with the object suffix -شان, so the clause means 'he made them of one rank' in the prescribed ceremony.
segments.s662-g287: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so.' گفت here functions as the verb 'said' (classical inversion places the verb before the agent), followed by the agent گوینده پیشرو 'the speaker who goes before / the leading speaker.' The order mirrors epic style: 'Thus spoke the leading speaker.'
segments.s662-g288: که introduces reported speech and is followed by the vocative ای شاه 'O king' (که‌ای here = که + ای). قیصر is the appositive 'Caesar/emperor.' جوانست is the fused form of جوان است 'is young' (present/predicate), و 'and', نو 'new/fresh' completes the predicate. The clause is direct reported speech: 'O king, Caesar is young and new.'
segments.s662-g201: Resume: 'بدو' is the prepositional phrase 'to him/unto him' that precedes the rest of the clause; 'کفشگر' is the nominative noun 'the shoemaker' (subject/agent); 'گفت' is the past-tense verb 'said' (here functioning as the main reporting verb). After the verb comes the vocative 'کای خوب‌چهر' = 'O good‑faced one' addressing the addressee.
segments.s662-g202: 'به رنجی' is an adverbial phrase 'with hardship/at difficulty' (به + رنجِ with ezāfe implied) modifying the verb of speech; 'بگویی' is the second‑person subjunctive/jussive form 'you should tell / go and tell' (a hortative/request sense); the second 'به' is the preposition 'to' introducing the addressee 'بوزرجمهر' (Bozorgmehr, a name).
segments.s662-g203: 'که' introduces an explanatory clause ('for/that'); 'اندر زمانه' = 'in this age/in these times' (prepositional phrase); 'مرا کودکیست' is a classical Persian construction meaning 'there is childishness in me' → 'I am a child/young', where 'مرا' (to me) precedes the predicate nominal 'کودکیست' (کودکی + -ست = 'is childish/is a child').
segments.s662-g204: Continuing the explanation: 'که' again introduces the clause; 'بازار او' = 'his market/trade/business' (بازار = market; او = his); 'بر دلم' literally 'upon my heart' = 'in my heart / to my mind'; 'خوار نیست' is a negated predicate 'is not despised/cheap' (خوار = despised/worthless, نیست = is not). The whole clause says that he does not regard that market with contempt.
segments.s662-g205: 'بگویی' is the hortative/subjunctive 'you should tell' or 'tell him'; 'مگر' here introduces a possibility (roughly 'perhaps' / 'maybe'); 'شهریار جهان' is a title phrase 'the king of the world' (شهریار = king, جهان = of the world) and functions as the potential agent of the following action.
segments.s662-g206: 'مرا' (me / to me) is the object; 'شاد گرداند' is a causative phrasing where گرداند (here causative/optative) means 'cause to be / make' and شاد is the adjective 'happy/glad' — together 'may make me glad'; 'اندر نهان' is an adverbial 'in secret / secretly'. The clause expresses a hope/possibility that the king will secretly make the speaker happy.
segments.s662-g207: 'که' begins a subordinate clause ('that'); 'او را' is the direct object 'him'; 'سپارد' (سپاردن) is the present/subjunctive/optative 'may entrust / should entrust' (to leave/entrust); 'بفرهنگیان' is the dative/benefactive phrase 'to the cultured / to the men of learning' (بـ + فرهنگیان). The clause says he should entrust that person to learned men.
segments.s662-g208: Explanatory reason: 'که' introduces the reason; 'دارد' is the present tense 'has/possesses'; 'سرمایه' means 'capital/resources'; 'و' = 'and'; 'هنگ آن' refers to its 'retinue/force/contingent' (هنگ here = accompanying force/array) and 'آن' = 'of it/that'. The clause gives the reason why entrustment is appropriate: he possesses wealth and a following.
segments.s662-g745: بپوشید is an imperative (or hortative) form of ساختن/پوشیدن meaning “make/put on” addressed to craftsmen/attendants. برما is the prepositional phrase ‘on us’ (بر + ما) marking the object being clothed. به رسم کیان is a prepositional phrase ‘in the manner/custom of the Kians’ where به indicates manner (‘in/by’) and رسم = custom; کیان names the royal house.
segments.s662-g746: بر آیین نیکان ما در میان is a continuing specification of manner/place: بر + آیین = ‘according to the practice/ritual’; نیکان is a plural substantive ‘the good/righteous ones’; ما is the possessor/pronoun ‘our’ (here ‘our righteous (ancestors/men)’); در میان is a locution ‘in the midst/among’ (در = in, میان = middle/among). Grammatically this whole phrase modifies how they should be clothed (i.e. according to that custom among our good people).
segments.s662-g747: بسازید هم is an imperative ‘make/build as well/also’ (بسازید = make; هم = also). زین نشان is a noun phrase: زین (here a qualifier — classically can be ‘saddle’ or used demonstratively) + نشان ‘mark/token/emblem’; تخت عاج is a compound ‘ivory throne’ (تخت = throne; عاج = ivory). The phrase functions as the direct object: ‘also make the (saddle-)emblem into/for the ivory throne.’
segments.s662-g748: بر آویخته = ‘hung on/upon’ (بر = on/upon; آویخته = hung/attached) and forms a participial phrase describing disposition/placement. ازبر here is an archaic/poetic sequence attached to عاج تاج and indicates relation to the ivory part of the crown; the final words عاج تاج = ‘ivory crown’ (عاج = ivory; تاج = crown). The whole segment says the object (the emblem/throne) is hung on the ivory crown.
segments.s662-g749: همان هرچه زرین به پیش اندرست is a nominal/clausal phrase introduced by همان (‘the same’/‘likewise’ used adverbially for emphasis) + هرچه (‘whatever’) + زرین (‘golden’) describing the items. به پیش = ‘before/in front (of you)’ (به = prepositional marker; پیش = before) and اندرست is an archaic present copula/verb meaning ‘is/are within/there’ (third person). Together: ‘Likewise, whatever golden things are there before you.’
segments.s662-g750: This line uses repeated اگر … اگر … with nominal predicates to mean ‘whether … or …’. طاس و جامست parses as ‘(whether) a bowl (طاس) or a cup (جام is here in predicate form جام‑ست = ‘is a cup’)’ — note the older enclitic copula ‑ست ‘is’. The second if‑clause اگر گوهرست = ‘or whether (it) is a gem’. The repeated اگر serves the function ‘whether … or …’ rather than a conditional.
segments.s662-g751: This is an enumerative clause giving counts: گلاب و می‌و زعفران جام بیست lists items with their container/count. گلاب = rosewater; و = and; می‌و here refers to a liquid (classical spelling/lexical variant) — the line enumerates liquids/contents — زعفران = saffron; جام = cup; بیست = twenty. The verb ‘is/are’ is omitted (elliptical), a common poetic compression: ‘(there are) twenty cups of …’.
segments.s662-g752: ز مشک و ز کافور و عنبر دویست continues the enumeration: ز (poetic preposition ‘of/from’) + مشک (musk) + و + ز + کافور (camphor) + و + عنبر (ambergris/amber) with دویست ‘two hundred’ as the stated quantity. Again the copula is omitted; the whole phrase means ‘two hundred (of) musk, camphor and ambergris.’
segments.s662-g609: بگاهی functions adverbially here—'at times/sometimes'. که introduces a subordinate temporal clause ('when/that') modifying بگاهی. تنها بود is a simple past copular clause ('was alone'); در نهفت is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in concealment' or 'kept hidden' (نهفت here describes the state of being hidden). Word order is relatively free and poetic: adverbial time → relative particle → predicate clause → locative phrase.
segments.s662-g610: پشیمان شود is a verbal phrase: پشیمان is an adjective 'regretful' and شود (شد in literary present/subjunctive/third-person) yields 'becomes/regrets'—a habitual or potential action. زان is a prepositional/formal genitive 'from those' and introduces the cause ('from those words'). سخنها is the plural noun 'words/speeches' and که گفت is a relative clause ('that he said') modifying سخنها; که + past verb گفـت makes a defining relative ('which he said').
segments.s662-g611: هم here is an additive/introductory particle 'also/even'. اندر زمان is a temporal prepositional phrase 'in/at the time'. چون is used in a temporal sense ('when/once') and introduces the subordinate clause. گشاید is the literary present (subjunctive/indicative) of 'open/begin' applied to سخن ('to open/utter speech'); note the poetic word order places the verb before its object: 'چون گشاید سخن' = 'when he opens (begins) speech.'
segments.s662-g612: به پیش آرد is a transitive verbal phrase: به پیش = 'forward/before (the assembly)' and آرد (آورد) = 'he brings/produces' (literary present). آن لافهای کهن is the object: آن 'those/the', لافهای plural 'boasts' and کهن postmodifies as 'ancient/old'. The whole phrase means he brings forward those old boasts; the verb precedes its object in classic poetic order.
segments.s662-g613: خردمند is a nominative descriptor 'the wise (man)'. و‌گر is a classical conjunctive 'and if / or if' linking an alternative condition; مردم بی‌هنر is a coordinated noun phrase 'the people unskilled' (بی‌هنر = lacking skill). The clause sets up contrasting possibilities ('whether X or Y') without an explicit copula, typical of literary Persian.
segments.s662-g614: کس is the indefinite subject here used in a negative context meaning 'no one/anyone' (classical Persian frequently uses کس with a negated verb to mean 'no one'). از آفرنیش is a prepositional phrase 'from his creation' (آفرنیش = what one has made/creation). نیابد is the negative present/subjunctive of یافتن ('does not/cannot find' – literary present form), and گذر is a noun 'passage/escape'. Together: 'no one finds (is afforded) passage/escape from his creation.'
segments.s662-g615: چنین بود is a simple past copular statement 'it was thus/so it was.' تا here introduces the duration clause 'while/until' (i.e., 'as long as'). بود دوران دهر repeats the copula and noun for poetic emphasis—'the era/turn of time existed'—so the line means 'so it was as long as the age lasted.' The doubled بود is rhetorical parallelism common in epic verse.
segments.s662-g616: یکی ... یکی is a correlative pair meaning 'one ... another' (distributive). یابد is the present literary form 'finds/receives' (habitual or general). زهر is 'poison' (literal or figurative 'harm'), and پای زهر literally 'the foot of poison'—a poetic/metaphorical object of harm. The construction states contrasting fates: 'one gets poison, another gets the foot/step of poison.'
segments.s662-g337: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (here rendered as 'for/of'). دینار is a direct object 'dinar(s)'. گفتند is a past, 3rd-person plural reporting verb 'they said' (used to introduce what they asked for). وز is a fused poetic sequence (و + ز) meaning 'and from'. گاو پوست is a noun phrase 'cow-hide' (noun + noun; Persian allows the modifier to follow).
segments.s662-g338: ز again marks purpose/reason 'for' or 'of'. کاری here means 'a (necessary) thing / the matter / what is needed'. که introduces a relative/nominal clause 'which / by which'. آرام is an adjective 'calm/secure' describing the state of the journey; روم is the place-name 'Rome/Byzantium'. اندروست is an archaic/poetic form — it can be read as a proper name or as a contracted poetic phrase (compare 'اندر اوست' = 'is in/within it'); here it functions to complete the relative clause and give a sense like 'by which it is safe / one is safe to go to Rome'.
segments.s662-g339: چنین is a discourse adverb 'thus/so'. گفت here is the past form 'said' introducing reported speech (the list gives noun senses for گفت, but here it is the verb 'he said'). موبد is the speaker 'the mobed / Zoroastrian priest'. اگر begins a conditional 'if'. زر is the object 'gold'. دهید is the 2nd-person plural/polite present-subjunctive/imperfect form 'you give' (used here to mean 'if you give').
segments.s662-g340: ز is the same preposition 'of/from'. دیبا is 'dība' (brocade / fine silk cloth). چه مایه is an idiom 'what reason / what cause / why' (مایه here = 'cause, reason'). بران is a poetic locution meaning 'upon that / on them' (بران = بر + آن). سرنهید is a verbal phrase: classical Persian can use سر + نهادن/نهاده forms; here the intent is 'place on the head / put upon them' (the printed senses emphasize humility, but in context it functions as an instruction to set/place the robe), i.e. 'why not put (the brocade) on them?'.
segments.s662-g341: بهنگام برگشتن is a temporal phrase 'at the time of returning' (بهنگام = 'at the time of', برگشتن = infinitive/masdar 'returning'). شهریار is the noun 'the king' and functions as the subject/time-reference of the clause.
segments.s662-g342: ز marks the relevant material 'of/from'. دیبای is the genitive/constructed form of دیبا 'dība (brocade)'. زربفت is a compound adjective/noun 'gold-woven / gold-brocade'. باید is an impersonal/modal verb 'it is necessary / it was required' (here aligning with 'a thousand' that follows in the verse). The numeral 'thousand' is implied in the verse line break (ز ... باید هزار = 'a thousand of gold-brocade are required').
segments.s662-g343: که introduces an explanatory clause 'for / since / which'. خلعت is 'robe of honour / cloak' (the gifted robe). بود is the past copula 'was'. شاه را uses the object/topic marker را after 'king' to indicate possession/benefit 'for the king'. هر زمان is 'at every time / always' (هر = every, زمان = time).
segments.s662-g344: چه ... و چه ... is a correlative pair meaning 'whether ... or ...'. با means 'with'. کهتران here denotes the lesser/low-ranking people (an antonymic pair with مهتران); the precise lexeme is archaic/collective. و is the coordinating 'and'. مهتران are the greater/high-ranking people; both nouns indicate that the robe belonged to the king regardless of company (with the humble or the exalted).
segments.s662-g761: This is a short nominal phrase introduced by the preposition ز (from/on account of). فرزند here means 'son/descendant' and ارجمند modifies the preceding noun phrase as an adjective 'esteemed, honored'. The sequence وز دوده is opaque/poetic here and appears as an internal name or compound modifier; it hangs with ارجمند to mean 'the venerable X' and links to the next clause by the notion of persons who might be affected.
segments.s662-g762: This is a verbal clause stating the desired result: کسی (anyone) as subject and آید گزند (literally 'harm comes') as the predicate. ز مرگ من is a genitive/causal phrase 'because of/from my death' placed before the verb; کش is an archaic/poetic element attached to کسی and functions as part of the idiomatic expression here. The verb آید is simple present/subjunctive sense 'may/shall come' and گزند is the noun 'harm'.
segments.s662-g763: بیاساید is an intransitive verbal form meaning roughly 'be at rest/cease' or 'refrain'; it governs the following oblique phrase از بزم و شادی 'from feasting and joy'. دو ماه is a time phrase 'for two months' placed after the object phrase as a duration. Word order is Verb — prepositional phrase — duration, typical of older Persian poetic usage.
segments.s662-g764: This is a subordinating clause introduced by که 'that/because', giving the reason or explanatory remark: این باشد آیین 'this shall be the custom' where باشد is the subjunctival/present-form of 'to be' used to state a rule, آیین 'custom/ritual' is the predicate noun, and پس از مرگ شاه 'after the king's death' is a temporal phrase (پس از = after + مرگ شاه = the king's death).
segments.s662-g765: سزد‌گر is a deverbal/nominal expression meaning 'one for whom it is fitting' or 'it is fitting that'; هرآنکو is a relative pronoun 'whoever' (literally 'every one who'), and بود is the copula 'is/was' linking to پارسا 'pious' — here used in a classical sense to produce a conditional/relative: 'whoever is pious'. The sequence functions as the subject of the following clause.
segments.s662-g766: بگرید is an imperative or hortative verb form 'let him/her weep' (from گریستن). برIN (بر + ین) means 'for this' or 'over this' with ین here an orthographic/poetic form of این; نامور پادشا is the object phrase 'the renowned king'. The verb commands the pious person named in the previous segment to lament this king.
segments.s662-g767: ز فرمان هرمزد is an oblique/genitive phrase 'from the command of Hormozd' (i.e., by the decree of Ahura Mazda). برمگذرید is an imperative-negative idiom here taken as 'do not pass over / do not disregard' (بر + مگذرید), so the line issues a prohibition against ignoring divine command.
segments.s662-g768: دم خویش is an idiomatic phrase 'one's very breath/action' (خویش + ش = 'his/her/one's own'); بی رای او is the prepositional phrase 'without his counsel/opinion' (بی = without, رای او = his counsel), and مشمرید is an archaic hortative/negative-form verb here meaning roughly 'do not (even) move/act/breathe' — together: 'do not so much as breathe without his advice.' The parts are therefore an emphatic prohibition tied to the divine command in the previous segment.
segments.s662-g305: بخندید is a past (3rd‑person) form of خندیدن; here it functions as 'laughed' and takes زان سخن as the thing laughed at (i.e., 'laughed at that speech'). Note the verb comes before the subject (نوشین روان) in this poetic word order. نوشین modifies روان ('sweet/pleasant‑souled') and زان here means 'at/from that' introducing the speech (سخن) which is the object of the laugh.
segments.s662-g306: که introduces a relative clause ('which/that') modifying 'سخن'. مرد فرستاده is a noun plus a participial adjective: مرد (man) with فرستاده ('sent') describing him. افگند is the past of افکندن ('threw/cast/overturned'), and بن ('foundation/root') is its object; the object marker را is omitted in poetic diction.
segments.s662-g307: بدو is the prepositional/in dative 'to him' (indirect object). گفت is the verb 'said' (past); اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). نامور is an adjective 'famed/renowned' and کودکست is an archaic fused copula form meaning 'is a child' (کودک + ـست). The clause is therefore 'He said to him: if the famed one is a child...'.
segments.s662-g308: خرد ('wisdom') is the subject/topic; با سخن ('with/by speech') is an instrumental phrase meaning 'by words'. نزد او means 'in his presence' / 'before him'. اندکست is the poetic contraction of اندک است ('is little/insufficient') — the copula is fused to the adjective in older/poetic Persian. The whole segment states that wisdom by words is of little effect in his presence.
segments.s662-g309: The paired چه ... چه ... is a correlative construction meaning 'whether ... or ...' (listing two alternatives). قیصر is 'Caesar' and آن بی‌خرد رهنمون is 'that foolish guide' (آن = that; بی‌خرد = foolish; رهنمون = guide). Word order places the demonstrative and adjective before the noun in the second member.
segments.s662-g310: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/by' (here marking source or cause). دانش ('knowledge') is the agent/source; روان را uses را to mark روان ('the soul') as the direct object of گرفته ('has taken/seized'). گرفته is perfective/past participial in sense 'has seized' and زبون is a predicative adjective 'abject/humbled' describing the state into which the soul has been brought — Persian often places such complements after the verb without a copula.
segments.s662-g311: همه is a universal quantifier 'all'. هوشمندان is the plural noun 'the wise/clever ones'. اسکندری is an attributive/genitival form 'of Alexander' (i.e., 'Alexander's'), placed after the noun to mark association — a normal poetic/genitive construction.
segments.s662-g312: گرفتند is the past (3rd‑person plural) of گرفتن 'they took/seized' with the plural subject 'all Alexander's wise men'. پیروزی و برتری are two direct objects joined by و ('and') — 'victory and superiority' — and را is omitted as common in poetic diction.
segments.s662-g753: نهاده is a past/participial form meaning 'placed' (classical style) and comes first by poetic inversion; ز + دِستِ چپ و دِستِ راست literally 'from/at the left hand and the right hand' functions as the locative phrase marking where something was placed. The coordination with و simply links the two parallel nouns دستِ چپ and دستِ راست.
segments.s662-g754: ز فرمان introduces a source or authority ('by/from the order'); نباید is the impersonal modal 'must/should not' and governs the clause that follows. نه here = 'nor' (a coordinating negative) precedes کاست (root کاستن used here as the verb 'to diminish'/'to make less'); the terse classical order omits auxiliary words, yielding 'by the command there should be neither increase (فزونی) nor decrease (کاست)'.
segments.s662-g755: ز خون 'because of/from the blood' gives the reason. The verbal elements are split by poetic word order: باید 'must/ought' combines with کرد (the verb 'to do/make') though the usual prose order would be 'باید کرد' — here the verb appears before باید for metrical reasons; تهیگاه خشک = 'the empty/dry chamber' (تهیگاه 'an empty place, chamber', خشک 'dry').
segments.s662-g756: بدو = 'to it' (dative clitic) and اندر = 'in/into'; together (بدو اندر) mark the target place 'into it'. افگنده is a perfect/past-participial verbal form meaning 'cast/placed' (literary), and follows the prepositional phrase for metrical order. کافور و مشک are the things thrown in; و simply coordinates them.
segments.s662-g757: ازان پس = 'after that/thereupon' (temporal connector). برآرید is an imperative/subjunctive form meaning 'raise/lift up' (addressing attendants), and درگاه را is the definite object marked by را ('the court/entrance'), so the clause commands: 'After that, lift the court's (entrance)'.
segments.s662-g758: نباید introduces a prohibition ('must not/should not'); که introduces the subordinate clause. Word order is inverted for emphasis: بیند (subjunctive/present 'see') is followed by کسی (an indefinite pronoun) and then شاه را (the definite object 'the king' with را). The whole means 'it must not be that anyone sees the king' (i.e. 'no one should see the king').
segments.s662-g759: چو = 'when/if/as'; زین گونه = 'in this manner' (poetic order: زین + گونه = 'this kind/way'); بد کار is a compound meaning 'ill-acted' or 'badly run' (بد modifies کار). آن بارگاه = 'that palace/court' (آن definite demonstrative + بارگاه 'palace, court'). Together: 'If/when that court is thus badly managed…'.
segments.s662-g760: نیابد is the (classical) present/subjunctive/negative form of 'یافتن' used impersonally here: 'will not find' or 'may not find'. بر ما = 'to/toward us' (بر = toward, ما = us). کسی نیز راه = 'anyone/anyone else (کسی نیز) a way/path (راه)'; because of the negative verb, the sense is 'no one will find a way to us either.' Word order places the verb before the prepositional phrase for poetic rhythm.
segments.s662-g217: 'که' introduces this relative/subordinate clause ('that' / 'for'); the prepositional phrase 'در پادشاهی' (= 'in the kingdom') comes first, followed by the indefinite 'یکی' used as 'a certain' and the compound noun 'موزه دوز' (literally 'slipper‑sewer' = 'cobbler'). Word order is typical for older Persian: locative → indefinite NP.
segments.s662-g218: 'برین گونه' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in this manner/so' (بر + این/ین + گونه); 'شادست' is the contracted poetic form of 'شاد است' (present copula: 'is happy/joyful'); the conjunction 'و' links the following epithet 'گیتی فروز' which describes him as 'world‑illuminating' or famed. The phrase modifies the cobbler introduced in the previous segment.
segments.s662-g219: Again 'که' introduces the clause; 'چندین درم' = 'several dirhams' (object/amount); 'ساخته' is a past participle meaning 'made/earned' and 'باشدش' is a periphrastic jussive/subjunctive form ('may it be to him' / 'may he have'), so together they convey 'that he may have earned several dirhams' or 'even though he has earned several dirhams.'
segments.s662-g220: 'مبادا' is an optative/negative-prohibitive meaning 'may it not be; lest'; 'که' introduces the subordinate clause; 'بیداد' is the agent/noun 'injustice/oppression'; 'بخراشدش' is a present/subjunctive form with prefix 'بـ' meaning 'may (it) scratch/maim him' (the object clitic '-ش' = 'him'). The whole is a negative wish: 'Lest injustice harm him.'
segments.s662-g221: 'نگر' is an imperative 'look!' or 'see!'; 'تا' here introduces purpose/result ('so that' / 'in order to') and together with the following words forms the phrase 'نگر تا چه دارد کنون آرزوی' = 'look to see what wish he has now'. 'چه' is the interrogative 'what', 'دارد' is present 'has', and 'کنون' is 'now'; 'آرزوی' is the noun 'wish/desire' (object).
segments.s662-g222: 'بماناد' is the jussive/subjunctive 'may it remain'; the locative 'بر ما' = 'upon/with us' indicates to whom it should remain; 'همین راه و خوی' = 'this same way and disposition' (راه = way/practice, خوی = temperament/habit). The clause expresses a wish: 'May this same conduct and character remain with us.'
segments.s662-g223: 'چو' here functions as a temporal/conditional 'when' or 'if'; 'فامش' = 'his fame/appearance' (فام with the clitic -ش 'his'); 'بتوزی' is an archaic/poetic verb form (contextually 'is/will be spread or becomes known'); 'درم صدهزار' is the quantified noun phrase 'a hundred thousand dirhams' (noun followed by numeral). The clause sets the condition under which the payment is to be made.
segments.s662-g224: 'بده' is the imperative 'give'; 'تا' introduces purpose/result meaning 'so that'; 'بماند' is jussive/subjunctive 'may remain/let remain'; 'ز ما یادگار' = 'a memento from us' (ز = 'from/of' in poetic register, ما = 'us', یادگار = 'keepsake/memento'). The whole is a purpose clause: 'Give (him) so that a keepsake from us remains.'
segments.s662-g313: This is a relative clause: کسی کو = 'someone who / whoever'. کو is an archaic/poetic relative particle here (equivalent to modern که). بگردد is a prefixed verb (ب‑ + گردَدن) meaning 'turn away' or 'revert' in a present/subjunctive/habitual sense; ز پیمان ما = 'from our covenant' (ز = 'from' + پیمان 'covenant' + ما 'our').
segments.s662-g314: بپیچید is another prefixed verb (ب‑ + پیچیدن) meaning 'turn aside'/'be turned'; its subject is دل 'the heart' which immediately follows (typical Persian VSO/OV order is flexible in poetry). از رای و فرمان ما = 'from our counsel and command' (از = 'from', رای = 'counsel/plan', فرمان = 'command', ما = 'our').
segments.s662-g315: از آباد بومش بر آریم خاک is a poetic verbal phrase: از آباد بومش = 'from his prosperous homeland' (آباد = prosperous/inhabited, بومش = his land). بر آریم is a compound where بر is a verbal prefix and آریم is a first‑person‑plural form (optative/subjunctive/poetic) meaning 'we raise/bring (up)'; خاک is the object 'soil/earth'—together 'we will raise the soil (i.e. reduce it to dust) from his prosperous land.'
segments.s662-g316: زگنج و ز لشکر نداریم باک is a coordinated clause: زگنج = 'from the treasure' (i.e. as to riches) و ز لشکر = 'and from the army' (i.e. as to military force). نداریم is present indicative (we do not have / we feel no) and باک = 'fear/concern', so 'we have no fear of treasure or of an army.'
segments.s662-g317: فرستادگان خاک دادند بوس is literal word order 'envoys (subject) earth (object) gave (دادند) kiss(es)'. دادند is simple past 'they gave' and together with بوس (object 'a kiss' or 'kisses') means 'they kissed the earth' (a formula of obeisance).
segments.s662-g318: چنانچون بود مردم چابلوس = 'just as / for so were the people sycophantic'. چنانچون = 'just as / thus', بود = past 'were', مردم = 'the people', چابلوس = 'sycophant' (descriptive predicate).
segments.s662-g319: که‌ای شاه پیروز برترمنش is a vocative/exclamatory phrase addressing the king: که‌ای (classical/poetic interjection equivalent to 'که ای' / 'O'), شاه = 'king', پیروز = 'victorious', برترمنش = 'haughty / of lofty manners'. The adjectives follow the noun (common in Persian) and describe the addressee.
segments.s662-g320: ز کار گذشته مکن سرزنش is an admonition: ز کار گذشته = 'for/from deeds already past' (ز = 'about/from', کار = 'deed', گذشته = 'past'), مکن = negative imperative 'do not', and سرزنش = 'reproach' (object). Word order is typical: temporal/causal phrase before the prohibitive verb.
segments.s662-g721: که‌گر is the conditional particle 'if' (archaic spelling of که اگر). پند ما را is the direct object: پند 'advice' with possessive ما 'our' and را marking it as definite/object. شوی is 2nd-person present/subjunctive ('you become' / 'you make yourself'), and کاربند is a predicate complement meaning 'bound/attached' (literally 'fettered' or 'making something a rule'). Together: 'if you become bound to our advice'.
segments.s662-g722: همیشه 'always' is an adverbial; بماند is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive/optative ('may it remain' / 'may it last'). کلاهت is the possessed noun 'your hat' (کلاه + enclitic -ت), and بلند is the predicative adjective 'high/lofty'. Poetic word order places the verb before its subject: literally 'always may your hat remain high.'
segments.s662-g723: که here introduces the optative blessing clause. نیکی دهش is a noun phrase where دهش ('grant/gift') relates to نیکی ('goodness') — effectively 'may goodness grant' or 'may goodness be the giver of...'. نیک‌خواه تو 'your well-wisher' (نیک‌خواه modified by the possessor تو). باد is the optative particle 'may it be'. The clause expresses a wish: 'may goodness give you a well-wisher.'
segments.s662-g724: همه نیکی is the subject phrase 'all goodness/every good thing'. اندر پناه تو is a prepositional phrase 'in/under your protection' (اندر = در, archaic/poetic 'in'). باد again is the optative 'may it be'. The whole means 'may all goodness be in your refuge/protection.'
segments.s662-g725: مبادت is the negative optative (مباد + enclitic ت) meaning 'may it not be' or 'may it never happen'. فراموش is the verbal adjective/participle 'forgotten' or the verbal noun 'forgetting'. گفتار من 'my speech/words' is the patient of the wish. Together: 'may my words not be forgotten.'
segments.s662-g726: اگر is the conditional 'if'. دور is an adjective 'far, distant'. مانی here is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive form 'you remain/you are' (classical/poetic form of ماندن), so the clause reads 'if you remain far'. ز is the preposition 'from', دیدار 'meeting/seeing/sight', and من 'my'. Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect: 'if you remain far from my sight.'
segments.s662-g727: سرت 'your head' (سر + enclitic -ت) is the subject of the optative phrase. سبز 'green' is the predicative adjective; باد (here omitted in the second clause) is the optative 'may' applied to these wishes. و links the coordinate clause; دلت is دل + -ت 'your heart' and شادمان is the predicate 'joyful'. The copula/optative is understood across both coordinated parts.
segments.s662-g728: تنت 'your body' (تن + -ت) is the subject; پاک 'pure' is its predicate. و connects to a prepositional phrase دور از بد بدگمان meaning 'far from bad/evil suspicion' — دور از 'far from', بد modifies بدگمان ('ill-suspicion' = 'malicious suspicion' or 'bad mistrust'). The optative verb ('باد') is again understood from earlier clauses.
segments.s662-g185: پیمبر is the subject ('the messenger') and باندیشه is a fused prepositional/phrasal form (با + اندیشه) meaning 'with regard to thought' or 'of a thoughtful mind'. باریک is a predicative adjective here meaning 'subtle, delicate', and بود is the past copula 'was'. Word order is subject — (prepositional phrase) — predicate — copula, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s662-g186: بیامد is a past narrative verb 'he came'. بشهری is be + شهری ('to/toward a city/town') indicating direction; که introduces a relative clause modifying بشهری, and نزدیک بود is a copular clause 'was near'. The clause therefore means 'he came to a town which was near.'
segments.s662-g187: درم is the object 'a dirham / a small coin' placed before the verb. خواست is the past verb 'wanted/asked (for)'. فام here means a 'trace/token/mark' (i.e. a small token or sign). از پی شهریار is a prepositional phrase 'in pursuit of / following the king' that situates the request. Overall: 'He asked for a dirham (a token) while following the king.'
segments.s662-g188: برو here functions as a narrative perfective/preterital 'he went' (although identical in form to the imperative). انجمن is 'assembly/gathering'; شد is the past of 'become/happen' and here combines with the subject (understood from context) to mean 'joined/was in the assembly' or 'became part of the gathering'. بسی is an adverbial intensifier (classical usage 'much/very') and مایه‌دار is an adjective 'wealthy'. The clause reads 'he went to the assembly and became very wealthy.'
segments.s662-g189: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain / someone' introducing a person; کفشگر is 'shoemaker' and بود is the past copula 'was'. و links the two coordinated roles; موزه فروش is a compound meaning 'seller of mūzeh' (here 'slippers' in regional/older usage). The phrase says 'a certain man was a shoemaker and seller of slippers.'
segments.s662-g190: به گفتار او is a prepositional phrase 'to/at his speech' (i.e. 'at what he said' or 'to his words'). تیز is an adverb/adjective 'keenly/sharply', and بگشاد is the past form 'opened' (here used with گوش). گوش is used idiomatically as 'ear / attention' — 'بگشاد گوش' = 'opened his ear', i.e. 'listened attentively.'
segments.s662-g191: درم is 'dirham(s)'; چند 'how many'; باید is the modal 'must/ought to'; بدو is the indirect object 'to him'; گفت here functions as the infinitive/verb 'to say/give' (classical Persian places the modal/baiad + infinitive construction together). مرد at the end is either a nominative/vocative 'the man' (identifying who is meant) or the subject carried over — the line reads as a question 'How many dirhams must be given to him, the man?'
segments.s662-g192: دلاور 'the brave one' is the subject; شمار درم is a noun phrase 'the counting/number of dirhams' (object). یاد کرد is a compound verb meaning 'made mention of / noted / counted' (یاد here carries the idea of 'mention/recall' and کرد is the light verb 'did'). Together: 'The brave man noted/mentioned the number of dirhams.'
segments.s662-g265: This is a noun phrase: 'طلایه' (vanguard) modified by the adjective 'پراگنده' (scattered), followed by the locative prepositional phrase 'بر گرد دشت' meaning 'around the plain'. In poetic word order the adjective follows the noun and the two-part preposition 'بر گرد' expresses location 'around' + its complement 'دشت'.
segments.s662-g266: 'همه شب' is a temporal adverbial 'all night'; 'همی' is the archaic iterative/continuous marker meaning 'continually/kept on'; 'گرد' here is the verbal stem 'to circle/about' (used before the subject in this poetic order); 'لشکر' is the subject 'the army/host'; 'بگشت' is the past tense verb meaning 'turned/went' — together meaning 'the army kept circling all night.' Note that 'همی' gives the verb an ongoing/repeated sense.
segments.s662-g267: The clause begins with the poetic 'ز' (from/of; here marking the occasion = 'when'), followed by 'ماهی' used poetically for a moonlike/beautiful aspect, then 'چو' = 'when/as', and the verb 'بنمود' (appeared/showed). 'خورشید' is the grammatical subject that follows the verb in this poetic inversion, and 'تاج' is the direct object 'crown' — literally 'when, like a moon, the sun showed (its) crown.'
segments.s662-g268: Past verb 'برافگند' (threw/ cast) begins the clause, followed by the object 'خلعت' (robe) with the definite/object marker 'را' attached to 'زمین' (the earth) to show that the robe was cast onto the earth. The final phrase 'ز عاج' gives the material/source 'of ivory' (literally 'from ivory'). The whole means 'it cast a robe of ivory upon the earth.'
segments.s662-g269: 'طلایه چو گشت' = 'when the vanguard turned' (چو = 'when', گشت = 'turned/changed'); 'از لب' = 'from the lip/edge/forefront'; 'کنده' is a participle meaning 'torn off/detached' describing the subject; 'باز' = 'again' (here preparing the following verb). The participial 'کنده' modifies the vanguard and links to the following action.
segments.s662-g270: The past verb 'بیامد' = 'came' is followed by the prepositional phrase 'بر شاه' = 'to/ before the king' (the prefix بـ- marks direction 'to'), and 'گردن‌فراز' is a postpositive epithet 'proud' applied to the king. So: 'came before the proud king.'
segments.s662-g271: The particle 'که' introduces the subordinate/explanatory clause ('that/when'); 'پیغمبر قیصر' is the noun phrase 'the emperor's/Caesar's prophet (or envoy)'; 'آمد' is the past tense 'came'; 'بشاه' = 'to the king' (بـ- + شاه = 'to the king' or 'before the king'). Word order here places the verb before the to-phrase in typical poetic inversion.
segments.s662-g272: 'پر از درد' = 'full of pain/sorrow' (پر + از + noun); 'و پوزش کنان' = 'and making apologies' where 'پوزش' = 'apology' and 'کنان' is a present-participial form ('doing/uttering') that turns the noun into an action; 'از گناه' gives the cause/reason 'for/ because of sin.' Altogether: 'full of grief and making apologies for his sin.'
segments.s662-g233: فرستاده (the messenger) is the subject; گوید is the (poetic/historical) present 'says' introducing reported speech. که is the complementizer 'that', and این مرد گفت is a subordinate clause 'this man said' (گفت is past narrative). The sequence mixes present-reporting گوید with the reported past گفت, a common narrative device.
segments.s662-g234: که introduces the quoted content: 'that ...'. شاه جهان is a genitival phrase 'king of the world'. با خرد is a prepositional phrase 'with wisdom'. باد is the optative particle 'may' (wishing), and جفت is used predicatively as 'paired/ matched' — together forming 'may the world-king be joined with wisdom.' Word order is typical of Persian: complementizer + subject + prepositional complement + optative particle + predicate noun.
segments.s662-g235: یکی 'a/one' introduces an indefinite 'a certain' or 'one' person; پور is 'son'. دارم is the first-person present 'I have', and رسیده is a past participle 'arrived/ripe' modifying پور (= 'who has come of age'). بجای here means 'to the appropriate place/position' or 'having come to maturity' — i.e. the son has reached his place/age.
segments.s662-g236: بفرهنگ is an adjective/adverbial 'cultured' (b- here is not a verbal prefix but part of the adjective form), جوید is the present/hegemonic form 'seeks' (with the older present-stative flavor), همی is a continuous/presentive particle 'still/continually', and رهنمای is the object 'guide/tutor'. The clause says he continually seeks a cultured guide.
segments.s662-g237: اگر introduces a condition 'if'. شاه باشد is a subjunctive/optative-style clause 'if the king be/consent'. The sequence ب + دین is the orthography of بدین 'to/for this' (here 'to this [request]'), and دستگیر functions as 'receive/accept into one's patronage' or 'take under protection' (so: 'if the king consents to this, accept him into your care').
segments.s662-g238: که is the subordinate/complementizer 'so that/that'. این پاک فرزند is 'this pure/virtuous child/son' (این + پاک + فرزند). گردد is the subjunctive/optative 'may become' (from گردیدن/گشتن), and دبیر is the result noun 'scribe/secretary/scholar'. Thus the clause gives the hoped-for outcome: 'that this pure son may become a scribe.'
segments.s662-g239: ز یزدان 'from God' (z is the poetic form of 'from'). بخواهم is first-person present/subjunctive 'I ask/wish' (here 'I pray/entreat'), همی is an adverbial emphatic 'continually/always', and جان شاه is the genitive 'the king's life' (object of the prayer). The phrase thus expresses a continual prayer to God for the king's life.
segments.s662-g240: که introduces the wished-for clause: جاوید 'eternal' with the optative باد 'may' (i.e. 'may ... be eternal'), followed by این سزاوار گاه 'this deserving/fit occasion' (این = this, سزاوار = worthy/deserving, گاه = time/occasion). Word order places the optative and adjective before the noun phrase, yielding 'may this worthy occasion be everlasting.'
segments.s662-g137: بی‌اندازه is an adverb meaning 'excessively' or 'countless' modifying the verb that follows. کشتند is the past-tense 3rd-person-plural of a verb (here 'to kill'), but the lemma senses given do not include 'to kill', so the grammatical use is past action 'they killed'. زایشان = ز + ایشان means 'of/from them' (poetic 'ز' = from), linking the killed victims to 'they'. بتیر is a prepositional/compound expression meaning 'by/with arrows' (here instrumental). Word order: adverb + verb + agent/target phrase + instrument follows the standard Persian structure (verb after modifiers and objects in older style).
segments.s662-g138: به رزم اندرون functions as a circumstantial phrase 'in/within the battle' where به (here 'towards/into') + رزم ('battle') + اندرون ('inside/within') set the location. چند شد دستگیر is a passive-like comment: چند ('several/a few') + شد as an auxiliary 'became/was' marking the resulting state, and دستگیر ('seized, captured') giving the result — together 'a few were taken captive'. The verb-like شد here serves as a light auxiliary forming a passive/resultative construction.
segments.s662-g139: به پیش سپه is a locative phrase 'before/in front of the army' where به indicates direction/position ('to/for' or 'toward') and پیش here means 'in the presence of / before'. کنده‌ای is a noun-like or participial form referring to something dug (a pit/trench); its precise lexical senses given do not match this usage so it is treated as a noun/participle 'the dug (pit)'. ساختند is the past 3rd-plural 'they made/constructed', so the clause means 'they made a dug-place before the army.'
segments.s662-g140: بشبگیر is an adverbial/time phrase meaning 'at night' or 'overnight'. آب اندر انداختند is 'they threw/poured water into (it)': اندر = 'into/within' and انداختند is the past 3rd-plural of انداختن 'to throw/cast/place', here used as 'to pour/throw (water)'. Word order follows older Shahnameh style where time phrase precedes the verb phrase.
