segments.s662-g141: بکنده is a participial/adjectival form meaning 'uprooted/dug up' (here indicating the manner or means). ببستند is past 3rd-plural 'they closed/bound/blocked'. برشاه راه is a prepositional-possessive expression literally 'the road to/upon the king' (i.e., the road leading to the king). The phrase therefore describes an action (they uprooted/removed and then blocked the king's road).
segments.s662-g142: فروماند is a past/passive-like adjective/verb 'was worn out/was spent'. از جنگ indicates cause or source 'from the battle' (از = from/out of). شاه و سپاه lists the sufferers 'the king and the army' (conjoined by و). The whole clause reads as a result-state: 'the king and the army were exhausted from the fighting.'
segments.s662-g143: برآمد is a past verb 'arose/occured/there came' used to introduce a temporal interval. برین is the compound of بر + ین (here functioning as a demonstrative-like or archaic locative particle roughly 'this/that'), so برین روزگاری دراز means 'in/through that long time' or 'a long while passed'. روزگاری دراز = 'a long time/long period.'
segments.s662-g144: بسیم و زر آمد is 'enough (or sufficient) and gold came' where بسیم (given sense 'enough') qualifies the following wealth; و = 'and'; زر = 'gold'. آمد is past 'came'. سپه را نیاز places the beneficiary/affected: سپه را ('the army' marked by the object/ topical marker را) and نیاز 'need' — literally 'need came to the army', i.e. 'sufficient silver and gold came to meet the army's need.' The unusual word order is typical of epic/older Persian.
segments.s662-g225: بدان here functions as a discourse adverb meaning 'thereupon/with that' (formed in the text from the pieces ب + دان); it links this clause to what just happened. زیردستان is the plural noun 'the subordinates/vassals' (subject). دلاور شد(ند) is the past-tense/resultative verb phrase 'became brave' (شدند = they became); the verb comes after subject as normal.
segments.s662-g226: This clause continues the narration: جهانجوی is a predicate-noun 'world-seekers/ambitious for the world' describing their aim. با + تخت (با تخت) means 'with the throne' or 'taking the throne'; وافسر here reflects a fused 'و افسر' = 'and crown/helmet' (i.e. regalia). شد(ند) again is the plural past 'they became/they obtained'. Word order: nominal predicate + prepositional phrase(s) + verb.
segments.s662-g227: مبادا is an optative/negative-purpose particle 'may it not be' / 'lest'. که introduces the subordinate clause ('that' / 'who'); here it attaches the description. بیدادگر is a substantive/adjective 'tyrant, unjust one' and شهریار is the head noun 'king'. Together they form 'a tyrannical king.'
segments.s662-g228: بود is the past/perfect form of 'to be' used here in the optative sense ('might be' / 'should be'). شاد is the predicate adjective 'happy/joyful'. برتخت is a prepositional phrase (بر + تخت) 'on the throne'. و 'and' links the following phrase; به روزگار ('in the times' / 'in his days') gives the temporal/span sense. The clause expresses a wish/fear introduced by مبادا.
segments.s662-g229: بشاه is the prepositional phrase 'to the king' (به + شاه fused), and جهان modifies شاه: 'king of the world' (title). گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' governing the direct speech that follows. بوزرجمهر is the speaker's name (proper noun) placed after the verb in the narrative.
segments.s662-g230: که‌ای here is a vocative formula (که + ای) introducing direct address—'O ...'. شاه is the addressee 'king'. نیک is an epithet 'good/virtuous' and اختر is a metaphorical epithet 'star' (auspicious); خوب‌چهر is a compound adjective 'fair‑faced' describing the king. The sequence lists epithets in apposition to the vocative.
segments.s662-g231: یکی is the indefinite 'one/a certain'. آرزو is the noun 'wish/desire' and کرد is the past verb 'made/did' (آرزو کرد = 'wished'). موزه فروش is an appositive identifying the speaker as 'the slipper-seller' (موزه here = slipper; فروش = seller). In narrative Persian the identifying noun can follow the verb phrase as here.
segments.s662-g232: اگر is the conditional 'if'. شاه is the subject 'the king'. دارد is the present-tense 'has/holds'. بمن is the indirect-object phrase 'for/to me'. بنده is 'servant' (the person he asks for) and گوش here is used metaphorically 'ear' = 'attention, one who will listen'. Together they form the conditional clause 'if the king has for me a listening servant.'
segments.s662-g33: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'; آمد is 3rd-person singular past 'came'. بدرگه (poetic form of به‌درگاه/درگاه) is the goal 'to the court/portal'. گشادند is a past plural verb 'were opened' (poetic word order: the verb precedes or interrupts its subject), and راه is the subject 'paths/ways'. Together: 'When he came to the portal, the ways opened.'
segments.s662-g34: فرستاده is the subject 'the messenger'; آمد is again past 'came'. بر is the preposition 'onto/on/at' governing تخت 'throne' (تخت = throne). و گاه links with تخت: گاه here is a poetic use meaning a 'place/seat' (though the provided sense list gives 'time'); the phrase means 'came to the throne and to a (sitting) place.'
segments.s662-g35: چو 'when' introduces the clause; قیصر is the subject 'Caesar/the emperor'. نگه کرد is a compound past 'looked' (نگه + کرد = 'gave a look'). وعنوان is 'and (the) titles' (وعنوان = و + عنوان) and بدید is past 'saw'. The verbs are straightforward past tense; the objects follow in poetic order.
segments.s662-g36: ز is the preposition 'from / because of' (poetic z- form). بیشی here means 'excess / greatness' (the superior quality of Khosrow). کسری is 'Kisra / Khosrow' (proper name). دلش is 'his heart' (دل + enclitic ش = his). بردمید is a past verb meaning 'carried off/was carried away' (used idiomatically: the heart was carried away/overwhelmed).
segments.s662-g37: جوان 'the young man' is the subject; نیز 'also/likewise' modifies it. بد here is an archaic/poetic prepositional-like element meaning 'by/at/near' (contracted form of به ...; not represented in the provided sense list). مهتر is 'the chief/headman'. نونشست is a compound past indicating 'sat down to eat' (نون = bread/meal, نشست = sat). Overall: 'The young man also sat down beside the chief to eat.'
segments.s662-g38: فرستاده را is the definite object 'the messenger' marked by را. نیز 'also' reiterates the adverbial sense. نبسود is a negative past form meaning 'did not benefit/help' (نَ + بسود), and دست 'hand' is the agent/means: literally 'the hand did not help the messenger' — idiomatically 'no help came to the messenger.'
segments.s662-g39: بپرسید is past 'he asked' (third person). ناکام is an adjective 'unsuccessful/failed' modifying that act. پرسیدنی is a substantivized/participial adjective 'worth asking / the act of asking' (poetic: qualities attached to the asking). Together they describe the attempt: 'an asking that was unsuccessful.'
segments.s662-g40: نگه is 'gaze/look' used as a noun; کردنی is a participial/gerundial form 'the act of doing / doable' that turns the verb into a noun-like quality. سست 'weak' qualifies the gaze. و links to the next phrase: کژ 'crooked/distorted' modifies دیدنی (a participle/noun 'seeing' or 'sight' — here 'the sight that was to be seen / the seeing'), so the line lists qualities: 'a weak glance and a distorted sight.'
segments.s662-g689: به + noun + verb: 'به نیکی گرای' uses به (a directional preposition) + نیکی (goodness) to mark the target of the action. گرای is an imperative/archaic verbal form meaning 'turn (toward)' or 'incline' (classical/poetic), and بکوش is the second imperative 'strive'. The coordinate ו links two parallel imperatives: 'turn toward good' and 'strive in good.'
segments.s662-g690: This clause directs action about counsel: بهرنیک here names the good (object/benefit) to be taken, followed by و + بد پند 'and (not) bad advice' and then دانا نیوش, an imperative formula 'listen, O wise (one)' or 'hearken to the wise.' نیوش in this poem functions as an imperative 'hear/listen' (classical use), though that sense is not listed in the provided lexeme data.
segments.s662-g691: نباید introduces an impersonal negative obligation 'it must not be / one ought not', and که marks the following subordinate clause. گردد is a (subjunctive/optative) verbal form 'become / happen', introducing the unwanted state. The following short clause بگرد تو بد places the verb بگرد ('turn/turn about') with the subject pronoun تو and the predicate بد 'bad', yielding 'that you turn/become bad.' Word order is archaic/poetic but functions as 'it must not be that you turn to badness.'
segments.s662-g692: کزان = 'from which / from that (cause)'. تو را uses را as a topical/accusative marker on 'you'. بی‌گمان is an adverb 'undoubtedly / surely', and بد رسد is a two-word predicate meaning 'evil will befall' where رسد is used in the sense 'to happen / befall.' The clause says that from that bad thing, surely badness will reach/ happen to you.
segments.s662-g693: همه here means 'entirely / completely' or 'all (of you)' modifying the compound that follows; پاک پوش is an adjectival/noun phrase meaning 'clean-clothed / having clean garments' (پاک modifies پوش 'garment / covering'), and likewise همه پاک خور parallels it with خور as 'one who eats' or 'eating' (i.e. 'eat purely'). This is a compact, poetic pair advising pure dress and pure food.
segments.s662-g694: همه پندها is a direct object 'all the counsels/advice' (پندها plural). یادگیر here functions as an imperative 'learn!' (classical/poetic imperative of 'یاد گرفتن'), followed by از + پدر 'from your father'. In modern lexicon the given form is listed as a noun, but in context it is an imperative verb.
segments.s662-g695: ز یزدان is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from God' (ز marks source or invocation). گشای is an imperative 'open' (i.e. 'ask/seek, open yourself to God' or 'ask of God'), and the coordinated phrase به یزدان گرای repeats the orientation: به + یزدان 'toward God' + گرای 'turn/incline' (imperative/archaic). Both verbs are imperatives directed at the addressee; گرای here is an archaic verbal form not represented among the provided senses.
segments.s662-g696: چو here is conditional 'if', خواهی is the second-person present 'you wish/want', and که introduces the subordinate clause. باشد is a subjunctive/present form 'may be / be' used optatively ('that there be'), تو را is a topical/object-marked 'for you', and رهنمای is the predicate noun 'guide'. The whole clause means 'if you wish that a guide be (for you)'.
segments.s662-g481: سخن را مگردان پس و پیش هیچ is an imperative prohibition followed by a fixed phrase. سخن را (object + definite marker) = “the words” as the direct object. مگردان is a negative imperative (“do not turn/alter”); پس و پیش here form the idiom “back and forth / backwards and forwards” (literally ‘after and before’); هیچ negates the whole phrase (“at all / nothing”). Together: “Do not twist/turn the words back and forth at all.”
segments.s662-g482: This clause states a quality of a chivalrous man. جوانمردی is the abstract noun “chivalry / a man of honor”; وداد here is tokenized as one item but functions syntactically as و + داد (“and justice/giving”) — the edition’s lemma list does not match this usage, so the exact lemma sense from the list is not used; دادن is the infinitive “to give” and بسیچ is an adverbial intensifier “much/very.” The line means roughly “(One of) chivalry: giving much.”
segments.s662-g483: اگر introduces a conditional (“if”). یادگیری is the verbal noun “learning/study.” چنین is a demonstrative/adverb “thus/like this,” and بی‌گمان is an adverb “undoubtedly.” The cluster qualifies the condition: “If learning is thus, undoubtedly…”
segments.s662-g484: گشادست is a predicative past/present-state form meaning “is wide/open.” برتو = “upon you / for you.” در آسمان locates the openness “in the heavens/sky.” Put together as an independent clause with optative/affirmative force: “the heavens are wide/open for you.”
segments.s662-g485: که here begins an explanatory clause (“for / that”). چندین = “many times” or “several times.” به گفتار is an adverbial phrase “in speech / to speak” (به + گفتار), and بشتافتم is the first-person past “I hurried / I hastened.” Together: “for I hastened to speak many times.”
segments.s662-g486: ز پرسنده = “from the questioner” (poetic z = از). پاسخ = “answer.” فزون is an adjective/adverb “more / abundant,” and یافتم is the first‑person past “I found/received.” The clause means “and from the questioner I received/obtained even more answers.”
segments.s662-g487: This is an optative (wish) clause. جهاندار = “world-ruler / sovereign.” آموزگار = “teacher.” تو = “you.” باد is the optative particle “may (it be).” Word order places the wished subject before باد: “May the sovereign be your teacher.”
segments.s662-g488: Another optative clause in parallel. خرد = “wisdom.” جوشن = “armor / protective covering” used metaphorically. و = “and.” بخت = “fortune / luck.” یار تو = “your ally/companion.” باد is again the optative “may.” Together: “May wisdom be your armor and fortune your ally.”
segments.s662-g177: بدو (to him) precedes گفت (said) to mark the indirect object ('he said to him'). ز ایدر is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from/here' (ز + ایدر). سه اسبه places the numeral before the noun (three horses). برو is an imperative 'go' addressing the addressee. Word order is VerbPhrase (indirect-object + said) then prepositional/command clause.
segments.s662-g178: گزین کن is an imperative compound: گزین (choose) + کن (the auxiliary/imperative 'do') giving 'choose!'. یکی is the numeral 'one'. نامبردار is a modifier meaning 'the named/aforementioned' or 'renowned' and here functions as the thing to be identified/named. گو is the simple imperative 'say' (tell/name it). The sequence is imperative verbs followed by their object/description.
segments.s662-g179: ز ... و ز ... is a repeated poetic preposition meaning 'from/among' when listing groups: 'from merchants and from peasants (of) the town.' شهر follows to qualify دهقان ('peasants of the town'). The order is preposition + group1 + and + preposition + group2 + qualifier.
segments.s662-g180: کسی را uses کسی 'anyone' as the focal noun with را marking it as the (definite/emphatic) object/topic of the question. کجا باشد is an interrogative construction where کجا ('where' / rhetorical 'is there') combines with باشد (present/subjunctive/third-person 'be') to ask whether such a person exists. از نام بهر is a prepositional phrase 'with respect to name/fame' (i.e. 'known by name').
segments.s662-g181: ز بهر سپه is a prepositional phrase 'for the sake of the army' (ز + بهر + سپه). این درم is 'this dirham / this small sum' (demonstrative + noun). فام here is a noun meaning a small trace/token/mark attached to the coin (poetic/archaic usage). خواه is an imperative/verb 'ask/request' (desire) addressing the addressee to request that token.
segments.s662-g182: بزودی is an adverb 'soon/quickly'. بفرماید is a polite/formal verb meaning 'he will command/issue (an order)' (classical third-person form used to report that someone will have something done). از گنج شاه is the source phrase 'from the king's treasury' (از + گنج + شاه).
segments.s662-g183: بیامد is a past tense verb 'came/arrived' placed before its noun subject (a common classical/Persian poetic order). فرستاده is the subject 'the envoy/messenger' and خوش‌منش is an adjectival epithet 'courteous' modifying فرستاده.
segments.s662-g184: This is a nominal series of attributes: جوان 'young' + و (and) + خردمندی 'wisdom/prudence' + و (and) + نیکوکنش 'good conduct/behavior'. Adjectives and abstract nouns are strung together to describe the envoy; the coordinating و links the qualities.
segments.s662-g273: فرستاده آمد همانگه دوان is a compact clause: فرستاده (subject) + آمد (past tense 'came'). همانگه is an adverb meaning 'at that moment/then' modifying آمد. دوان is a present participle 'running/rushing' placed after the temporal phrase to describe the manner of the arrival.
segments.s662-g274: نیایش کنان پیش نوشین روان: نیایش is the noun 'prayer/supplication' and کنان is a participial form 'doing/performing' that turns the noun into a verbal manner ('praying'). پیش is a preposition 'before/in the presence of', and نوشین روان is a poetic epithet ('sweet‑souled') composed of the adjective نوشین modifying روان 'soul', together referring to the person before whom the praying occurs.
segments.s662-g275: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. رومی is the subject 'the Roman'. سر تاج کسری is the object phrase: سر 'head' (anatomical/top) with تاج کسری 'the crown of Khosrow' (genitive relationship); بدید is the past tense verb 'saw'. The clause thus reads 'when the Roman saw the crown of Khosrow (on the head)'.
segments.s662-g276: یکی باد سرد از جگر برکشید: یکی here is the indefinite 'a/certain', باد سرد 'a cold wind' is the subject phrase, از جگر is an ablative 'from (the) liver/heart' (poetic for 'from the heart'), and برکشید is a past verb 'lifted/rose'—a poetic way to say 'a cold wind arose from his heart.'
segments.s662-g277: به دل گفت is an idiom 'he said in his heart' (i.e., he thought to himself): به + دل marks the internal addressee. گفت is past tense 'said' (here introducing the thought). کینت 'your/this vengeance' is the topic and سزاوار گاه 'worthy/deserving of a time/occasion' is the predicate that will be specified by the next segment.
segments.s662-g278: بشاهی و مردی و چندین سپاه is a nominal series specifying what the vengeance is worthy of: بشاهی 'kingship/royalty', و مردی 'and manhood/valor', و چندین سپاه 'and several armies'. The conjunctions link nouns in a list that completes سزاوار گاه from the previous segment.
segments.s662-g279: وزان فیلسوفان رومی چهل: وزان here means 'and from them/of those', introducing the group; فیلسوفان رومی is 'the Roman philosophers' (plural). Classical Persian often places the numeral after the noun, so چهل ('forty') attaches to فیلسوفان here to mean 'forty (of) the Roman philosophers' or 'forty tongues among those philosophers'.
segments.s662-g280: زبان برگشادند پر باد دل: زبان 'tongue/language' (here 'tongue'/'language') + برگشادند 'they opened/spoke' = 'they opened/speaked tongues' (i.e., they spoke). پر باد دل is a descriptive phrase 'full of wind of heart' (پر 'full (of)' + باد 'wind' used metaphorically + دل 'heart'), meaning their hearts were puffed up/boastful; it modifies the manner or state accompanying their speech.
segments.s662-g713: همه گوش ودل سوی درویش دار is an imperative instruction. The objects (همه گوش و دل) precede the direction (سوی درویش) and the verb دار, an archaic/poetic short-form imperative meaning “keep/hold/turn” (here 'direct' or 'give'), comes at the end as is normal in Persian. سوی + noun indicates the target (‘toward the dervish’).
segments.s662-g714: همه کار او چون غم خویش دار is another imperative clause parallel to the first. همه کار او (‘all his affairs’) is the object; چون introduces a simile ‘as/like’; غم خویش (here written as خوی + ش) means ‘his own sorrow’ or ‘one’s own grief’; the final دار again is the imperative ‘hold/regard’. Note the split خوی + ش forms the possessive/’own’.
segments.s662-g715: ور introduces a conditional ‘if’. ایدونک is a small poetic particle (here functioning as a concessive/nuance: ‘even so’ or ‘for a while’); the clause then states that an enemy (دشمن) شود (‘becomes’ — شود is the present/stative/become verb) دوستدار (‘friendly, an admirer/supporter’). Word order is subject–predicate with the verb toward the end.
segments.s662-g716: تو در بوستان تخم نیکی بکار is an imperative addressed to ‘you’ (تو): در بوستان (‘in the garden’) is locative, تخم نیکی (‘the seed of goodness’) is the object, and بکار here is the imperative verb ‘plant/sow’ (classical Persian b-kār). Note that the provided lemma senses do not list the ‘plant’ sense, so the verb is understood from context as an imperative of کاشتن.
segments.s662-g717: چو is the subordinating conjunction ‘when’. از خویشتن here means ‘by himself/through his own action’. نامور is an adjective ‘renowned’. The phrase نامور داد داد uses داد first as the noun ‘justice’ and the second داد as the verb ‘gave/administered’ (a common classical structure: noun + verb), so it reads ‘gave/administered justice that made him renowned’ or ‘administered justice and became famed’.
segments.s662-g718: جهان گشت ازو شاد و او از تو شاد is two coordinated clauses. گشت is the past form of ‘become’ (‘became’); ازو means ‘because of him’/‘from him’; شاد = ‘happy’. The second clause mirrors the first: او گشت شاد از تو (he became happy because of you). The conjunction و links the two results.
segments.s662-g719: بر ارزانیان گنج بسته مدار: بر + noun expresses direction/target (‘toward/for’). ارزانیان here refers to those who bestow/give freely (sense ‘bestowers’); گنج بسته = ‘treasure closed’ (بسته as adjective ‘shut’). مدار in this poetic register functions in a prohibitive/instructional sense (‘do not …’) or is part of an admonition meaning ‘do not keep…’ — the provided lemma senses for مدار are nominal and do not reflect this verbal/imperative usage, so the precise verb sense is classical/poetic.
segments.s662-g720: ببخشای بر مرد پرهیزکار is an imperative: ببخشای here means ‘give/grant away’ (imperative/optative), بر = ‘to/for’, مرد = ‘man/person’, and پرهیزکار = ‘pious/abstinent’. The clause directs the addressee to bestow (their wealth) upon the pious man. Note the older verb form ببخشای where modern Persian would use ببخش.
segments.s662-g1: چنین is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' placed before the verb for emphasis; گوید is the third-person singular narrative/present 'says' (a literary historic present used to report). از + نامه باستان literally 'in/from the ancient book' marks the source; word order (adverb + verb + source) is typical of poetic register.
segments.s662-g2: ز here means 'from' (introducing the source phrase); گفتار is 'speech/words' and آن 'that' points back to a previously mentioned or implied passage; دانشی is a genitive construction 'knowledge of/knowledge' and راستان is an adjectival qualifier 'true/upright' modifying دانشی. The whole phrase specifies that what follows comes 'from the words of that true knowledge.'
segments.s662-g3: که here introduces a clause ('for/that/when'); آگاهی functions as 'news/information' and آمد is the simple past 'came.' به + آباد بوم is a prepositional phrase 'to the prosperous/inhabited land' (آباد qualifying بوم). The verb precedes the location phrase, which is normal in Persian narrative.
segments.s662-g4: بنزد is a literary preposition meaning 'to (the presence of) / near'; جهاندار is a title 'world‑holder' = 'sovereign'; کسری is the name 'Khosrow/Kisra' (proper noun as object of بنزد). ز روم adds the origin 'from Rome.' Word order places the court/location phrase after آمد in continuation of the report.
segments.s662-g5: The initial که introduces a temporal clause ('when/that'); تو 'you' is the subject; زنده بادی here uses an archaic/formal verbal ending -ی (بادی) that functions like a stative/optative form meaning 'you were/you might be alive' (literary). The second که introduces the following clause about the Caesar: قیصر 'Caesar' and بمرد is a classical-form verb meaning 'died' (narrative past/optative form used in epic register).
segments.s662-g6: زمان و زمین 'time/era and land' are coordinated objects; دیگری را uses دیگری 'another' with the object marker را to mark the definite object; سپرد is the simple past 'handed over/entrusted.' In context the verb's subject is the deceased Caesar (implicit), so the clause means he handed the reign/realm to another.
segments.s662-g7: پراندیشه is a compound adjective meaning 'full of thought / worried'; شد is the copular/past 'became'; جان کسری 'the life/soul of Khosrow' is the grammatical subject that follows the predicate in this poetic inversion; ز مرگ means 'because of/from death' and gives the cause of the distress.
segments.s662-g8: شد again 'became' introduces the second result clause; آن لعل رخساره is a descriptive noun phrase 'that ruby-like cheek/visage' (لعل 'ruby/deep red' modifying رخساره 'face'); چون is a simile marker 'like/as'; زرد برگ 'yellow/pale leaf' is the comparison (زرد here carries the sense 'pale/wan' in the image of a leaf). The clause describes a change of complexion using a standard poetic simile.
segments.s662-g593: چنین (fronted modifier) qualifies the following noun phrase and means “such.” دوستی here functions as a noun meaning “friend” (not the abstract ‘friendship’), followed by مرد نادان, a nominative identification: ‘(a) man, ignorant.’ بود is the past copula ‘was.’ The word order is modifier + head + identification + copula, typical of Persian poetic phrasing.
segments.s662-g594: سرشتش is سرشت (disposition, nature) with the enclitic -ش ‘his/her,’ so ‘his nature.’ بد is the predicate adjective ‘bad.’ و links the two predicates. رای گردان is a noun + adjective/participle: رای ‘opinion/counsel’ described by گردان ‘turning/revolving,’ i.e. ‘fickle counsel.’ بود is again the past copula. The segment states two qualities of the same subject (‘his disposition was bad and his counsel was changeable’).
segments.s662-g595: دگر ‘another/other’ opens this clause. آنک is an archaic relative/pronominal particle ‘who/that,’ introducing the speech or question. گوید here functions as a present-tense verb ‘says/asks,’ but the provided sense list did not include that lexical meaning, so its sense is flagged below. ستمکاره is the noun ‘oppressor.’ کی is the interrogative ‘who.’ ست is the archaic short form of the copula ‘is’ (est), completing the question ‘who is the oppressor?’
segments.s662-g596: بریده is a participial/adjectival form literally ‘cut off’ and here used figuratively ‘whose heart is broken/torn.’ دل is the noun ‘heart.’ ازشرم is a prepositional phrase ‘from/out of shame.’ و links the two interrogatives. بیچاره is the predicate noun/adjective ‘the unfortunate/poor wretch.’ کی … ست again frames the question ‘who is …?’ with ست as the copula.
segments.s662-g597: چوکژی is a locative noun ‘market-square’ (here adverbial context). کند is the present tense third-person singular of ‘to do/make’ and here forms the question ‘what does … do?’ followed by مرد بیچاره ‘the wretched man/poor fellow.’ خوان is used as ‘reciter/reader’ (a person who reads or sings), so the segment asks what a wretched reciter can do in the market-square. The order places the place-word first for emphasis.
segments.s662-g598: چوبی ‘wooden’ modifies شرمی (here ‘shame’ or ‘humiliation’) to produce a vivid, concrete image — literally ‘wooden shame’ (possibly implying being struck with a club). آرد is the present tense/past-form of ‘bring’ (here ‘brings’). ستمکاره خوان combines ‘oppressor’ with خوان ‘reciter/reader,’ i.e. ‘the oppressor (in relation to the) reciter’ or ‘the oppressor brings shame upon the reciter.’ The clause describes the oppressor’s action toward the reciter.
segments.s662-g599: هرآنکس ‘whoever’ + که introduces a relative clause. او پیشه گیرد is a nominalized action: او ‘he’ + پیشه ‘profession/trade’ + گیرد (present-subjunctive/indicative ‘takes up’), so together ‘whoever makes it his trade.’ دروغ (placed at the end) is the object, ‘lying/falsehood’: ‘whoever takes lying as his trade.’ Word order places the wh-word and relative clause before the object for emphasis.
segments.s662-g600: ستمکاره‌ای is s-temkāre plus an -ای ending giving an indefinite or characterizing sense ‘an oppressor’ or ‘one given to oppression.’ خوانمش is the verb خواندن with the object pronoun -مش attached; here it means ‘I call/name him’ (literally ‘I call him [so]’). بی‌فروغ is a compound adjective ‘without radiance’ (figuratively ‘dull/without glory’), predicating the name the speaker gives him. The clause is the speaker’s judgment: ‘I name him an oppressor, without radiance.’
segments.s662-g297: The paired particle چه ... چه ... is a correlative meaning “whether ... or ...”; here it introduces two parallel noun phrases. Note the poetic word order: خاقان چینی (khaghan + adjective) and در هند شاه (locative در هند placed before شاه) are inverted from more colloquial word order for metrical reasons.
segments.s662-g298: یکایک is an adverb/quantifier “one by one / each”; پرستند is a third-person plural verb used here as a general/habitual present “they revere/worship.” The object appears after the verb in poetic order: این تاج و گاه (‘this crown and (its) seat/occasion’), with و coordinating the two nouns (or linking crown to a following noun/adverb).
segments.s662-g299: اگر marks a conditional clause “if.” کودکی نارسیده is a noun + past participle: کودکی (‘a child’) qualified by نارسیده (‘not yet mature / not arrived’). بجای functions as a postpositional phrase “in place of / instead of,” so the whole segment means “if an unripe child instead (of someone)...”.
segments.s662-g300: سخن گفت is a compact verbal phrase meaning “uttered speech / speaks.” In epic poetry the verb گفت (literally ‘said’) is used in a general/habitual sense. The clause finishes with two adjectives/nominals describing manner: بی‌دانش “ignorant” and رهنمای “without a guide / (without) adviser,” linked by و.
segments.s662-g301: ندارد is the negative of داشتن (‘to have’) in third‑person singular: “he does not have.” Poetic inversion places the verb before the subject شهنشاه. ازو is the old fused form of از او, meaning “from/of him” (here ‘toward him’), and کین و درد are coordinated nouns ‘hatred (revenge) and sorrow.’
segments.s662-g302: که here is a causal conjunction “for / because.” شادست is the fused poetic form of شاد است (“is joyful”) in third person singular. The order is again poetic: predicate شادست + reason ازو + subject گنبد لاژورد (the lapis‑lazuli dome), i.e. ‘the dome is glad because of him.’
segments.s662-g303: همان = “the same / that same.” باژ here means a report/cry/dispatch (poetic word for message or outcry). روم names the source (Rome / Byzantium). آنچ is an archaic relative ‘that which’ (modern آنچه). بود is the past copula ‘was,’ and از نخست = ‘from the first / from the beginning’ — together forming a relative clause “that which was from the first.”
segments.s662-g304: سپاریم is a first‑person form of سپردن (‘to entrust / deliver’) used here as a promise ‘I will entrust / deliver.’ The conjunction و links the next action: عهدی بتازه درست literally ‘a covenant newly (بتازه) درست (made).’ درست here functions in a verbal sense (‘make/establish’) in poetic usage, so the phrase means ‘and I will establish a fresh covenant.’ Word order places the object (عهدی) and adverb (بتازه) before the verb.
segments.s662-g105: This is a sequence of prepositional phrases introduced by the preposition به (be, “to/for”) listing recipients: به یزدان پاک (to the Pure God), then joined by و (and) to the sun and the moon. Word order in Persian places the preposition before the noun; the adjective پاک follows the noun here for emphasis (poetic word order).
segments.s662-g106: Another coordinated prepositional phrase beginning with به (to). آذر گشسب here functions as a proper name (Azar-Gushasp), followed by a coordination و and a compound reading بتخت و کلاه that in context means 'to the throne and (to) the crown/hat' — the reading treats بتخت as a fused form (poetic orthography) meaning به تخت ('to the throne').
segments.s662-g107: This subordinate clause is introduced by که (ke, 'that/for'), followed immediately by کز (kaz, a poetic relative 'which/that') + هرچ ('whatever') and the locative phrase در پادشاهی اوست ('is in his kingdom'). The copula ست (ast, 'is') ends the clause; the whole clause functions as an explanatory relative ('for that which/whatever is in his kingdom').
segments.s662-g108: This main clause uses the poetic preposition ز (ze, 'from') + گنج کهن ('the ancient treasure') as source, followed by the verb پرکند (porkand, 'fills') which governs the object گاو پوست (cow-hide). The verb is present/past-poetic in form meaning 'he fills' or 'he fills up (with)'.
segments.s662-g109: This clause begins with نساید (nasāyad), here used in the lexical sense 'to rub/abrade' in an optative/wishful sense ('may ... not rub'). سرتیغ ما ('the tip of our blade') is the subject phrase and رانیام is the object/name; word order is subject–verb–object in poetic sequence, and the verb carries a negative wish ('may it not ...').
segments.s662-g110: This is an independent optative clause: حلال جهان ('what is lawful in the world') + باد (bād) used as an optative particle 'may' yields 'may the world's lawful (things)...' بر من حرام ('be forbidden on me') places the effect on the speaker; Persian uses بر + pronoun + adjective (حرام) to mean 'be forbidden to me.'
segments.s662-g111: بفرمود (befarmud) is an honorific past verb 'he ordered/commanded.' تا here introduces a purposive/subordinate clause 'that/so that': تا بر درش کرنای ('that at his door/gate trumpets ...'). بر درش is a locative 'on/at his gate' (در = 'gate/door', ش = his) and کرنای is the noun 'trumpet(s)'.
segments.s662-g112: دمیدند (damidand) is a past plural verb 'they blew.' It is followed by an instrumental phrase با سنج و هندی درای ('with sanj and (with) Indian -darāy'); با marks the instrument. In classical Persian listing, musical instruments are coordinated with و. Note that some instrument names are poetic/archaic compounds here (هندی درای likely describes Indian players or Indian instruments).
segments.s662-g369: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. تنگ here is a personal name (Tang) placed where a noun normally stands; اندر is a poetic preposition 'into/in', followed by آمد (past 'came'). به جای نشست is a fixed sequence meaning 'sat in/at the place' (به = to/at, جای = place, نشست = sat/settled). The whole clause is a temporal clause 'When Tang came in, he sat in his place.'
segments.s662-g370: بهرمهتری is an adverbial/locution meaning 'for precedence/advantage' or 'to show deference/recognize superiority'; شاه is the direct object/indirect target 'the king'; بنمود is the archaic past of بنمودن 'to show/make (a gesture)'; دست here is literal 'hand' and denotes a gesture. Word order is Verb (بنمود) after its indirect object (شاه) and the thing shown (دست) follows the verb.
segments.s662-g371: سرآمد here functions as an epithet 'the foremost/chief', followed by سخن گفتن 'speaker' (سخن گفتن literally 'speaking' used as 'speaker'), and موزه دوز is a compound noun 'slipper-maker' (موزه 'slipper', دوز from دوختن 'to sew' used as the agent). The phrase names the person who spoke first: 'the chief speaker, the slipper-maker.'
segments.s662-g372: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (here introducing elapsed time). ماه محرم 'the month Muharram' is a time phrase, گذشته is a participle meaning 'has passed/gone', and سه روز follows as the quantity 'three days'. Word order is typical Persian: preposition + time phrase + past participle + duration = 'three days have passed since Muharram.'
segments.s662-g373: This is a string of epithets in apposition describing a person: جهانجوی 'world-seeking, ambitious', دهقان 'farmer / rural landowner', آموزگار 'teacher'. Persian often stacks such modifiers without additional particles; they jointly label the speaker.
segments.s662-g374: چه گفت is the question 'what did (he) say' (چه = what; گفت here marks speaking), اندرین is the fused prepositional adjective 'in this', and گردش روزگار is a common idiom 'the turn/circuit of time/fate'. The clause asks what that named person said about the present turn of fortune.
segments.s662-g375: که introduces a clause 'that/which' or a general statement. روزی ... روزی uses parallel adverbial 'one day ... another day ...' (classical Persian uses روزی to mean 'a day; at times'), فرازست and نشیب are predicative: فرازست = 'is exalted/above' (فراز + است fused as -ست), نشیب = 'descent/low point'. The clause states the alternation of ups and downs in fortune.
segments.s662-g376: گهی is an adverb 'sometimes'. با + verb (خرامیم) means 'with/ by (doing)'; خرامیم is 1st person plural present 'we stride/prance' (خرامیدن), here used generically 'we sometimes stride proudly'. گه ... با نهیب contrasts the other case: گه 'at times' + با 'with' + نهیب 'a rebuke/thunderous cry'. The couplet balances proud advance and sudden rebuke as alternations of fortune.
segments.s662-g113: همه is a distributive determiner 'all/every' modifying the noun کوس (drums). The noun کوس (war-drum) follows; بر is the preposition 'on/upon' introducing the location phrase کوهه (archaic form of 'کوه', 'mountain/hill'). ژنده پیل is a poetic compound where ژنده ('ragged, tattered') qualifies پیل ('elephant'); the ezâfe linking them is omitted in verse. Together this phrase means 'all the drums upon the hill of the ragged elephant.'
segments.s662-g114: ببستند is a past tense, 3rd person plural verb ('they bound/tied/fastened' — here 'hung/fastened' the drums). The coordinating و links to شد, a past/perfective copula/verb meaning 'became/turned'. روی گیتی is a nominal phrase 'the face/surface of the world' serving as the subject of شد. چونیل is a poetic simile/adverbial phrase 'like indigo' describing how the world's face became; word order puts the result predicate at the end.
segments.s662-g115: سپاهی is a collective noun meaning 'army/host' (here subject). گذشت is the simple past 'passed/marched'. از introduces the origin 'from Madā'in' (مداین, Ctesiphon/the cities) and به + دشت indicates movement 'into/to the plain'. The normal S V + oblique phrase order is used.
segments.s662-g116: که here introduces the result/clause ('and/so that/when') describing the effect of the army's passing. دریای سبز is a metaphorical nominal phrase 'the green sea' (the grassy plain). اندرو is an archaic locative 'in it/within it' placed after the noun, and خیره گشت is a verb phrase meaning 'became astonished/stunned' (خیره 'astonished/staring' + گشت 'became'). The verb comes at the end as typical in Persian subordinate clauses.
segments.s662-g117: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of' introducing a causal phrase. نالیدن is a gerund/noun-like infinitive 'the wailing/clamour', governing the following objects بوق 'horn/trumpet' and (with و) رنگ درفش 'the colour/appearance of the banner' (رنگ modifying درفش). The phrase gives a cause or accompaniment to what follows.
segments.s662-g118: Another causal phrase introduced by ز. جوش here means 'surge/ardour/passion' (a figurative 'boil' of action) and takes سواران 'riders' as its possessor ('the riders' ardour'). زرینه کفش is a compact descriptive phrase: زرینه ('golden') qualifies کفش ('shoes/footgear'), producing 'the riders with golden shoes' (the ezâfe again is omitted in the poetic compression).
segments.s662-g119: ستاره is the vocative 'O star' (the addressee). توگفتی is second-person past 'you said'. The clause به آب اندرست is a predicative phrase with به 'to/in' + آب 'water' and اندرست (archaic copular/locative verb) meaning 'is in/within', so the whole means 'you said it is in the water.' Note the archaic verb اندرست used instead of modern است + در.
segments.s662-g120: سپهر روان is a compounded nominal phrase: سپهر 'the sky/heaven' modified by روان ('flowing/moving', an epithet). هم is the adverb 'also/too'. بخواب here functions as a locative/adverbial 'in sleep/asleep' and is linked by the archaic copula اندرست 'is within/among', so the clause reads 'the moving heavens, too, are in sleep.' The word order places the predicate (هم بخواب اندرست) after the subject phrase.
segments.s662-g121: چو here is a temporal conjunction attached to آگاهی ('news/awareness'), so چوآگاهی آمد means 'when the news came'. ب + قیصر (بقیصر) is the dative/benefactive prefix indicating 'to the Caesar'. ز شاه ('from the shah') gives the source. Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect but reads literally: 'when news came to the Caesar from the shah.'
segments.s662-g122: که introduces a consequent clause describing the Caesar's action. پرخشم is a predicate adjective 'full of wrath/furious'. ز ایوان is a source phrase 'from the iwan/portico', and بشد here is an archaic/past-inchoative use meaning 'he rose/issued' or 'it came to be', followed by با سپاه 'with (his) army' showing accompaniment.
segments.s662-g123: بیامد is the past tense 'he came'. ز عموریه gives the origin 'from Amorium'. تا before a place here functions as 'as far as / up to', so تا حلب marks the destination 'to Aleppo'. Classical/poetic order places these prepositional phrases after the verb.
segments.s662-g124: جهان کرد is an idiom 'he made (the) world'—with کرد functioning as causative 'made/caused'—and here means to 'fill the world with...'. پر is the adjective/particle 'full (of)' and heads the following list of nouns (جنگ و جوش و جلب) meaning 'war, turmoil/surge, and bringing/plunder'. The conjunctions و simply list the items.
segments.s662-g125: سواران رومی is the nominal subject 'Roman horsemen'. چو before a numeral here has a comparative/approximate sense ('as many as' / 'about'), so چو سیصد هزار means 'about/numbering some three hundred thousand'. This is a classical poetic way to give an approximate force size.
segments.s662-g126: حلب را is the definite object marked by را. گرفتند is the perfect past plural 'they seized/took'. یکسر functions adverbially 'entirely/utterly' and modifies حصار; حصار here is best read as 'siege' (i.e. they besieged it completely). Word order places the adverb before the noun it qualifies.
segments.s662-g127: سپاه اندر آمد is 'the army entered/arrived' with اندر ('in/into') marking location. ز هرسو 'from every side' gives source/direction and به جنگ 'to/into battle' marks the purpose or direction of movement. This is a compact classical ordering of source and goal phrases around the verb.
segments.s662-g128: نبد is an older form of نبُد 'there was not/there did not exist', used here as an existential negator. جنگشانرا is the definite object 'their fighting/war'. فراوان درنگ is a noun phrase 'much/great delay' (فراوان adjectival 'abundant'), so the whole line says there was no great delay in their fighting—i.e., they did not tarry.
segments.s662-g465: دگر is a preposed particle here meaning “another / furthermore.” The phrase بردباری و بخشایشست is a nominal predicate: بخشایشست is the archaic fused form of بخشایش است (“is forgiveness”), so the clause reads “(another) is patience and forgiveness.” Word order is Topic + Predicate with the copula attached to the final noun.
segments.s662-g466: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause “for / which,” and تن را uses را as the definite object/target marker meaning “for the body.” بدو (= به + او) here means “by them / to it,” referring back to the virtues. آرایشست is the fused archaic form of آرایش است (“is ornament”), so the final predicate is “(is) name and ornament.”
segments.s662-g467: بپرسید is a past narrative form “he asked.” کز is the classical contracted relative/partitive (= که + از) linking to the following noun phrase, here “of/from which.” نیکوی سودمند is a genitival description “useful goodness / beneficial virtue.”
segments.s662-g468: بگو is an imperative/quotational ‘say’ used to introduce the quoted question. ازچه is the interrogative ‘from what / why.’ گردد is the classical third-person singular (subjunctive/present) of شدن/gشتن “become.” چو here means “when,” and the second گردد repeats the same verb; بلند is an abstract “lofty / exalted.” The clause asks for the cause by which one becomes exalted when exaltation occurs.
segments.s662-g469: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed narrative phrase “he gave such an answer / thus answered”; داد is the past of دادن (“gave”) and پاسخ is the object. که introduces the content of the answer; آنک is an archaic demonstrative/continuative particle (= that/for) and از نخست means “from the first / from the outset.”
segments.s662-g470: بنیک و بد form a paired adjective phrase “good and bad” (بنیک is an archaic form meaning ‘goodly’). آزرم is an archaic/obscure noun here (context suggests something like ‘esteem / honor’), and هرکس بجست is a past contruction “whoever sought.” The literal sense: “whoever sought honor (by good or bad means).”
segments.s662-g471: بکوشید is a past narrative verb “he strove / endeavored.” تا here introduces a purposive/result clause “so that.” بردل is a fused prepositional phrase meaning “in the heart” (به + دل), and هرکسی here functions as “every person / each person.”
segments.s662-g472: ازو = از + او “from him / because of him.” رنج is “suffering / hurt.” بردن combined with رنج (رنج بردن) means “to suffer / to cause suffering / to endure suffering” — here the sense is passive/experiential “suffering from him.” نباشد is the negative subjunctive/present third-person “there be not,” introducing non-existence of that suffering, and بسی means “much / enough.” The whole clause: “that there not be much suffering from him.”
segments.s662-g377: سرانجام is an adverb placed up front meaning 'in the end/finally'. بستر بود تیره خاک is a nominal predicate with the copula بود (past tense 'was') linking subject and predicate: 'the bed was dark earth'. تیره is an adjective qualifying خاک; word order (adjective before noun) and the postverbal copula are typical of Persian poetic phrasing.
segments.s662-g378: This clause is elliptical: the verb (بود) is omitted and carried over from the previous clause. یکی را ... یکی را ... uses را as the object/affected marker with یکی ('one' / 'some person(s)') to mean 'for one (person) ... and for another ...'. فراز and مغاک are nouns showing the contrasting fates ('on high' / 'a chasm'). و simply coordinates the two alternatives.
segments.s662-g379: نشانی is the direct object 'a sign/trace' placed before the verb. نداریم is the negative present tense 'we do not have'. ازان رفته‌گان is a prepositional/genitive phrase 'of/from those gone'; ازان links to رفته‌گان ('the departed/those gone before').
segments.s662-g380: که introduces a descriptive relative clause referring back to 'رفته‌گان' ('who/that'). بیدار و شادند is a coordinate predicate ('they are awake and they are glad'), with شادند showing the 3rd‑person plural ending -ند. اگر here introduces an alternative/conditional sense 'or if / whether', leading into خفته‌گان ('the sleeping ones'); گان is the plural-forming element attached to خفته to make 'those who are asleep/entombed'.
segments.s662-g381: بدان گیتی functions as a locative phrase 'in that world' (بدان = 'in/to that', combined with گیتی 'world'). ار is an older form of اگر 'if' introducing the condition. چندشان برگ نیست literally 'there is not a leaf of theirs' — برگ ('leaf') here is figurative for a record or remembrance, and نیست negates existence. The clause thus reads 'if there is not a leaf (record) of them in that world.'
segments.s662-g382: همان به که ... نیست is a formula meaning 'it is the same as that ... does not exist' or 'it is as if ... were not'. آویزش مرگ is a possessive/genitive construction where آویزش (a 'holding'/'attachment'/'suspension') takes مرگ ('death'): 'death's hold/attachment'. نیست negates that phrase: 'as if death had no hold.'
segments.s662-g383: This is a paired conditional: اگر صد بود سال اگر بیست و پنج = 'if the year were a hundred, if (it were) twenty‑five' — repeating the particle اگر for emphasis/parallelism. بود is the past/copulative used here in a hypothetical sense ('were'). سال follows as the noun 'year'; the second if-clause omits the repeated copula (elliptical). The sense is comparative/indifferent to length of years.
segments.s662-g384: یکی شد literally 'became one' (یکی = one, شد = became) meaning 'it comes to the same' or 'it all amounts to one'. چو introduces a temporal clause 'when/whenever' and یاد آید is a fixed phrase 'the memory comes (to mind)' with آید (present/future‑oriented 'comes'). از روز رنج is a genitive phrase 'of a day of suffering' specifying what is remembered.
segments.s662-g393: نبندد is a negative present/jussive form meaning 'do not close/let not close' and governs دل as its object; اندر is the preposition 'in/within' placed before the locative noun (classical word order). سپنجی is an adjectival/qualifying form ('spongy') modifying the following noun سرای ('house/dwelling'), so the literal order is 'do not close the heart in a spongy dwelling.'
segments.s662-g394: خرد یافته is a compound participial phrase: خرد 'wisdom' with یافته (past participle) 'having obtained / acquired' — together 'having obtained wisdom' or 'wise.' مردم پاکرای follows as a noun phrase 'people of pure intent' describing those whom (or which) wisdom has found; word order is noun/participle then qualifier (people + adjective).
segments.s662-g395: بگاه here introduces a temporal clause 'at the time when / when.' بسیجیدن is an older/poetic infinitive/verb 'to muster/assemble' and is used impersonally with مرگ 'death' as the agent — 'when death musters/gathers.' می (separated here) is the noun 'wine' introduced as the following clause's topic; the sequence places the time/clause before the comment about wine.
segments.s662-g396: چو begins a simile/temporal clause 'when/as'; پیراهن is a garment image (here 'robe/shroud') and شعر is the unexpected 'garment' being commented on — 'poetry as a garment.' باشد is the copula ('is / will be') in subjunctive/optative register, and بدی qualifies it as worthless/bad. The clause reads: 'when death comes, (though) wine is like a garment, poetry will be no good.'
segments.s662-g397: فسرده modifies تن as a past‑participle/adjectival form meaning 'cold' (here physical corpse or chilled body) and is placed before the noun, a common classical order. اندر میان is a double locative 'in the midst of / among,' and گناه names what surrounds the body: 'a cold body in the midst of sin.'
segments.s662-g398: روان is the soul/spirit and serves as the subject for the following clause; سوی introduces the goal 'toward' and فردوس is the destination 'Paradise.' گم‌کرده is a compound participle 'having lost' modifying راه 'way/path' — together 'having lost the way to Paradise' (word order: subject + direction + adjectival phrase describing the lost path).
segments.s662-g399: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'; یاران is the genitive/oblique 'companions.' بسی ماند is an idiomatic sequence meaning 'many remained' or 'much was left' (بسی here an adverb of quantity modifying ماند 'remained'). و چندی گذشت is a parenthetical time marker 'and some time passed' — گذشت is the past tense 'passed' and چندی 'a while.'
segments.s662-g400: تو is the vocative/subject 'you'; با introduces accompaniment 'with' and جام is the instrument/companion 'cup' (metonymically 'wine/cup'). همراه مانده is a perfective participial phrase 'remained accompanied' or 'stayed with (as companion)'; به دشت sets the location 'on the plain.' The whole clause: 'you remained on the plain, the cup as your companion.'
segments.s662-g561: This clause opens with a rhetorical/exclamatory question. چه (che) functions as an interrogative/exclamative 'what' or 'what can one say'; گوید here would normally be the verb 'says', but the supplied lemma senses do not include the verbal meaning (so treat as verbal 'says'). که introduces the subordinate clause 'that you know that ...'; دانی is 2nd-person present 'you know'. The second که begins the content clause 'that Shadi ...' and شادی here functions as a proper name (Shadi) rather than the common noun 'joy'.
segments.s662-g562: This is a simple verbless/past-copular clause describing relation: برادر 'a brother' is the predicate noun; بود is the past copula 'was'; با 'with' introduces the companion; دلارام is a female proper name; دوست here means 'friend' or 'beloved' and restates the affectionate relationship. Word order (predicate before بود) is normal in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s662-g563: دگر introduces 'another' or 'on the other hand'; آنک is a relative pronoun 'he who'/'that who'; پرسد (present/factual) 'asks' is the verb governing the following prepositional phrase ز کار زمان 'about/from the affairs/works of the time' (ز + کار + زمان). The phrase means 'he who asks about the affairs of the age.'
segments.s662-g564: زمانی here means 'at times'/'sometimes' (temporal adverbial); کزو = 'from which' (ک + ز + او contracted) points back to 'the time/affairs' mentioned; گم 'lost' and شود 'becomes' form a compound 'becomes lost/confused'; بدگمان is a predicative adjective meaning 'distrustful/suspicious' or 'full of doubt.' The clause says that such a questioner sometimes becomes confused and mistrustful because of the times.
segments.s662-g565: روا باشد is a fixed impersonal expression 'it is fitting/allowable'; ار is the conditional 'if'; چند here has the sense 'a little'/'somewhat' (qualifying the praising); بستایدش is a verbal form meaning 'he praises him' or 'one praises him' — together: 'it is fitting that if someone praises him even a little...' Note that باشد is subjunctive/optative in tone in this idiom.
segments.s662-g566: هم 'also' links the thought; اندر ستایش 'in praise' is the locative phrase; بیفزایدش is a causative/optative verb 'one should add to him/it' (i.e., increase the praise). The whole segment means 'one should also add to the praise.'
segments.s662-g567: دگر again marks 'another' case; آنک is the relative 'who/that'; پرسید is past 'asked'; ازمرد is the prepositional compound 'from/of a man' (از + مرد); دوست here is the noun 'friend.' The clause describes 'the other (one) who asked of a man about a friend.'
segments.s662-g568: ز 'from' introduces the generalization; هر 'every' qualifies دوستی 'friendship' (here meaning any friendship or relation); یارمندی denotes faithful help/companionship or loyal favour in friendship; نکوست is the predicate 'is good' — so the clause states a general maxim: 'from every friendship, loyal support is good.'
segments.s662-g153: The prepositional phrase sets the standard of comparison: از + noun phrase = “relative to / given.” The genitive chain اندازه لشکر شهریار is head-final: اندازه (size) governed by لشکر (army) which is in turn specified by شهریار (the king).
segments.s662-g154: کم آمد is an idiomatic past construction meaning “was insufficient / fell short” (literally “came little”); درم تنگ places the noun ‘dirhams’ before an adjective notionally meaning ‘tight/scarce’; the numeral سیصد هزار gives the amount lacking. Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s662-g155: بیامد is simple past “came.” The phrase برشاه = “before/to the king.” موبد چوگرد is a noun phrase ‘the mobed Chogird’ (title + name); here چوگرد is a name or identifier (no supplied lexical sense fits exactly). The verb-first order (verb then locative phrase) is common in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s662-g156: به گنج = “to/at the treasury” (به as locative). آنچ بود از درم is a relative/nominal phrase “that which was of/consisting of dirhams,” with بود as the past copula. یاد کرد is a light-verb construction meaning “mentioned / reported” (یاد + کرد). The whole clause: “he mentioned how many dirhams were in the treasury.”
segments.s662-g157: دژم کرد = “made (his face) sullen/grew grim” where دژم (sullen) precedes the light verb کرد. شاه is the subject; اندران کار = “in/at that affair” (اندران = “therein/at that”), and چهر (“face/appearance”) indicates the grim expression. The clause describes the king’s stern reaction.
segments.s662-g158: بفرمود is the polite/honorific past “he commanded/ordered.” تا here introduces the subordinate clause meaning “that” (so that/that), followed by رفت (go) and the name بوزرجمهر (Bozorgmehr) as the person to be sent. The structure is a classical command: “he ordered that Bozorgmehr go.”
segments.s662-g159: بدو = “to him.” گفت‌گر is a speech-introducing formula here (literally ‘he said’ / ‘he who says’), used to launch direct speech. گنج شاهی = “the king’s / royal treasury” (شاهی modifying گنج), and تهی = “empty.” Together they introduce the content of the speech: “If the royal treasury is empty…”
segments.s662-g160: چه باید مرا is a rhetorical/exclamatory question: چه باید = “what (is the use/need)?” م is an archaic/clipped form of ‘هست’ meaning roughly “is/there is (for me).” را is the object/definiteness marker attached to تخت شاهنشهی = “the imperial throne.” The literal order is classical: “What is there for me (in) the imperial throne?” → “What use is the imperial throne to me?”
segments.s662-g257: نخواهیم is a first-person-plural negative form of خواستن used here to mean 'we will not / we shall not accept'; روزی (sustenance/livelihood) follows it as the thing being refused. جز 'except' introduces the sole permitted source, and از گنج داد is a prepositional phrase 'from the treasure he gave' (داد here is the past verb 'gave'). Word order is marked: negative verb + noun (object/abstract) + exception phrase.
segments.s662-g258: درم زو مخواه is an imperative prohibition: مخواه is the negative imperative 'do not ask/seek', with زو (archaic poetic ز + او) meaning 'from him'. The following و مکن هیچ یاد links another prohibition: مکن (do not do) + هیچ یاد 'any mention/reminder' — literally 'do not make any mention'. The two clauses are coordinated by و.
segments.s662-g259: هم‌اکنون (an adverb) opens the clause 'right now'. بازگردان is an imperative verb 'return / give back' addressed to someone; the direct object شتر (camel) precedes it, which is typical in poetry where word order is flexible. به راه is the directional phrase 'onto the road/path'.
segments.s662-g260: درم خواه is an (elliptical) imperative construction: خواه here carries the sense 'ask (for) / desire' with درم 'a dirham' as its object. وز is an archaic/poetic preposition here meaning 'from' (equivalent to از); since the provided sense list doesn't include that use it is marked separately. موزه دوزان is a compound plural noun 'slipper (shoe) stitchers' (the people from whom one must not request), and مخواه is the negative imperative 'do not ask'. The clause therefore contrasts asking a dirham in general with forbidding asking it from those slipper-makers.
segments.s662-g261: فرستاده برگشت is a simple past clause 'the messenger returned'. The coordinating و + شد here links a resultant state: شد با درم indicates 'he was/appeared with a dirham' or 'he came back with a dirham' (شد functioning as a light auxiliary/verb of becoming in narrative). با درم is an adjunct 'with a dirham'.
segments.s662-g262: دل کفشگر گشت is 'the shoemaker's heart became' (گشت/گشتن = become). پر here is the adjective 'full (of)' and takes the following nouns as contents: درد و غم 'pain and sorrow'. Word order is possessor (دل کفشگر) + predicate (گشت پر ...).
segments.s662-g263: شب آمد is a formulaic past-tense clause 'night came' (an introductory temporal phrase). غمی شد 'grief came into being' uses شد in the sense 'became/arose', and ز گفتار شاه is an ablative phrase 'from the king's speech' indicating the source of that grief.
segments.s662-g264: خروش جرس is a genitive/nominal compound 'the clamor/ringing of the bell(s)'. خاست is a past tense/poetic form meaning 'arose' or 'began', and از بارگاه specifies the origin 'from the (royal) court'. The clause describes a loud sound emanating from the palace.
segments.s662-g41: یکی جای دورش فرود آورید is an imperative clause: یکی ('one' = 'some/a certain') modifies جای ('place'), دور + ش is 'دورش' = 'far/remote' with the clitic -ش referring to the person to be put down, and فرود آورید is the polite/imperative plural of آوردن 'to bring/set down' (literally 'cause to descend' → 'set down/put'). The whole phrase means 'set him down in some distant place.'
segments.s662-g42: بدان نامه پادشا ننگرید is an instruction: ب- is the prepositional prefix 'to/for' attached to the following element (here functioning with the demonstrative/phrase), نامه is 'letter', پادشا is an abbreviated poetic form of پادشاه 'king', and ننگرید is a negative jussive/imperative of نگریستن 'to look, to show' meaning 'do not let ... look/see'. Together: 'Do not let the king see that letter.'
segments.s662-g43: یکی هفته is an adverbial phrase 'for one week' (یکی here as numeral 'one'); هرکش که is an indefinite relative 'whoever (that)'; بد رای is a compound where بد 'bad/unfavorable' qualifies رای 'opinion/counsel', and زن is the object 'about the woman'—so 'whoever had an unfavorable opinion of the woman.'
segments.s662-g44: به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'near/close to', قیصر is the proper name 'Caesar', شدند is the past plural form of شدن ('they became/they came/they were'), here functioning to indicate that an assembly came together, and انجمن 'assembly/meeting' names what was formed; the clause means 'they assembled near Caesar.'
segments.s662-g45: سرانجام is an adverb 'finally/at last'; گفتند is the past plural 'they said' (here introducing reported speech); ما is the pronoun 'we'; کهتریم is an adjectival predicate with the 1st-person-plural ending -یم meaning 'we are inferior/lesser.' The clause reports: 'Finally they said, "We are inferior..."'.
segments.s662-g46: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/against' (here marking the object/source of transgression), فرمان 'command/order', شاه جهان is a title 'the King of the World' (شاه + جهان), and نگذریم is 1st-person-plural present/subjunctive negative of گذشتن/گذریدن meaning 'we do not pass/ we will not transgress'—so 'we will not transgress the king of the world's command.'
segments.s662-g47: سزا 'desert/punishment' precedes the rest for emphasis, خود 'his own' emphasizes possession, ز کسری means 'from Khosrow' (کسری = Kisra/Khosrow), چنین نامه 'such a letter', and بود is the past copula 'was'. Literally: 'Such a letter from Khosrow was his own punishment.'
segments.s662-g48: نه is the negation 'not', برکام (بر کام) idiomatically means 'to one's taste/pleasure' (here 'to his liking'), بایست is the modal 'ought/should' (here used in the past narrative sense), بدکامه is an adjective 'ill-fated / not to one's wish' (بد- 'bad' + کام 'wish'), and بود 'was'; the clause says 'It was not to his liking; it was ill‑fated.'
segments.s662-g241: بدو (a prepositional clitic meaning 'to him/unto him') precedes گفت (the verb 'said'), giving the phrasing 'he said to him'. شاه is the subject 'the king'. ای خردمند مرد is a vocative phrase addressing the addressee ('O wise man'), placed after the subject as direct speech begins; classic Persian often strings subject + vocative like this before the quoted question.
segments.s662-g242: چرا is the question word 'why' fronted. The clause follows an SOV order: دیو (subject) + چشم تو را (direct object, with را marking definiteness/object) + تیره کرد (past tense 'made/darkened') — so literally 'why did the demon your eye-OBJ darken'. تو is the possessor of چشم; را attaches to چشم to mark it as the object.
segments.s662-g243: برو is the imperative 'go' addressing the addressee. همچنان is an adverb 'still / in the same manner' that modifies the following imperative بازگردان ('return' or 'give back'), so the pair functions as two linked commands: 'Go on; return the camel.' شتر is the direct object placed after the verb (no object marker).
segments.s662-g244: مبادا introduces a negative purpose or warning 'lest / may it not be that...'. کزو is a compound relative/adverb 'from which / from whom' and points to the source; خواهیم is first‑person plural (here modal/volitional) meaning 'we will/would desire' or 'we might seek'. و connects to در, which here remains elliptical or part of an idiom; the whole clause expresses a caution ('lest we demand silver from him and ...').
segments.s662-g245: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. بازارگان is the noun 'merchant' (subject), بچه گردد is a predicate with بچه 'child/inexperienced' + گردد (present/subjunctive 'becomes'), giving 'when the merchant becomes childlike'. دبیر follows as the topic/subject of the resulting clause: 'the scribe...' — classical Persian often sequences clauses this way.
segments.s662-g246: This is a coordinate list of predicates/adjectival descriptions applied to دبیر: هنرمند 'skillful/artful', و (...) بادانش (here spelled together meaning 'with-knowledge' = 'learned'), و یادگیر 'and a learner' (one who learns). The conjunctions و simply link the adjectives in a triadic description.
segments.s662-g247: چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause. فرزند ما 'our son' is the subject; برنشیند is an older/poetic third‑person singular form meaning 'sits upon / mounts' (here 'ascends the throne'). بتخت is a fused form meaning 'on the throne' (orthographically merged), but its listed senses are uncertain in the glossary — in context it functions as the prepositional complement 'on the throne'.
segments.s662-g248: دبیری is the noun 'secretaryship / the office of secretary' (object/predicative noun). ببایدش is a compound modal: بباید (it must be / it is necessary) + ـش (the enclitic pronoun 'for him'), giving 'a secretary is required for him'. پیروزبخت is an adjectival predicate 'fortunate/prosperous' that comments on the result
segments.s662-g17: که here introduces a wish/optative clause (poetic 'may ...'). یزدان is the subject 'God'. تو را uses را after the pronoun to mark the person who receives the gift (dative/accusative marking in classical style). زندگانی is the thing given ('life'), and دهاد is the archaic/poetic third‑person present/subjunctive form of دادن used optatively ('may give'). Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect but follows Subject – (to) object – Verb.
segments.s662-g18: This is a coordinate clause with an omitted subject (the same یزدان). همت خوبی and کامرانی are direct objects of دهاد; همت ('ambition/resolve') is qualified by خوبی ('goodness, noble'), then و links کامرانی ('success, prosperity'). دهاد repeats the optative/benefactive verb 'may (he) give'. The verb again comes after its objects, typical of Persian poetic inversion.
segments.s662-g19: نزاید is the poetic/archaic verbal form from zâd/زاد meaning 'is born / comes into being'. جز از مرگ is an excluding phrase 'except from (or: except to) death' (i.e., 'only to die'). را is the object/contrast marker attached in front of جانور ('creature, living being'); the verb appears before the noun here by poetic inversion but the sense is 'a creature is born only to die'.
segments.s662-g20: سرای here denotes 'abode' or 'dwelling' (poetic word order places it before the predicate). سپنجست is an adjective/predicative word meaning 'uncertain/unstable' or 'perishable'. و connects the following clause; ما is the pronoun 'we'. بر گذر literally 'on the passage/road' (بر = on/upon; گذر = passage/way) meaning 'we are passing by' or 'we pass along the way'.
segments.s662-g21: اگر introduces a concessive/conditional clause ('if/whether'). تاج ساییم is poetic: تاج 'crown' plus ساییم (a form of ساییدن here used idiomatically as 'we wear/occupy' the crown). و‌گر is 'or if' (a fused form of و اگر). خود و ترگ juxtaposes 'ourselves' with 'rags' (ترگ = 'rag, tatter'), i.e. 'whether high (crowned) or low (in rags)'.
segments.s662-g22: رهایی 'release, freedom' is the object. نیابیم is the first‑person plural subjunctive/optative (with negative prefix here), formed from یافتن and used to express inability or inevitability ('we do not / will not obtain'). از چنگ مرگ is an ablative phrase 'from the grasp/claw of death' (چنگ = claw/talon used metaphorically). The clause therefore says we will not find escape from death's clutch.
segments.s662-g23: چه ... چه ... sets up a 'whether ... or ...' comparison (both possibilities included): قیصر (Caesar) and خاقان (Khaghan). چو آید زمان is a temporal subordinate 'when the time comes' (چو = when; آید = comes; زمان = time). The whole segment prepares the general statement about rulers' fate.
segments.s662-g24: بخاک اندر آید is a verbal phrase 'comes into the earth / returns to dust' (بـ = to/into + خاک = earth/dust; اندر آید = enters/comes in). سرش = 'his head' (سر + enclitic ش = his). بی‌گمان 'undoubtedly' is an adverb emphasizing certainty. Word order again is poetic, with the locative/goal phrase up front and the possessed noun appearing after.
segments.s662-g193: چنین گفت is an adverb + verb sequence: چنین (thus) modifies گفت (said), a third-person past verb. The following phrase کای پرخرد مایه‌دار is a nominative/title phrase (کای = the Kay/kingly title) with two epithets (پرخرد, مایه‌دار) attached; in poetic Persian epithets often follow the title, so this whole nominal group identifies the speaker.
segments.s662-g194: This segment lists an amount and unit. چهل is the cardinal numeral 'forty' modifying من (here a classical unit of weight/measure, not the pronoun); درم names the coin ('dirham'). هرمنی صدهزار is a noun + numeral/value: هرمنی (name of a coin or unit) followed by صدهزار ('one hundred thousand') giving the value per هرمنی. Word order in poetry can place the value after the unit.
segments.s662-g195: بدو (to him) is an allative/dative clitic-like adverb placed before the subject; کفشگر (shoemaker) is the subject, and گفت (said) is the past-tense verb. The clause من این دهم follows: من = 'I', این = 'this', and دهم here is a first-person present/first-person verb-form meaning 'I give/take' (poetic contraction of دادن/گرفتن) — context shows the speaker declaring what he will do with 'this.'
segments.s662-g196: سپاسی = 'a thanks' or 'as thanks' (object/phrase indicating the item is accepted as thanks). ز is the poetic form of از ('from'), so ز گنجور = 'from the treasury' (گنجور = treasury/treasure-house). بر سر نهم is an idiomatic sequence: بر سر = 'upon the head' and نهم is a first-person verb form ('I place / I put'), so together 'I will place it on my head' — an idiom meaning to accept or to put as a sign of honor.
segments.s662-g197: بیاورد is a past-tense verb ('he/it brought' or 'he produced'). The list قپان و سنگ و درم is a simple coordinated noun list joined by و: قپان (a cap/hood or article of clothing), سنگ (here likely 'gem' in the sense of precious stone), and درم (dirham, coin).
segments.s662-g198: This is a negative existential clause in archaic/poetic order. نبد is an older negative past copula 'there was not' (negated existence). هیچ دفتر = 'no/none of a ledger' (هیچ following the verb is common in classical/poetic Persian). به کار و قلم ('for business and pen') indicates the ledger's purpose — i.e., there was not a single notebook/ledger for business or for writing with a pen.
segments.s662-g199: چو introduces a temporal clause meaning 'when'. بازارگان را uses را to mark the affected party/experiencer (the merchants). درم سخته شد literally 'the dirham became hard' — سخته (hard/difficult/scarce) with شد ('became') indicates scarcity or that money became hard to get for merchants.
segments.s662-g200: فرستاده ... شد uses فرستاده as a past participle/agent ('sent') with شد to form 'was sent' (or it can be read as 'a messenger was sent'). زان کار = 'from/for that affair' (زان = from that/therefore). پردخته شد is a passive/ resultative: پردخته (well‑dealt/settled) + شد = 'was settled/handled'. The two parts together say that a messenger was dispatched and the matter was taken care of.
segments.s662-g161: بروهم is an imperative/command form (poetic contraction) meaning 'go and bring/assemble' or 'go together' — here used as a command to dispatch someone. کنون is an adverb 'now' placed after the verb for emphasis. ساروان را uses را as the definite/direct‑object marker (the caravan‑leaders are the people to be summoned). بخواه is an imperative of خواستن 'to ask/call/command'—so the clause as a whole reads 'Go now and summon the caravan leaders.'
segments.s662-g162: This clause continues the orders. هیونان is the plural of هیون 'beasts/pack‑animals' (the animals of a caravan). بختی is an adjective/adverbial ('fortunate, prosperous') qualifying those animals or their loads. برافگن is a poetic verbal form meaning 'set ablaze/put in motion/launch' (here a figurative imperative idea 'send forth' or 'set onto the road'). به راه is a directional prepositional phrase 'onto the road / on the way.' Grammatically the noun + qualifier + verb + direction sequence is typical of Ferdowsi's poetic order.
segments.s662-g163: صد 'a hundred' is the quantity placed before the source phrase. از introduces the source 'from the treasure.' گنج مازندران is a genitive phrase 'the treasure of Mazandaran.' بارکن is an imperative 'load!' (بار + کن), commanding that a hundred loads be taken from that treasure.
segments.s662-g164: وزو is an archaic/poetic contraction (here functionally 'from it' or 'of that') introducing an additional instruction; Persian epic often uses such condensed forms. بیشتر 'more' modifies بار 'loads' (so 'more loads'). دینار is the object 'dinar(s),' and کن is the imperative 'do/make' attached to the verb phrase (i.e. 'load more dinars'). The clause gives the further order to load additional money from the same source.
segments.s662-g165: بشاه جهان is a dative phrase 'to the king of the world' (به + شاه + جهان) placed before the reporting verb for emphasis. گفت is the past‑tense reporting verb 'said.' بوزرجمهر is the speaker's name placed after گفت in this poetic word order, so the whole literal order reads 'To the king of the world said Bozorgmehr' (= 'Bozorgmehr said to the king of the world').
segments.s662-g166: که‌ای is a vocative/introductory particle here ('O' / 'you who are'), addressing the king directly; که in older Persian often introduces direct address. شاه is the addressee 'king.' با is the preposition 'with,' linking qualities that follow. دانش, داد, and مهر are a coordinated series of nouns ('knowledge, justice, and affection/kindness') joined by و 'and'; they function as attributes of the king in the vocative phrase.
segments.s662-g167: سوی introduces direction 'toward(s) / to' and governs the genitive phrase 'گنج ایران' ('the treasure of Iran'). درازست is a poetic fused form meaning 'is long' (دراز + است), placed before the noun it describes. راه 'road' is the logical subject; the poetic order places the predicate before the subject: literally 'Toward the treasure of Iran long is the road.'
segments.s662-g168: تهی‌دست و بیکار are a coordinate predicate 'impoverished and idle' describing سپاه 'the army,' which appears at the end as the subject. باشد is the present/subjunctive/formal copula 'will be / shall be / is to be' used here in narrative to state the expected condition. The clause therefore states that the army will be or becomes impoverished and idle.
segments.s662-g513: ز گیتی is a poetic prepositional/locative phrase meaning 'in/of the world' (ز used as a literary 'from/in' marker). زیانکارتر is the comparative adjective 'more harmful' (the -تر comparative suffix); کارچیست is effectively کار چیست fused in classical style and means 'what work/thing is' — together: 'What thing in the world is more harmful?'. Word order is topical (location first) then adjective then the head noun + copula.
segments.s662-g514: که introduces a relative clause modifying کار ('that/which'). بر کرده خود is a prepositional phrase 'over/on one's own deed' with خود emphatic 'one's own'. بباید is the modal 'one must/ought' and combines with the following verb گریست to mean 'must weep' (classical usage where بباید + bare verb expresses obligation). گریست here is a non‑past/subjunctive/infinitival form 'to weep' used after the modal.
segments.s662-g515: ز چیزی is a classical genitive/partitive 'of/something of' and که introduces the relative clause 'that/which'. مردم is the subject 'people' and همی‌پرورد is the archaic/persianised present form with the older prefix همی (continuous/habitual) + root پرورد 'nurture/raise' meaning 'people nurture/foster'.
segments.s662-g516: چه چیزیست = 'what thing is it' (interrogative). کان here is an archaic relative/particle linking back to the preceding clause (classical 'that/which'); it does not carry the modern senses listed and functions to introduce the clause 'which passes sooner'. زودتر is the comparative 'more quickly/sooner' and بگذرد is the present/subjunctive form 'passes' used after the comparative to express 'pass away sooner'.
segments.s662-g517: ستمکاره کش is a compound: ستمکاره 'one who does injustice' plus the agentive/derivative element کش 'one who (does)'; together 'the oppressor'. نزد is the preposition 'near/before/in the presence of' and اوشرم is a proper name (here in classical form). نیست is the negated existential/copula 'is not/there is not', making the whole clause a rhetorical negation ('Is there no oppressor who feels shame before Ousharm?').
segments.s662-g518: کدامست is the interrogative 'which is it/which (one) is' and کش again forms 'one who...'. مهر وآزرم (literally 'love and ___') is a coordinated noun phrase meaning 'love and reverence/awe' here; the whole predicate مهر وآزرم نیست ('has no love nor reverence') is negated by نیست, so the line asks rhetorically 'Which man is there who lacks both love and reverence?'
segments.s662-g519: تباهی بگیتی is a fronted noun phrase 'destruction/ruin in the world' (بگیتی = 'in the world' with the older prepositional b-). ز گفتار is 'from/sourced in speech' using the preposition ز. کیست is the interrogative 'what/ who is it', so the clause asks 'What in the world is the destruction that comes from speech?'
segments.s662-g520: دل دوستانرا = 'the heart(s) of the friends' where دوستانرا carries the object/case marker -را attached to دوستان (classical particle use). پر آزار is a predicative phrase 'full of harm' (پر = 'full (of)'). کیست again makes this interrogative: 'What is it that fills friends' hearts with harm/suffering?'. Word order places the affected phrase before the interrogative verb.
segments.s662-g705: چو + یابد (چویابد) is a conditional/temporal clause: چو = 'when/if' and یابد is 3rd-person present (here 'finds'). خردمند is the noun subject 'the wise man'. نزد تو راه is an adverbial phrase meaning 'a way to your presence' (نزد = 'in the presence of / toward', تو = 'you', راه = 'way'); the word order is poetic but the sense is 'finds a way to reach you'.
segments.s662-g706: بماند is an optative/jussive-form here meaning 'may ... remain' (a wish or benediction). بتو is a dative 'to you / for you' and governs the three coordinated nouns (تاج، تخت، کلاه) which are the things hoped to remain. The conjunctions و simply join the nouns.
segments.s662-g707: هرآنکس که = 'whoever / anyone who' introduces a relative clause. باشد is the 3rd-person present (here functioning as 'is' / 'may be') describing existence or status. تو را زیردست uses topical/emphatic را after تو and places زیردست ('underling, subordinate') after it: literally 'whoever is, your-underling' = 'whoever is under you.'
segments.s662-g708: مفرمای is an imperative/polite command-form meaning 'cause / see to it / do not allow' (here used in an admonitory sense: 'do not let'). در بی‌نوایی نشست is the clause governed by that imperative: در = 'in', بی‌نوایی = 'poverty/destitution', نشست = 'to sit' (state of sitting/being seated). Together the phrase means 'let [him] not sit in poverty' / 'do not allow [him] to be left in want.'
segments.s662-g709: بزرگان و آزادگان is a coordinated noun phrase 'the great (nobles) and the free (noble commoners)'. را here marks them as the topical/definite group being spoken about. بشهر = 'to the city' (ب + شهر, poetic prepositional form); the order places destination after the people in a compact, poetic clause.
segments.s662-g710: ز = poetic 'from' or 'by'; داد تو = 'your justice' (داد = justice, تو = your). باید که is a modal construction 'it is necessary / they must' and یابند is the 3rd-person plural subjunctive/optative meaning 'find/obtain' (here 'obtain/receive advantage'). بهر follows as a postpositive adverb meaning 'for advantage / for their benefit.' The whole clause says these people should, through your justice, obtain benefit.
