segments.s662-g711: ز نیکی = 'from (your) goodness / from kindness' (ز is the poetic preposition 'from'). فرومایه را is the definite/marked direct object 'the low-born / base people' (را marks emphasis/definiteness). دور دار is an imperative-like combination meaning 'keep (them) distant' or 'keep (them) away' (دور = 'far / away', دار = 'hold/keep'); together: 'keep the low-born away from your goodness.'
segments.s662-g712: به = 'to / for'; بیدادگر مرد = 'an unjust / tyrannical man' (بیدادگر qualifying مرد). مگذار is the negative imperative 'do not allow / do not place' and governs کار ('affair, business, function'): مگذار کار = 'do not entrust the affair' or 'do not put the work [in his hands]'. The sequence admonishes not to give responsibilities to a tyrant.
segments.s662-g345: برین برنهادند و گشتند باز is a compact poetic clause: برین (بر + این) is a demonstrative/adverbial 'on/thereupon/these', برنهادند is the past tense verb 'they raised/took up' (بر + نهادند), and گشتند باز 'they turned/returned (back)'. Word order places the verb before or with its object as is common in poetic prose; و simply links the two verbs.
segments.s662-g346: همه پاک بردند پیشش نماز: همه 'everyone', پاک functions adverbially ('with purity/devoutly' or 'purely'), بردند is the past verb 'they took/performed' used idiomatically with نماز (نماز بردن = to perform prayer), پیشش 'before him/in his presence' and نماز is the object. The verb بردند precedes its object in the clause, a normal poetic order here.
segments.s662-g347: ببد شاه چندی بران رزمگاه: ببد is an archaic/polite past copula 'was' (classical bavad), شاه is the subject 'the king', چندی 'for a while', and بران رزمگاه 'on/at that battlefield' (بران = thereon/thereupon + رزمگاه = battlefield). The predicate 'was' appears before the subject, a frequent poetic inversion.
segments.s662-g348: چوآسوده شد شهریار و سپاه: چوآسوده is a fused idiom meaning 'when (being) at ease/relieved', شد is the past 'became' or 'was made', and شهریار و سپاه are the subjects 'the lord (king) and the army'. The clause thus reads 'when the king and the army became at ease'; note the verb شد precedes the coordinated subject, typical in older poetic Persian.
segments.s662-g349: ز لشکر یکی مرد بگزید گرد: ز (poetic 'from') marks origin ('from the army'), یکی مرد 'a certain man' (indefinite), بگزید is the past verb 'he chose/selected', and گرد here is an infinitival/participial or verbal adjunct meaning 'to go about / go round' — so the phrase means he 'chose one man to go about' or 'one man from the army he selected for the task'.
segments.s662-g350: که داند شمار نبشت و سترد: که introduces a relative clause 'who', داند is present 'knows' or 'is able', شمار 'count/tally', and the two bare past-stem verbs نبشت و سترد follow as verbal complements meaning 'to write down' and 'to hide/conceal' respectively. In this construction داند governs the following verbs as abilities/skills ('who knows how to count, to write down the tally and to conceal it').
segments.s662-g351: سپاهی بدو داد تا باژ روم: سپاهی here means 'a force/troops' (object), بدو 'to him', داد is the past 'gave'. تا introduces a purpose clause ('so that / in order that'), and باژ روم is an object phrase meaning 'the tribute/levy of Rome' (باژ روم = tribute from Rome/Byzantium); the target verb of the purpose clause (understood next) is 'ستاند' — so 'he gave him troops so that he might exact Rome's tribute.'
segments.s662-g352: ستاند سپارد به آباد بوم: ستاند is a past/stative root meaning 'to exact/take' (here 'to exact [tribute]'); سپارد is the past 'he entrusted/sent' (from سپردن) and governs the following prepositional phrase به آباد بوم 'to the prosperous land/settled region' (به = to, آباد = inhabited/prosperous, بوم = land/homeland). The sequence gives two actions: he exacted (tribute) and then sent/entrusted him to the prosperous region.
segments.s662-g57: فرستاده شاه ایران is a genitive noun chain: “the messenger (فرستاده) of the Shah (شاه) of Iran (ایران)”; Persian puts the possessed noun before the possessor(s). رسید is a simple past/punctual verb ‘arrived/reached’ placed at the end as the sentence’s main verb.
segments.s662-g58: بگوید here functions as a subordinate verb meaning ‘to say / to tell’ used with a purposive or narrative sense (in classical poetry it can appear where English uses ‘came to say’); ز بازار ما ‚from/ concerning our market‘ names the source/topic, هرچ ‘whatever’ quantifies the following verb, and دید is the past ‘he saw’. Word order: prepositional phrase + possessor (ما) follows the noun.
segments.s662-g59: سخن گفتن is a fixed verbal phrase meaning ‘to speak (about)’. از introduces the topic ‘of/ about’ (از اندوه و شادی = ‘about sorrow and joy’). هرچ گفت puts the quantifier ‘whatever’ before the past verb گفت ‘he said’ to mean ‘whatever he said’. گفت is simple past narration.
segments.s662-g60: This is an impersonal/modal negative construction: نباید (‘ought not / should not’) + نهفت (literally ‘hidden’). غم و شادمانی is the coordinated noun phrase (‘sorrow and rejoicing’). The result reads ‘sorrow and joy should not be hidden’ — نهفت functions as a participial/adjectival complement rather than an independent present tense verb.
segments.s662-g61: بشد and شد are past/preterite forms meaning ‘it happened / became’ or ‘he became’. The phrase reads as two coordinated clauses: بشد قیصر (‘Caesar arose / there became a Caesar’) و تازه شد قیصری (‘and a new/renewed Caesar came into being’). تازه is an adverb/adjective ‘new/fresh’ modifying قیصری; word order places the verb before the noun in the second clause (poetic inversion).
segments.s662-g62: که introduces a relative clause modifying قیصری: ‘who…’. سر بر فرازد is a classical periphrasis meaning ‘raises his head’ or ‘lifts himself up’; فرازد is a third-person verb meaning ‘he lifts / exalts’. ز هرمهتری is a comparative/ablative phrase ‘above/from every great one’ (ز in poetry often marks the comparison source).
segments.s662-g63: ندارد is the negated present/past auxiliary ‘does not have / had not’ used impersonally here. ز شاهان ‘from/among the kings’ is a genitive/partitive phrase, کسی را is the object with the definite/object marker را, and بکس is a substantive meaning ‘a single/ solitary person’ used as the object complement. The clause literally states that ‘among the kings he has no solitary person’ (i.e. there is no solitary one among the kings).
segments.s662-g64: چه here functions as a conjunction meaning ‘for/since’ (a classical linking particle). کهتر is the comparative/adjective ‘lesser/inferior’ and بود is the past copula ‘was’. شاه is the noun ‘king’ and فریادرس is a compound noun ‘rescuer / deliverer’ used as a predicate apposition: ‘for the king, being lesser, was a rescuer.’
segments.s662-g441: Two third-person jussive/present verbs in sequence (ببخشد، بپرهیزد) state desirable actions: 'ببخشد' is a third-person verb meaning 'he gives/bestows' (here an exhortation or character description), followed by 'بپرهیزد' ('he refrains/avoids'). The prepositional phrase 'از مهر گنج' ('from the love of treasure') is the object of بپرهیزد and uses the preposition از before the noun phrase.
segments.s662-g442: 'نبندد' is third-person present/jussive negative meaning 'he does not (or should not) close/shut'; the direct object دل ('heart') precedes the locative phrase 'اندر سرای سپنج' ('in/within the house/household of سپنج'). اندر is the archaic/pre-classical preposition 'in'. Note: سرای here functions as a locative 'house, dwelling' (poetic use); the image is metaphorical—do not lock the heart away.
segments.s662-g443: سپاسم is a first‑person expression of thanks ('my thanks' / 'I give thanks'), followed by the poetic preposition ز ('to/from') and یزدان ('God'). The subordinating particle که introduces the following clause; فرزند is the subject and هست is the copula ('is').
segments.s662-g444: A simple coordinate predicate: three adjectives joined by و. Each adjective (خردمند, دانا) attributes qualities to the preceding subject; ایزد پرست is a compound meaning 'one who worships God' (ایزد = deity + پرست as the worshipper suffix), giving the final quality.
segments.s662-g445: وز ایشان is a poetic locution meaning 'from/of them' (وز here = از). بهرمزد functions as a phrase relating to receiving reward or merit ('deserving of reward' / 'for reward'). The final element ترم is an archaic/poetic comparative (تر + ـم) 'I am more...' — the clause as a whole expresses a comparative claim relative to those people. Several items in this segment are archaic or textualized forms whose precise sense is literary and not fully captured by the supplied sense-list entries.
segments.s662-g446: برای 'for' or 'because of' begins a coordinated clause; و links the two comparatives. بهوشش literally 'by his sense/awareness' (bɛ-hosh + possessive), used instrumentally ('by/through his wit'); فرازان is an adjective meaning 'exalted/lofty' (here applied to intellect), and ترم again marks the comparative 'I am more...'. Overall: 'and by his understanding I am loftier.'
segments.s662-g447: A nominal list introduced by the poetic preposition ز ('from/because of'): three abstract virtues joined by و. بخشایش here is used in the sense of generous giving, بخشش in the sense of giving/grant or forgiveness (a related but distinct nuance), and راستی means 'uprightness/honesty'.
segments.s662-g448: نبینم is first‑person present negative 'I do not see'; همی is an emphatic/continuous particle ('ever/always/indeed'); در دلش is the locative phrase 'in his heart' where ش is the third‑person possessive enclitic 'his'; کاستی is the noun 'deficiency/shortcoming'. The whole clause states the speaker's observation that no deficiency exists in that heart.
segments.s662-g97: گماند is a past-tense/perfective verb meaning 'he thought' or 'it seemed.' که introduces the content clause (that ...). ما را uses را as the definite/object marker attached to ما ('us' / 'our'), and همو is an emphatic demonstrative 'that same one.' دوست نیست is the negative copula 'is not a friend'; the clause as a whole reports his thought: 'He thought that that very one is not our friend.'
segments.s662-g98: اگر چند here serves a concessive/contrastive function ('although' / 'even though'), introducing a clause that qualifies the previous judgment. او را again uses را as object/topic marking for 'he.' پی و پوست is a fixed pair meaning 'flesh and skin' (literally 'pi and skin'); پوست is 'skin' and پی here refers to flesh/tissue. نیست negates the clause. The whole segment means 'although he has no flesh and skin' (i.e. lacks substance/attachment).
segments.s662-g99: کنون نیز places the time adverbially ('now, likewise'); یک تن is an idiom meaning 'one person' (تن used as 'person' here). ز + رومی نژاد is a classical poetic genitive/partitive: 'of Roman lineage' (ز meaning 'of/from' in poetic register). The segment sets up 'not even one person of Roman descent remains.'
segments.s662-g100: نمانم is the negative form of ماندن used impersonally/with existential sense 'does not remain / is not left.' که introduces a subordinate clause ('who would be...'). باشد is the present/subjunctive form 'would be / might be.' ازان تخت شاد means 'happy on that throne' with ازان = 'of/that' (poetic), تخت = 'throne,' and شاد = 'happy.' The clause as a whole: 'who would be happy on that throne.'
segments.s662-g101: همی is a frequentative/adverb meaning 'continually / always' in older Persian. سر فرازد is a present/frequentive poetic form meaning 'he lifts (up) his head' (سر = head; فرازد = raises). که introduces reported speech or the sense behind the gesture, here followed by من قیصرم 'I am Caesar.' The clause ties the physical gesture to the boastful content.
segments.s662-g102: گر is the conditional 'if' (sometimes 'though'), and از here means 'of / among' in the partitive sense. نامداران = 'the renowned' (nominative/agentive plural). یکی functions as 'one (of them),' and مهترم is 'I am the chief/foremost' (مهتر + first-person suffix). The whole conditional clause reads 'If I am one of the renowned, I am their chief.'
segments.s662-g103: کنم here functions as a verbal auxiliary/intentional marker 'I will / I shall' combined with the subsequent شوم ('I become') to express a future/volitional meaning. زین سپس literally 'from this afterwards' (zīn sometimes written as 'ز این' meaning 'from this') marks 'from now on.' روم را with را marks the domain/target (in classical usage 'to/among Rome'). نام شوم is a causative/expectational idiom 'I shall become (of) name' = 'I will become famous.' Note: some classical words (زین) appear in a poetic register.
segments.s662-g104: برانگیزم is a first-person present-future form 'I will arouse/raise' (causative), taking آتش 'fire' as object. ز is the poetic preposition 'from,' آباد qualifies بوم: آباد بوم = 'inhabited/Prosperous homeland' or 'the settled land.' Together the clause means 'I will kindle fire from (across) the prosperous homeland' — i.e., set the land ablaze.
segments.s662-g617: همه پرسش این بود و پاسخ همین reads as a compressed, parallel statement: همه پرسش این بود = “the whole/question in its entirety was this,” with بود as the past copula. پاسخ همین is elliptical for «(پاسخ) همین (بود)», reusing the copula. و simply connects the two balanced noun phrases.
segments.s662-g618: که introduces a content clause (“that …”). برشاه باد uses باد as an optative particle (’may it be’), with برشاه = ‘upon/for the king’ (بهِ شاه), and از جهان‌آفرین gives the source/agent ‘from/by the Creator of the World’ (poetic ordering: ‘may this be upon the king from the world‑creator’).
segments.s662-g619: زبانها (plural subject) precedes a verb phrase with بفرمانش = بهِ فرمانِ او (‘by/at his command’). گوینده is a present participial/nominal predicate ‘speaking’ and باد is the optative particle ‘may’ — together: ‘May tongues be speaking at his command.’ The verb/optative follows the noun phrase as is common in poetry.
segments.s662-g620: دل راد او places adjective and possessor after the noun (دلِ رادِ او = ‘his generous heart’). شاد is the predicative adjective ‘glad/joyful’ and و links the next phrase. جوینده باد repeats the optative (باد) with a nominal subject (the seeker); the second clause omits a repeated adjective (شاد) by ellipsis, so both clauses are governed by the final باد (‘may … be joyful’).
segments.s662-g621: شهنشاه کسری is title + name in apposition (‘the emperor Khosrow’). ازو = از او indicates the object/target of astonishment (‘at him’), and خیره ماند is a compound of adjective + ماند (remain/stand) meaning ‘remained astonished’ — a typical poetic ordering where the predicative adjective precedes the verb ماند.
segments.s662-g622: بسی here intensifies آفرین but the supplied sense list doesn’t include the ‘many/much’ sense (so marked -1). آفرین کیانی = ‘Kayanian acclamations/praises’ and بخواند is a past narrative verb meaning ‘he uttered/voiced’ (here ‘he gave/raised’ acclamations). The subject is the same king just mentioned (omitted but understood).
segments.s662-g623: ز گفتار او uses the poetic preposition ز (از) = ‘from/because of’ + گفتار او ‘his speech/words’. انجمن شاد شد is a normal copular clause: انجمن (the assembly) + شاد (joyful) + شد (became/was), so ‘the assembly became joyful at his words.’
segments.s662-g624: دل شهریار places the possessor after the noun (‘the heart of the king’). از غم = ‘from sorrow’ and آزاد شد = ‘became free / was freed’ (آزاد as adjective + شد as copula/become), so the clause means ‘the king’s heart was freed from sorrow.’
segments.s662-g529: گرانمایه هرمزد is a noun phrase with an adjective-like honorific before the name (literally “esteemed Ahura Mazda”). برپای is an adverbial/locative phrase “to his feet / standing,” and خاست is the classical past tense of خاستن (“he rose / stood up”). Word order is subject/nominal phrase + locative + verb, typical of Persian narrative style.
segments.s662-g530: یکی آفرین کرد is object-before-verb: یکی آفرین (“a single acclamation/one ‘bravo’”) is the direct object and کرد is the past-tense verb “made/did” (i.e. ‘uttered’). بر شاه راست is a prepositional phrase “for/to the true king,” with بر marking the beneficiary/target and راست as the adjective “true/rightful.”
segments.s662-g531: که here introduces the content of the wish/phrase (“that …”). از شاه marks absence with از + noun = “of/from a king,” گیتی is the subject “the world,” مبادا is the negative optative particle meaning “may it not be / lest,” and تهی is the adjective “empty / devoid.” The clause therefore expresses a negative wish: “may the world not be empty of a king.”
segments.s662-g532: همی‌باد is an archaic optative formula meaning “may it be / may there be.” بر تخت is the locative “upon the throne,” and شاهنشهی is a substantival/ adjectival compound referring to the imperial/kingship sphere (“imperial” or “of kingship”), so تخت شاهنشهی = “the imperial throne.” Together this continues the wish: “may there be (a king) on the imperial throne.”
segments.s662-g533: مبادا is the negative optative/precautionary particle “may it not be / let it not happen.” که introduces the content clause “that ….” بی‌تو is the prepositional phrase “without you,” and ببینیم is first-person-plural present/subjunctive (‘we see / may we see’ here used in the optative sense). تاج is the noun “crown”; together the clause reads “may it not be that we see a crown without you.”
segments.s662-g534: گر … وگر is a paired concessive/conditional “if/though … or if …” used here to list alternatives (“whether … or …”). آیین شاهی is a compound meaning “the ceremony/ritual of kingship” (آیین = ritual/custom, شاهی = royal/kingship), while تخت عاج is a noun + modifier meaning “an ivory throne” (عاج = ivory). The line offers two possible forms of kingship (ceremony or ivory throne) that should not be seen without the addressee.
segments.s662-g535: به here marks manner/means (“by/with/as”), پوزش is a noun meaning “apology / pardon,” and جهان is the subject “the world.” پیش تو is a locative phrase “in your presence / before you,” خاک is the noun “dust,” and باد is the optative particle “may it be.” The whole phrase is a hyperbolic wish: “as apology, may the world be dust before you.”
segments.s662-g536: گزند تو را places گزند (“harm / injury”) as the topic and تو را marks the person affected (“to you / for you”). چرخ is a poetic word for the sky/fate, تریاک is a metaphorical noun “opium,” and باد is again the optative “may it be.” The clause therefore prays: “may the heavens be opium (i.e. a narcotic/neutralizer) against any harm to you.”
segments.s662-g633: This is a double protasis using repeated conditional 'اگر' ('if') to stack conditions: 'اگر تاج داری' = 'if you have/wear a crown' (داری is the present second-person singular of داشتن, 'to have'). The second clause 'اگر گرم و رنج' continues the conditions: گرم here is adjectival 'ardent/zealous' and رنج is a noun 'suffering/hardship' linked by و 'and'. The implied subject of both clauses is 'you', carried into the result clause that follows.
segments.s662-g634: بگذری is a present/subjunctive (second person) form meaning 'you pass / you will pass' used as the apodosis (result) of the preceding 'اگر' clauses; in Persian poetry the present-subjunctive often expresses a future or potential result. همان functions as a demonstrative 'that very (thing)' or 'then,' and زین here is the archaic poetic contraction meaning 'از این' ('from this'). سرای is the noun 'house/abode' (here 'this life/house'), and سپنج is a poetic/obscure noun attached as the object or epithet of سرای; the sense of سپنج is archaic/uncertain in this context.
segments.s662-g635: بپیوستم is a first-person past/perfect form 'I joined / I united' (with the poetic prefix b-). این عهد is the direct object 'this pledge/oath'; نوشین is an adjective 'sweet/delightful' modifying عهد, and روان here describes manner as 'flowing/pleasant (to the ear/spirit)', forming the compound adjective 'نوشینِ روان' ('sweet-flowing') that qualifies the pledge.
segments.s662-g636: به introduces the beneficiary/goal 'to/for the victory'; پیروزی is 'victory', شهریار is 'king' (here the addressee or celebrated ruler), and جوان is the adjective 'young' modifying شهریار. The whole prepositional phrase marks what the pledge is joined to: the young king's victory.
segments.s662-g637: یکی is an indefinite 'one' (a single/one of), نامه here means 'book/volume' (the 'book of kings' tradition), and شهریاران is the genitive plural 'of the kings.' بخوان is an imperative or hortatory present 'read!' (second-person singular) instructing the addressee to read one such book/letter.
segments.s662-g638: نگر is an imperative 'look!/consider!' directing attention; تاکه is a connective 'so that' (introducing purpose/result), باشد is the present/subjunctive third-person 'may it be' or 'it should be,' and چو is the simile 'as/like.' نوشین روان repeats the compound adjective 'sweet-flowing' (nourishing the idea that what you read should be pleasant and fluent). Grammatically, this is a purpose/result clause (so that it will be...), commenting on the preceding imperative.
segments.s662-g639: This segment lists spheres of action with repeated conjunctions: برای (here 'in matters of' or 'in/for'), و links items, and the nouns that follow enumerate qualities/activities (traditionally generosity, justice, feasting, and war). Many of these words are given in a condensed/poetic form; they function as parallel objects/complements describing the man's conduct.
segments.s662-g640: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; روزش is 'his day' (روز + possessive -ش), سرآمد is 'its end/conclusion' ('when his day ended'), نبودش is a negative past/copular construction meaning 'he had no / there was not for him' (here 'he did not have any delay'), and درنگ is the noun 'delay/loitering.' Together they form a clause meaning 'when his day ended, he did not tarry/delay.'
segments.s662-g505: گرانمایه‌تر is the comparative of گرانمایه ('precious/esteemed') formed with the -تر suffix meaning 'more precious'. کیست is the interrogative 'who is' (کی + ست, with ست = copula) — archaic inverted word order places the question word before the copula. از here is the comparative preposition 'than' (literally 'from'), and دوستان is the plural noun 'friends'.
segments.s662-g506: کز is an archaic relative/conjunctive form (from که + از) introducing a relative clause and here functions like 'whose'/'that from'. آواز او places the possessor after the noun (poetic order): 'his voice'. دل شود بوستان is verb-final: دل (object 'the heart') + شود ('becomes') + بوستان ('a garden'), so literally 'the heart becomes a garden' with شود as present/passive 'become'.
segments.s662-g507: کرا is an archaic relative/interrogative 'who' (used instead of modern چه/کی). بیشتری (here بیشتر) is the comparative 'more'/'greater', دوست is 'friend', and اندر جهان is a locative phrase 'in the world' (اندر = in/within; جهان = world). The sequence follows older Persian word order with the locative after the noun.
segments.s662-g508: که introduces a relative clause ('who/that'). یابد is a present 3rd-person form of یافتن 'to find/attain' meaning 'obtains' or 'gains' here. بدو ('to him') is the indirect object pronoun. آشکار ونهان pairs two antonyms (آشکار 'open/manifest' and نَهان 'hidden') joined by the coordinating element (the manuscript form shows them together), so the clause means 'who obtains for him the manifest and the hidden.'
segments.s662-g509: همان نیز is a demonstrative phrase 'that (same) also' or 'likewise'; the order places the demonstrative before نیز ('also'). دشمن is the noun 'enemy'. کرا is again the archaic relative 'who' introducing the clause that follows. بیشتر is the comparative 'more/more so', modifying the idea of enmity.
segments.s662-g510: که begins the relative clause ('who'). باشد is a subjunctive/optative or present copular form here meaning 'is' (archaic usage). برو بر functions as an older locative/ directional combination meaning roughly 'toward/against' (برو here appears as part of the compound expression with بر), and بداندیش‌تر is the comparative adjective 'more ill‑minded/ill‑disposed'. Together the clause says 'who is (even) more ill‑disposed toward him.'
segments.s662-g511: سزاوار آرام بودن is an infinitival nominal phrase: سزاوار ('worthy/appropriate') + آرام بودن ('to be at rest/at ease'). کجاست is the interrogative 'where is it' (کجا + ست), so the full phrase asks rhetorically 'Where is it appropriate to be at ease?'
segments.s662-g512: که introduces the explanatory clause 'since/that'. دارد is the present tense 'has/possesses'. جهاندار ('ruler, sovereign') is the subject possessing the backing. ازو is the old-style prepositional pronoun 'from him/of him' (از + او). پشت راست is a noun phrase meaning 'a firm/right backing/support' (پشت = support/protection; راست = true/firm), so the clause says the sovereign 'has his firm support from him.'
segments.s662-g25: ز قیصر (z + proper name) is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from the Caesar'; تو را uses the enclitic را after the pronoun to mark the beneficiary/object 'to you'. مزد بسیار is the noun phrase 'much/abundant reward' with adjective بسیار after the noun (poetic word order). باد is the optative particle 'may (it be)', placed at the end to make the whole phrase a blessing.
segments.s662-g26: مسیحا روان is a genitival/nominal phrase 'the spirit (روان) of the Messiah (مسیحا)' with the proper name preceding the noun in this poetic line. تو را again marks the beneficiary 'to you'; یار is the predicative noun 'companion/friend', and باد is the same optative particle 'may it be' — together giving the formulaic blessing 'may X be your companion'.
segments.s662-g27: شنیدم که is the reporting clause 'I heard that…' where که introduces the subordinate reported clause. بر نامور تخت اوی is a locative/possessive phrase: بر + noun 'on' + adjective نامور 'renowned' preceding تخت 'throne', and اوی is the third‑person possessive 'his' attached after the noun (classical/poetic placement). The whole phrase sets the place/possessor for the following verbs.
segments.s662-g28: نشستی and بیاراستی are past tense verbs describing what occurred in the reported clause: (you/he) 'sat' (نشستی) and 'adorned/made splendid' (بیاراستی); in classical poetic Persian the -ی ending can mark second‑person or be an archaic 3rd‑person form depending on context. بخت اوی is the object 'his fortune/destiny' with اوی as the possessive pronoun; together the verbs apply to that throne/fortune established in the previous phrase.
segments.s662-g29: ز ما 'from us' marks the source/provider. هرچ باید is a nominalized clause 'whatever is needed' (هرچ 'whatever' + باید 'is necessary'); ز نیرو is a prepositional phrase 'from (our) power/force', and بخواه is the imperative/subjunctive 'ask' (verb placed at the end, as is common). The verb بَخواه governs the following coordinated list of resources.
segments.s662-g30: This is a coordinated list of what may be requested: ز اسب و سلیح و ز گنج و سپاه uses repeated preposition ز ('from') and و ('and') to enumerate items. Each noun is a direct object of the implied verb بخواه in the previous segment; the ordering and repetition are typical of poetic parallelism.
segments.s662-g31: فرستاده 'the envoy/sent one' is the subject; از پیش کسری is the ablative/locative phrase 'from the presence of Khosrow' (از پیش = from the presence/of before, کسری = Khosrow). برفت is a past motion verb 'he went away/departed' (here a prefixed past form); the clause describes the envoy's departure from Khosrow's court.
segments.s662-g32: به نزدیک قیصر is a directional phrase 'towards/near the Caesar'. خرامید is the past tense 'he strode/proudly advanced' (verbs placed after the directional phrase in poetic narrative). The final word تفت is unattested in the provided sense list for this usage and appears to be an archaic or onomatopoetic intensifier; its exact sense is unclear here but it functions as a final emphatic particle or variant form, so its sense is marked unknown.
segments.s662-g249: هنر (noun) is the theme 'skill/art' placed up front for emphasis; باید is a modal auxiliary meaning 'ought/must' and governs the following source phrase. از introduces the source ('from'), and مرد موزه‌فروش is a noun phrase 'a man who sells موزه' — here موزه‌فروش functions as a compound 'shoe-/slipper-seller' (seller as a postnominal modifier). The word order is Topic + modal + source phrase.
segments.s662-g250: بدین is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in/with this' (بـ + این); کار دیگر = 'another matter/this other affair'. تو (you) is the subject of the verb مَکوش, which is a negative imperative/prohibitive form (مَ + کوش) meaning 'do not attempt/strive'. با من 'with/against me' marks the opponent/target ('do not contend with me about this').
segments.s662-g251: بدست (بِدست) is a prepositional phrase 'in / by the hand(s) of' introducing the agents. خردمند 'the wise' and مرد نژاد 'a man of noble lineage' are coordinated (و = 'and'); نژاد is used attributively 'of lineage/stock' forming 'nobly born man'. Word order is Preposition + coordinated noun phrases.
segments.s662-g252: نماند is the verbal phrase 'there remained / was left' (here in the idiom نماند به جز ... meaning 'nothing remained except ...'). به جز = 'except'. حسرت 'regret/yearning' is the main remaining noun; وسرد 'and cold' links another nominal quality, and باد at the end is the optative particle 'باد' ('may it be'), a poetic wish/marker rather than the literal 'wind'.
segments.s662-g253: شود is the present/factual auxiliary 'becomes / happens to be'. پیش او = 'in his presence / before him'. خوار is a predicate adjective 'lowly/despised', and مردم‌شناس is a compound noun 'one who knows/understands the people' (a shrewd observer of people). The clause means 'the people-knower is humbled/despised in his presence.'
segments.s662-g254: چوپاسخ (چو پاسخ) is a temporal clause 'when he answers'; دهد is the verb 'gives' (here 'gives an answer'). ز = poetic contraction of از 'from' (indicating source/object: 'from him'). پذیرد سپاس = 'accepts (پذیرد) thanks (سپاس)'. The sequence describes: 'when he replies, he takes/accepts thanks from him.'
segments.s662-g255: بما بر = 'upon us' (بما 'to/for us' + بر 'upon'). پس از مرگ = 'after death' (پس 'after' + از 'of/from' used in the temporal phrase). نفرین بود = 'there was a curse' (نفرین 'curse' + بود past copula 'was'). The phrase states that a curse was laid upon them after death.
segments.s662-g256: چوآیین is a fused poetic expression 'چو آئین' meaning 'according to custom / as the practice'. این روزگار 'this age/these times' is the topic, and این بود 'this was' (بود past copula) states that such was the way of the times. The segment explains or justifies the previous clause as the custom of the age.
segments.s662-g729: همیشه (adverb) sets the time/extent 'always'. خِرْد پاسبانِ تو is a predicative noun phrase: خِرْد ('wisdom') is placed before the role پاسبانِ تو ('your guardian'); تو is the possessor following the noun. باد is the optative particle here ('may it be'), so the whole clause is a wish: 'May wisdom always be your guardian.'
segments.s662-g730: همه نیکی is a noun phrase 'all goodness/every good'. اندر is the older/prepositional form meaning 'in/within' and governs گمانِ تو ('your thought/belief'), where تو again follows the noun as possessor. باد is the optative particle ('may it be'), so the clause reads as another benediction: 'May all goodness be in your thought.'
segments.s662-g731: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. من is the first‑person subject. بگذرم is first‑person singular present/subjunctive used in a temporal clause (here: 'when I shall pass / when I pass away'). زین here functions archaically as زِ این ('from this'), governing جهان فراخ ('this wide world'); note that the provided lemma senses do not include this pronominal/construction and so are marked separately below.
segments.s662-g732: باید is the modal 'must / it is necessary'. برآورد here means 'to bring up / to raise (up)' — the verb is paired with باید (necessity + verb). یکی خوب کاخ is the direct object phrase 'one/ a single fine palace' (یکی = one, خوب = fine/beautiful, کاخ = palace). Word order (modal + verb + object) is typical of older/poetic Persian.
segments.s662-g733: بجای here means 'at / in the place' (literally 'in the place of'); کِزو is the relative/adverbial 'from which' linking back to that place. دور باشد is a predicative clause 'be distant' with باشد in the subjunctive/optative sense ('may be' / 'is [situated] far'), and گذر is the noun 'pass' (the mountain pass or route). Together the phrase describes 'a place from which the pass is far.'
segments.s662-g734: نپرد is the negative present/subjunctive or jussive of پریدن ('not fly / let it not fly'). بدو is the clitic/particle meaning 'to it / unto that place' (به + او). کرکس تیزپر is the subject phrase 'the swift‑winged vulture' (تیزپر is a compound adjective after the noun). The verb precedes the subject here (a common poetic inversion): 'May the swift vulture not fly to it.'
segments.s662-g735: دور برچرخ modifies ایوان بلند: برچرخ is a frozen poetic locution 'on the heavens / on the celestial sphere' and ایوان بلند is 'a high/lofty iwan/portico'. دری here is the word for 'gate/door' (archaic/poetic درِی), forming a compound idea 'a distant gate on the heavens'; the supplied lemma list did not include the 'gate' sense, so that particular sense is marked separately below.
segments.s662-g736: ببالا is an adverb 'upward / on high'. برآورده is a participial/pp form 'raised / brought up' (here describing the portico). چون is the comparative 'like / as', and کمند is a noun 'snare / lasso' (a common simile for coils or loops); the full phrase (چون ده کمند) means 'like ten coils of a snare' (ده 'ten' appears in the line but was not listed among the segment's word entries). Grammatically this is a simile modifying the raised portico.
segments.s662-g625: نبشتند is a past 3rd-person-plural verb ('they wrote/inscribed'). The phrase is SOV-like but here the verb precedes the object phrase for poetic rhythm: 'they wrote a covenant' (عهدی) and بفرمان شاه ('by the king's order') is an oblique prepositional phrase specifying agency/authority.
segments.s662-g626: که introduces a reported/content clause ('that ...'). هرمزد را uses را as the definite object marker; داد is a past transitive verb 'gave' with تخت و کلاه as the compound direct objects ('throne and hat/crown'). Word order is typical Persian: subject/agent implied in the clause, object marked with را, then verb.
segments.s662-g627: چو here functions as a subordinating conjunction 'when/as'. چوقرطاس رومی شد is a nominal phrase with شد (past of شدن) 'became/turned' agreeing with چوقرطاس(rag/ parchment) and modified by رومی ('Roman/Byzantine-like'). از باد خشک gives the cause/agental circumstance 'from/by the dry wind'.
segments.s662-g628: نهادند is past 3rd-plural 'they placed/put' (here a verb of affixing). مهری is the direct object but its provided sense list doesn't include 'seal' (so sense is unspecified for this lexeme in the given list). بروبر is an adverbial/qualifying word meaning 'face‑to‑face' or 'directly' and z (ز) marks the material/source 'of musk' (مشک).
segments.s662-g629: به + noun (موبد) marks the indirect object/recipient 'to the mobad'. سپردند is past 3rd‑person‑plural 'they entrusted/handed over'. پیش here means 'in the presence of' (third sense in the list) and introduces those before whom the act was done. ردان's provided senses do not clearly match a role here (so no exact sense given in the list).
segments.s662-g630: This is a nominal apposition enumerating witnesses/participants: بزرگان 'the great ones' + و 'and' + بیدار دل 'awake/alert‑hearted' modifying بخردان (the wise). بخردان is the plural/nominal form meaning 'the wise' (sense 0 in the list). Word order is noun phrases linked by و.
segments.s662-g631: جهان را نمایش uses را as an object marker giving 'the world's display/appearance' (نمایش). چو is the comparative/conjunctive 'as/like', linking نمایش to کردار ('deed, conduct'), and نیست is the negated copula 'is not'. The clause says the show/appearance of the world is not the same as its deeds.
segments.s662-g632: نهانش ('its hidden/secret part') is the topic; جز ... نیست is an exclusory construction 'is nothing except ...'. از marks source/reason 'from', and رنج و تیمار are the two nouns giving the content of that exception: 'suffering and care/tending'. The final نیست negates existence except for those things.
segments.s662-g553: هران کس is an archaic contraction of 'هر آن کس' meaning 'whoever' or 'anyone who'. که introduces a relative clause ('who/that') and نیکی فرامش کند is the verb phrase: فرامش (noun-form 'forgetfulness' used with کردن) plus کند (3rd-person singular present/factual marker 'does/forgets'). Word order is subject ('هران کس') + relative clause.
segments.s662-g554: خرد را بکوشد uses را as the object marker attached to خرد ('wisdom' as a noun receiving action). بکوشد (بـ + کوشد) is a 3rd-person verbal form meaning 'strives/attacks' (classical present). که introduces the purpose/result clause; بیهش is an archaic fused form meaning 'to/for him' and کند is the verb 'does/makes' completing the clause.
segments.s662-g555: دگر گفت is an idiom meaning 'another said' or 'another (view) is expressed' (دگر = 'other/another'). ازآرام ('from calm/peace') modifies راه گریز ('the path of flight/escape'); Persian places these modifiers before the head noun: ازآرام راه گریز = 'the escape route from calm/peace'.
segments.s662-g556: گرفتن is the infinitive/masdar 'to take' used here as a verbal noun ('taking the path'). کجا is an interrogative/exclamatory 'where/when' introducing the rhetorical question; خوبتر is comparative 'better' and از marks the standard of comparison 'than' before ستیز ('strife/fighting'). The phrase reads as a rhetorical comparison: 'Is taking flight better than fighting?'
segments.s662-g557: به شهری uses the preposition به to introduce a locative/indefinite noun phrase ('in/to a city'); که begins a relative clause modifying شهری. بیداد شد is a past verbal phrase 'injustice occurred/ran rampant' (شد here = 'happened/became'), and پادشا ('king') appears as the topical noun tied to that clause—classical word order permits this sequencing where the relative clause and title appear together.
segments.s662-g558: ندارد is the negative present of داشتن used here idiomatically with روا to mean 'it is not fitting/appropriate'; خردمند بودن is the masdar + بودن ('to be wise' / 'being wise'). The whole predicate states that 'being wise' is not permissible/appropriate in the described situation.
segments.s662-g559: ز is the poetic variant of از meaning 'from'; بیدادگر شاه is a genitival/attributive phrase 'the tyrant king'. باید is the modal 'must/ought to' and گریز is a noun 'flight/escape' — together: 'one must take flight from a tyrant king.'
segments.s662-g560: کزن is a classical fused relative 'from whom / from which'; خیزد is a 3rd-person present/future-like verb 'arises/comes forth'. اندر جهان is the locative 'in the world' and رستخیز (a compound) means 'resurrection/uprising' — together: 'from whom an uprising will arise in the world.'
segments.s662-g521: چه چیزیست is a classical word-order inversion for 'what thing is' (چه + چیزی + ست, where ست = است, the copula). کان here is an archaic relative/conjunctive element linking to the following clause (not enumerated in the provided senses), and ننگ پیش آورد is a verb phrase where ننگ is the object 'shame' and پیش آورد (پیش = 'in the presence of/before'; آورد = past-form used generically) means 'brings/causes to appear before (others)'. The past form آورد is used in classical diction to state a general or habitual action.
segments.s662-g522: همان بد begins with همان 'that very/the same' modifying بد 'evil/disgrace'. ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/because of', گفتار 'speech/words' is the source, خویش + ش is a possessive construction 'his own' (خویش = own; ش = his), and آورد again is the past-form used to mean 'brought/caused'. The clause states that the disgrace originated in his own words.
segments.s662-g523: بیک روز تا شب is a temporal phrase 'from one morning until night' (بیک here is an archaic/poetic element meaning 'one/at one', not the chieftain title), with تا 'until'. برآمد ز کوه places the verb after the time phrase: برآمد (past 'arose/issued forth') + ز 'from' + کوه 'mountain' — 'he came forth from the mountain.'
segments.s662-g524: ز گفتار دانا is 'from the speech of the wise' (ز = from, گفتار = speech, دانا = wise). نیامد ستوه uses نیامد (literally 'did not come') with ستوه 'weary' — a classical idiom meaning 'did not become/was not made weary', i.e. 'he did not grow tired.' The negative past form gives a general descriptive past.
segments.s662-g525: چو هنگام شمع آمد از تیرگی is a temporal clause: چو 'when', هنگام شمع 'the time/hour of the candle' (چو + هنگام = 'when the time came'), آمد 'came' (past used to mark occurrence), از تیرگی 'out of the darkness' (از = out of/from, تیرگی = darkness). The clause sets the scene: as the candle's hour arrived and light came from the gloom.
segments.s662-g526: سرمهتران is a plural agentive/collective 'those with/applying kohl' (literally 'kohl-people'); تیره is an adjective 'dark/gloomy/turned dark' describing their appearance or mood; از here = 'from' and خیرگی means 'astonishment/stupefaction' (so تیره از خیرگی = 'dark/enveloped by astonishment'). The noun order is poetic: the group is named, then their changed state is given.
segments.s662-g527: ز گفتار ایشان 'from their words' (ز = from, گفتار = speech, ایشان = their). غمی گشت شاه places the predicate before the subject in poetic order: غمی گشت 'a grief became/befell' and شاه 'the king' is the one affected. گشت is the past of گشتن 'to become/befall' used here to mean 'befell/wasfalling upon.'
segments.s662-g528: همی‌کرد is the classical progressive past 'was doing/would do' marking continuous action; خامش 'silent' (used as predicate/noun 'silence' or 'silent') describes his state. بپاسخ is the prefixed prepositional/infinitival form meaning 'for/to answer' and نگاه 'gaze' completes the phrase — together 'he remained silent, gazing as if to answer' (ب- prefixed forms often indicate purpose or direction).
segments.s662-g385: چه here is a rhetorical interrogative 'what/who' introducing a clause (literary use). آنکس که = 'that person who' (آنکس = 'that person, he who'; که = relative 'who/that'), and گوید is the literary present 'he says' (گوید is the present form often used in poetry rather than spoken گفت/می‌گوید). خرامست is built from خرامیدن + the archaic copula -ست (short form of است) = 'he struts/strides proudly', and وناز is 'و + ناز' appended as a noun phrase 'and coquettishness/air' completing the predicate.
segments.s662-g386: چه again introduces a rhetorical question 'what (then)'; گوید repeats the literary present 'he says' (sense is 'say', not the noun senses listed); که is the subordinating/relative 'that'; دردست is a locative/possessive compound 'در + دست' meaning 'at hand / in (his) possession'; the coordinating و links رنج and نیاز ('suffering' and 'need'), giving the sense 'pain, hardship, and need are at hand'.
segments.s662-g387: کسی را ندیدم = 'I have seen no one' with را as the definite/object marker on کسی and ندیدم the past perfect/negative 'I did not see'; بمرگ آرزوی is a short infinitival/oblique phrase 'a wish for death' (ب- = prepositional prefix 'to/for' + مرگ = 'death'; آرزوی = 'wish'), so the whole phrase means 'I have seen no one who desires death.'
segments.s662-g388: نه بی‌راه is a parenthetical negation 'not (without) reason / not unwarranted' (بی‌راه = 'wrong, unwarranted'); the coordinating و links that remark to the following prepositional phrase از مردم نیکخوی = 'from/among well‑bred (good‑natured) people' (از = 'from/among', مردم = 'people', نیکخوی = 'well‑bred, kindly').
segments.s662-g389: چه ... چه is the correlative 'whether ... or ...' (here introducing alternatives). دینی = 'religious/believer'; اهریمن = 'Ahriman' or figuratively 'demon'; بت‌پرست = 'idolater' — the sequence lists different types of people without affecting grammar (all are subjects of the following verb).
segments.s662-g390: ز is the poetic form of از ('from'/'because of') used here before the verb; مرگند is present‑plural 'they die' (مرگ‑ + ـند), so 'ز مرگند' functions as 'they are of death / they die.' بر سر نهاده دو دست is a verbal phrase with the perfect participle نهاده 'placed' taking the prepositional phrase بر سر 'upon (their) head' and the agent/subject marker 'دو دست' ('two hands' = both hands) preceding the predicate in typical Persian order: 'they have placed both hands upon their heads.'
segments.s662-g391: چوسالَت شد = 'when years (your years/age) became/passed' with چو (archaic/poetic چو = 'when') + سالت (your years/age) + شد 'became/was'; ای پیر is a direct address 'O old man/elder'; برشست is the past 'sat down' (بر + شَست / نشست in literary form), so the clause reads 'When years passed, O elder, he sat down.'
segments.s662-g392: می‌و جام = 'wine and cup' (می = wine, joined by و to جام 'cup'); و‌آرام شد is 'and became calm/quiet' (و as coordinator + آرام شد 'became calm'); بی‌نمک is an adjective 'tasteless / dull' describing the wine/cup. Note: می‌و in the provided senses list does not include 'wine', so the lexical sense used here is not in the supplied list.
segments.s662-g657: هرآنگه که is a classical subordinating phrase meaning 'whenever/whatever (way)'. باشی is the present-subjunctive/optative form (2nd person singular) used here like an exhortation or general conditional 'be'. بدو is the clitic pronoun 'to him' placed before the adjective; شادتر is the comparative adjective ('more cheerful'), so the clause literally orders/urges 'in whatever way you are, be more cheerful to him.'
segments.s662-g658: ز introduces the source 'from' (poetic variant of از). رنج زمانه is a genitive phrase 'the suffering of the time/the world' with زمانه as 'the age/world'. دل آزادتر places the noun 'heart' before the comparative adjective 'freer' (آزادتر) — a common poetic order meaning 'a heart more free (from ...)'.
segments.s662-g659: همه شادمانی is a noun phrase 'all joy' (or 'all the joy'). بمانی is the present-subjunctive/optative (2nd person singular) used here as a wish or jussive 'may ... remain' — 'may all joy remain'. به جای is a fixed phrase 'in its place' or 'in place'.
segments.s662-g660: بباید is the impersonal modal 'it must' / 'one must'. شدن is the verbal infinitive 'to become' / 'to be' used here in an impersonal construction implying necessity of departing or ceasing to be in the dwelling. زین before a noun is an archaic classical form (here functioning like 'from this' / 'of this'); سپنجی سرای is a compound 'spongy/fragile house/dwelling' used metaphorically for the body or worldly abode. Together the clause says 'one must leave this spongy dwelling.'
segments.s662-g661: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. اندیشه رفتن is a noun phrase where رفتن is a verbal noun 'the thought of going/departing'. آمد is the past tense 'came' and فراز is an adverbial 'up/aloft' (poetic 'rose up' or 'came upon you'), so the clause means 'when the thought of departing came up/occurred.'
segments.s662-g662: برخشنده is an adjectival/epithetic form 'radiant, shining' modifying روز و شب 'day and night'. دیریاز is a compound adverb/adjective meaning 'long-standing/long-lasting'. The phrase describes 'the long, shining days and nights' — poetically presenting the span of time that now appears or is perceived.
segments.s662-g663: بجستیم is the simple past first-person plural 'we sought/sought for'. تاج کیی را is the definite object 'the Kayan (Kianian) crown' marked by را. سری here is 'head' (i.e., a headpiece) used appositively — 'we sought the Kayanian crown, a headpiece.' The word order places the object and its appositive after the verb, typical in classical Persian.
segments.s662-g664: که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'. بر هر سری is a prepositional phrase 'on every head'. باشد is the present-subjunctive used here statively ('is' / 'may be' / 'is found'), and او is the third-person pronoun 'it' placed before the predicate اف‌سری (افسری) 'a diadem/helmet' — classical word order putting the pronoun before the predicate for emphasis. The clause states that the crown is a diadem placed on every head.
segments.s662-g673: جز آرام وخوبی نجستم برین — 'نجستم' is the past 1st-person singular of جستن (to seek): 'I sought'. جز + noun phrase means 'nothing except' or 'except for'; here 'جز آرام و خوبی' = 'nothing but calm and goodness'. برین is an older compound meaning 'in/on this' (بر + این), so it attaches a locative/limiting sense ('in this [world/condition]'). Word order is standard poetic inversion: object-like phrase (جز آرام وخوبی) precedes the verb نجستم.
segments.s662-g674: که باشد روان مرا آفرین — که introduces a subordinate/result clause ('so that' / 'that'). باشد is the present/subjunctive/hortative form of بودن used optatively ('may it be' / 'let it be'); here it gives the wish/result sense. روان = 'soul, spirit'. م is the contracted enclitic for مرا (object pronoun 'my / to me'), attached before the object marker را which follows; together 'روان مرا' = 'my soul'. آفرین in early Persian poetry functions as a noun meaning 'praise' or a blessed/well-being sense (i.e. 'praise/blessing for my soul').
segments.s662-g675: امیدم چنانست کز کردگار — امیدم is 'my hope' (امید + possessive suffix -م). چنانست is the contracted poetic form of 'چنان است' = 'is such'. کز = archaic/prepositional compound of که + از meaning 'that/from' and here introduces source: 'that from (the) Creator'. کردگار = 'the Creator / God.'
segments.s662-g676: نباشی جز از شاد و به روزگار — نباشی is the negative subjunctive/optative form of بودن (ن + باشی) used here to express a wish: 'may you be not (anything) except...'; جز = 'except'. از + شاد = 'from / of happy' (here 'only happy'); به روزگار is an idiom meaning 'in good fortune / prosperous times' (به + روزگار). The clause therefore wishes that, by God's will, you be nothing but happy and prosperous.
segments.s662-g677: گر ایمن کنی مردمان را بداد — گر is the conditional 'if'. ایمن functions as an adjective 'safe' and with کنی (subjunctive/present of ساختن/کردن used as 'make') gives 'if you make (someone) safe'. مردمان را — مردمان = 'the people'; را is the definite-object marker. بداد here is a noun meaning 'oppression' or 'wrong, ill-treatment'; the phrase means 'secure the people from oppression' (i.e. make them safe with respect to injustice).
segments.s662-g678: خود ایمن بخسبی و از داد شاد — خود 'yourself'. ایمن again 'safe'; بخسبی is the present/subjunctive of خسبیدن (to sleep) used here in the sense 'you may / will sleep' (poetic present). و = 'and'. از داد شاد literally 'glad because of justice' (شاد = 'happy, glad'; از = 'from / because of'; داد = 'justice'). The sequence says the ruler will himself sleep safely and be joyful if he secures people from oppression.
segments.s662-g679: به پاداش نیکی بیابی بهشت — به پاداش = 'as the reward (for)'. نیکی 'goodness / good deed' is the object of پاداش. بیابی is the present/future/optative form of یافتن ('you will find / you shall attain'); in poetry this form expresses certainty or promise. بهشت = 'paradise / garden' — the object attained as reward.
segments.s662-g680: بزرگ آنک او تخم نیکی بکشت — بزرگ 'great' qualifies بهشت. آنک is an archaic relative/conjunction (that/who/for) introducing the explanatory clause. او = 'he' (referring back to the doer). تخم نیکی 'the seed of goodness' (تخم = seed; نیکی = goodness). بکشت is an archaic poetic perfect/past-form of کشتن/کاشتن here meaning 'he sowed' or 'he planted' — 'for he planted the seed of goodness', giving the reason for the great paradise.
segments.s662-g585: This clause is an assertion + question: 'زیانکارتر کار گفتی' = 'you called the affair more زیانکار.' زیانکارتر is the comparative adjective ('more زیانکار') placed before the noun کار; گفتی is the 2nd-person past/speech verb 'you said/called' (classical diction). The following که چیست begins a direct question ('what is it'), with که as a relative/conjunction linking the question and چی 'what' and ست (archaic copula) finishing the interrogative.
segments.s662-g586: که introduces the subordinate clause ('that/which'); فرجام is the noun 'end/outcome'; ازان is a pronoun-like prepositional phrase 'of/from that'; بد is the adjective 'bad'; بباید is the modal 'one must/it ought to' (classical bāyad-form); گریست is the infinitive/nominal form 'to weep' or an impersonal present meaning 'one weeps/one should weep' — together: 'that its outcome is so bad that one must weep.'
segments.s662-g587: چوچیره شود is a temporal/adverbial phrase: چو (here 'when') + چیره 'prevailing/dominant' + شود 'becomes' = 'when (it) becomes dominant.' بر دلت is the preposition بر 'upon' + دل 'heart' + enclitic ت (ت) marking 2nd-person possessor 'your' = 'upon your heart.' The following بر هوا indicates the thing that prevails is 'هوا' (desire/whim): 'when caprice/whim prevails upon your heart.'
segments.s662-g588: هوا is repeated as the subject/theme 'that mood/whim'; بگذرد is the classical present/subjunctive 'passes' (from گذشتن); همچو 'like' introduces a simile; باد 'wind' is the image; the final هوا reiterates that the passing pertains to that very whim: 'the whim passes like the wind.'
segments.s662-g589: پشیمانی 'regret' is the subject; آرد is the archaic present/preterite form of آوردن 'brings'; بفرجام is the adverbial phrase 'toward/at the end'; سود is 'benefit/profit.' The clause means roughly 'regret, when it comes at the end, brings (no) benefit' (negative force is completed in the next clause).
segments.s662-g590: گل آرزو را is a genitive/accusative construction: گل 'flower' + آرزو 'of longing/desire' + را object marker; نشاید is an impersonal classical negation meaning 'it is not fitting/it cannot be' or 'cannot be of use'; بسود 'to advantage/for profit.' Together: 'the flower of desire cannot be of advantage' — i.e. late regret cannot profit the object of desire.
segments.s662-g591: دگر 'another/other' + آنک 'that/who' introduces a relative 'another who says'; گوید here is the classical verb 'says' (present/gnomic); که introduces reported content ('that'); گردان is used adjectivally 'turning/revolving' meaning 'changeable' or 'fickle'; ترست is the comparative copula 'you are more' (تر + است). The clause reports someone calling the addressee more changeable.
segments.s662-g592: The subordinate clause introduced by که begins with چون 'when/as' (temporal 'when'); پای جویی is a compact phrase literally 'the foot of seeking' (a petitioner/one who seeks); بدستت combines بدست 'to/into the hand' with the 2nd-person enclitic ت 'your' = 'to your hand' or 'at your hand'; سرست is an older/poetic form meaning 'has come/arrived' (classical perfective sense). Altogether: 'that when a seeker's foot has come to your hand' (i.e. when someone comes asking you).
segments.s662-g681: نگر is an imperative used to address the listener ('see to it' / 'take care'). تا here introduces a purposive/subjunctive clause ('so that' / 'that'), and نباشی is the present-subjunctive/negative form meaning 'be not' (i.e. 'do not be'). به جز is the idiom 'except' and بردبار is the predicate describing what you should be ('patient'); the phrase orders the object/quality after the verb, typical of Persian imperatives.
segments.s662-g682: که introduces an explanatory clause ('for / because'). تندی here means 'harshness/fierceness' rather than 'haste'. نه خوب آید is an idiomatic negation literally 'does not come well' = 'is not fitting/ becoming'; آید is the present tense of آمدن used impersonally ('comes' / 'becomes'). از شهریار marks the agent/source ('from a king' = 'for a king').
segments.s662-g683: This is a nominal phrase listing qualities: جهاندار ('sovereign/ruler') followed by وبیدار (the prefixed و is the connective 'and'; وبیدار = 'awake/alert') and فرهنگ‌جوی ('seeker of culture/learning'). In Persian poetry noun + modifiers are often listed before the verb that follows.
segments.s662-g684: بماند is a present-subjunctive/jussive form here giving a wish or injunction ('may he remain' / 'let him remain'). همه‌ساله is an adverbial phrase 'every year' placed after the verb, and با آبروی ('with honor/reputation') is a prepositional complement describing the manner/condition in which he should remain.
segments.s662-g685: بگرد is an imperative that can mean 'go about' or 'seek' (here 'go seeking'). دروغ is the direct object 'falsehood/lie'. ایچ گونه is an idiom 'in no such manner / not at all of that kind' and مگرد is used to prohibit/deny membership in that kind ('do not become / be not of that sort'); together they form a strong prohibition against lying.
segments.s662-g686: چوگردی (here 'چو' = 'when/if' + گرد/گردی imagery 'like dust' or 'when turned to dust') begins a temporal clause; شود is the present-tense auxiliary 'becomes/happens'. The construction بخت را روی زرد literally orders the object (بخت marked by را) before the predicate: 'for fortune (بخت) a face (روی) becomes yellow/pale (زرد)' — i.e. 'fortune's face turns pale.' This inversion is typical in poetic Persian.
segments.s662-g687: دل و مغز را is the definite object ('heart and brain/mind' marked by را). دور دار is an imperative phrase meaning 'keep (them) distant / keep away' (دور = 'distant', دار functioning as 'hold/keep' in the verbal phrase). از شتاب is the prepositional phrase 'from haste' that expresses what they must be kept away from. Word order places the object first for emphasis.
segments.s662-g688: This clause has an object-fronting order: خرد را (wisdom, marked by را) appears before the subject شتاب ('haste') and the verb اندرآرد ('brings in / causes'). اندراَرد is a causative verb meaning 'causes to enter' or 'puts into' and is followed by به خواب ('into sleep'), so the whole means 'haste puts wisdom to sleep.'
segments.s662-g497: سزاکیست is a fusion of سزا (deserving) + کیست (who is), functioning as a rhetorical predicate 'who deserves (it)'. کو را is an archaic/poetic relative/object phrase ('whom') with را marking it as the definite object. نکوهش کنیم places نکوهش (reproach) before the verb; کنیم is a present-subjunctive/optative form ('we should / we might do') so the whole segment asks rhetorically who it would be proper to reproach.
segments.s662-g498: ز کردار او is a prepositional phrase ('from/on account of his deed(s)') that functions adverbially. چون introduces the subordinate clause with a causal/conditional sense ('when / since / if'); پژوهش کنیم uses پژوهش (investigation/examination) plus کنیم (subjunctive 'we do / we examine'), so the clause means 'if/when we examine his deeds' and conditions the main question.
segments.s662-g499: ز گیتی is an adverbial phrase 'from/ in the world' placed frontally for emphasis. کجا is the interrogative adverb 'where' and بهتر آید is a poetic impersonal construction 'is better / comes better'; گریز is the noun 'escape/flight' functioning as the thing being evaluated. Word order is flexible and poetic: the location phrase and interrogative precede the verb phrase.
segments.s662-g500: که begins a relative clause modifying گریز ('the escape which...'). خیزد is the present/poetic form 'arises' or 'springs up'; از آرام او is 'from his calm' (از marking source); رستخیز is placed appositively or in apposition as the defining name of that rising — 'Resurrection' or 'uprising'. The clause thus specifies the kind of escape praised above.
segments.s662-g501: بدین روزگار is a topical adverbial 'in these times' (بدین = 'in this/these' formed from prefix ب- + this). از چه is a rhetorical 'for what reason / why'; باشیم is the present-subjunctive/optative form 'we should be' (here expressing 'should we be' or 'are we to be') and شاد is the predicative adjective 'happy/joyful'. Together they form a rhetorical question 'why should we be happy in these times?'.
segments.s662-g502: گذشته is the topic 'the past'. چه بهتر is an exclamatory phrase 'how much better' or 'how much better it would be', with que emphasis. که introduces the clause of consequence/purpose, and گیریم is a present-subjunctive/optative 'that we take/keep' (گریم = 'we take/hold'), with یاد as its object 'memory / remembrance'. The clause recommends holding the memory of the past.
segments.s662-g503: زمانه is the noun 'age/era' (topic). که introduces a relative/subordinate clause describing that era: او را is the object pronoun 'him' marked by را; بباید is the archaic/modal form 'ought / must' (future/modal auxiliary) and ستود is the verb 'to praise'. The clause reads 'an age that ought to praise him' (i.e., 'an age deserving to praise him').
segments.s662-g504: کدامست is the interrogative 'which is it?' or 'which one is it?'. و ما here links the question to the next: 'and we' (introducing the speaker's perspective). از چه is 'from what / of what' and داریم سود is a present-tense clause 'we have benefit' (داریم = we have; سود = benefit). Together they ask rhetorically what profit we gain from that era.
segments.s662-g457: Word order is classical Persian: modifier + noun + verb, i.e. "نخستین سخن گفت بوزرجمهر" = "(the) first speech said Bozorgmehr." گفت is the past‑tense verb 'said' (3rd singular) and بوزرجمهر is the subject (proper name) placed after the verb for poetic effect.
segments.s662-g458: که‌ای here functions as a vocative interjection ("O ...") addressing the king; شاه نیک اختر خوب‌چهر are a string of epithets in apposition (king / good/virtuous / star / fair‑faced), each modifying the addressee.
segments.s662-g459: چه دانی is an interrogative phrase literally 'what do you know' / 'how do you know' (دانی = you know). کزو = 'from which / from whom'; جان پاک و خرد is a coordination: 'the pure soul and (the) wisdom', with پاک qualifying جان.
segments.s662-g460: شود is the 3rd‑person (present/subjunctive or modal) of شدن 'become' (here: 'become / become manifest'). روشن is the predicate adjective 'luminous/clear'. وکالبد = و + کالبد 'and the body' (poetically joined), and برخورد is a verbal noun or present sense of برخوردن 'to encounter/meet' (here 'encounters' / 'is met by'); together: 'become luminous and the body encounters (it)'.
segments.s662-g461: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed phrase 'thus gave answer' (داد = gave, پاسـخ = answer). که introduces the content clause that follows. دانش به است = 'it is by/through knowledge' — به here functions adverbially ('by/through'), and است is the copula 'is'.
segments.s662-g462: This subordinate clause gives the claim: که داننده ... است. داننده is the subject 'the knower'. The sequence that follows uses the preposition بر twice to mark superiority/position: (بر)مهتران = 'above the great/important ones' (here represented in the text as a compact form) and بر مه = 'above the moon'. است is the copula 'is'. (Note: برمهتران appears as a poetic compound; grammatically it functions as 'بر + مهتران'.)
segments.s662-g463: بدانش is a fused/prepositional form (literally 'by/with that knowledge' = به دانش); بود is past 'was' (here 'there was' or 'he had'), مرد را uses را as a direct‑object/experiential marker ('for the man'), and ایمنی is the nominal predicate 'safety/security' — together: 'By that knowledge the man had safety.'
segments.s662-g464: ببندد is a present/subjunctive or modal form of بستن ('it binds' / 'it secures'). ز is the poetic preposition 'from/against', بد دست = 'evil hand' (بد modifying دست), and اهریمنی is the adjectival qualifier 'demonic / of Ahriman', so: 'it secures (him) from the evil hand of the demon.'
segments.s662-g89: Two past verbs narrating motion: بیامد (he came) then رسید (he reached/arrived). The phrase به نزدیک کسری is a directional phrase (به + noun) meaning “toward/near Khosrow”; کسری is the proper name Khosrow. The verbs are simple past, and the sequence emphasizes approach and arrival.
segments.s662-g90: بگفت is the past reporting verb “he said” (introducing reported speech or a question). آن points to the matter being enquired about, followed by a coordinated series of past verbs (رفت, دید, شنید) linked with و, listing actions: “where did he go, and what did he see and hear?” The subject is omitted (understood) and the verbs are 3rd singular past.
segments.s662-g91: ز گفتار او is a causal/ablative phrase “from his speech/words.” تنگدل گشت شاه is inverted: verb (گشت, ‘became’) + predicate (تنگدل, ‘sorrowful’) + subject (شاه, ‘the king’), so: “the king became sorrowful/from his words.” The verb گشت is past ‘became.’
segments.s662-g92: بدو گفت = “he said to him” (بدو is the dative pronoun ‘to him’). What follows is reported speech: برخوردی از رنج راه reads as a clause meaning “you encountered/suffered (برخوردی) from the hardship(s) of the road.” برخوردی here is an archaic/poetic 2nd‑person past form (“you met/encountered”), and از رنج راه specifies source/thing encountered.
segments.s662-g93: شنیدم is first‑person past “I heard.” که introduces an indirect statement (“that”). هرکو is the archaic fused form of هر که meaning “whoever.” هوا پرورد is a verb phrase where هوا (desire/passion) is the object and پرورد (nurtures) is the verb (3rd singular, classical/poetic present), so: “whoever nurtures desire.”
segments.s662-g94: بفرجام is an adverbial phrase “in the end/at last.” کردار کیفر برد is a compact clause where کردار (deed) and کیفر (punishment) appear before برد, which here is the verb from بردن meaning “to take/receive” (3rd singular past): “in the end the deed receives punishment” (i.e. punishment befalls the deed). Word order places the object/affected noun before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s662-g95: گر introduces a conditional “if.” از دوست دشمن نداند is an idiomatic negative clause meaning “does not know enemy from friend” (از marking the contrast ‘from’). نداند is 3rd‑person present/past negative of دانستن “to know,” and همی is an archaic emphatic/continuous particle common in epic diction.
segments.s662-g96: چنین (thus/so) modifies راز دل (“the secret of the heart”). بر تو is the dative “to you” (بر used poetically for direction), and خواند همی is a classical 3rd‑person imperfective/future‑oriented form meaning “he tells/reads (to)”; همی again gives continuous/emphatic coloring. Literal order: “Thus the secret of the heart to you he tells continually.”
segments.s662-g129: بیاراست is a past narrative verb (Árástan-type) meaning ‘he/they put in order / set up’; here it functions as the main past action. بر هر دری is a prepositional phrase ('بر' + 'هر' + noun) meaning 'on/against each gate' and precedes the noun منجنیق (the direct object), a typical poetic inversion where the location phrase comes before the object.
segments.s662-g130: ز is the preposition 'from'; گردان روم is a genitive/nominal group 'the Roman troop/host' (روم modifies گردان). آنکه is a relative/pronominal particle 'he who / the one who' introducing a nominal identification, and بد جاثلیق is an attributive nominal phrase 'the bad/evil Catholicos' with no overt copula (classical Persian often omits 'was'). Together this clause names a person coming 'from the Roman ranks' in apposition.
segments.s662-g131: حصار سقیلان is a noun phrase 'the fortress of S(q)ilan' (name in postposition to حصار; classical verse may omit the ezafe). بپرداختند is a past plural verb: the prefix بَـ plus پرداختند here carries the sense 'they set about / they dealt with / they attacked' in narration—the main past action on that object.
segments.s662-g132: کزان سو is a fused direction/explanatory phrase: کِ (کـ) + ز + آن → 'from that (place/point)'; سو here means 'side / direction.' همی‌تاختن is the present/stative stem with the old prefix همی- (poetic progressive) 'to charge/attack' used as a verbal noun/infinitive, and ساختند is a light verb 'they made/put into effect' that takes that verbal stem as its object—together meaning 'they launched/caused attacks from that side.'
segments.s662-g133: حلب is the place-name 'Aleppo' and is the sentence subject. شد is the past copula/auxiliary 'became' (resultative). بکردار is a poetic adjectival/adverbial phrase modifying the predicate (literally 'in character/appearance'), here functioning to link the subject to the following nominal predicate دریای خون 'a sea of blood' (a figurative predicative noun phrase).
segments.s662-g134: به زنهار is an adverbial/prepositional phrase 'with alarm / to warning' (زنهار 'warning, alarm'); شد is again the past copula 'became,' and لشکر باطرون is the subject 'the army of Batron' (باطرون here a proper name or epithet attached to لشکر). The clause therefore means the named army was put into alarm/was alarmed.
segments.s662-g135: بدو هفته is an adverbial time phrase ('in that week' / 'within a week') formed idiomatically in classical Persian; here it sets the time frame. از رومیان marks the source 'from the Romans.' The numeral phrase سی هزار (here understood in the line) is the object 'thirty thousand' that they captured.
segments.s662-g136: گرفتند is the past plural main verb 'they took / they captured.' و is the coordinating conjunction 'and.' آمد here is the past 'came,' and بر plus شهریار marks direction/goal 'to (the) king' (بر used to indicate movement toward). Together: 'they captured ... and came to the king.'
segments.s662-g289: This is a nominal phrase placed before the verb: 'پدر' (father) modified by the adjective/participle 'مرده' (dead). The conjunction 'و' links a coordinated descriptor 'ناسپرده' (literally 'not entrusted / unrevealed'), and 'جهان' (the world) follows as the topical subject or scene-setter. Word order is topical/poetic (noun phrase before the verb), so this whole chunk functions as the object/topic that the following verb will comment on.
segments.s662-g290: 'نداند' is the classical 3rd-person singular negative of دانستن, 'does not know.' The particle 'همی' is an older continuous/emphatic particle (here carrying the sense 'still/indeed/at all') that emphasizes the verb. 'آشکار و نهان' is an adverbial pair 'openly and hiddenly' (i.e. 'whether apparent or secret') that complements 'نداند.' Note the omitted copula/predicator typical of classical Persian — the verb directly governs the preceding nominal phrase.
segments.s662-g291: 'همه سر به سر' is an idiomatic intensifier meaning 'entirely / altogether / through and through' (literally 'all head to head'). 'باژدار' is an adjectival epithet 'resounding, clamorous' that qualifies the speaker's devotion or praise. 'توایم' is an older fused form meaning 'I am yours' (تو + am), so the clause reads 'I am wholly (this) to you.' The adjective cluster precedes the copular sense carried by 'توایم' as is common in poetic Persian.
segments.s662-g292: 'پرستار' here is the noun 'worshipper / adorer' and continues the speaker's self-description. The conjunction 'و' links the next prepositional phrase. 'در زینهار' is 'in (or under) admonition/warning' (زینهار = warning/admonition); with 'توایم' again ('I am yours') the clause means 'and I am (also) within/under your admonition' — i.e., obedient or heedful of you. The structure repeats the predicate 'توایم' for emphasis.
segments.s662-g293: This clause is compact and somewhat elliptical. 'تو را' places 'you' with the enclitic 'را' (object/emphatic marker used here for topicality/dative sense), and 'روم' and 'ایران' are the place-names (Rūm/Rome and Iran). The particle 'چو' means 'when' (or 'as'), and the second 'روم' repeats the place-name; the line juxtaposes going to Rūm and to Iran and introduces a temporal/conditional 'چو' ('when/as'). Poetic compression leaves some relations implied (i.e. 'if/when I go to Rūm or Iran').
segments.s662-g294: 'جدایی' is the noun 'separation' placed front for emphasis/topic. 'چرا' is the interrogative 'why' and 'باید' is the modal 'must / ought (to be).' 'این مرز و بوم' is the definite demonstrative phrase 'this border and land' (مرز = border, بوم = homeland). The order is topical/interrogative: 'Separation — why must this border and land (be)?' i.e., 'Why must there be any separation of this land?'
segments.s662-g295: 'خرد' (wisdom) is the subject; the prepositional phrase 'در زمانه شهنشاه' ('in the age/time of the King of Kings') specifies the era. The adjective 'راست' follows as predicate ('was right/upright/straight') — in classical Persian the copula is often omitted and the adjective follows the nominal phrase it qualifies.
segments.s662-g296: 'وزو داشت' literally reads 'had wuzu/ablution' (وزو = ablution), here idiomatically 'kept ritual purity' or 'was ceremonially pure/observant.' 'قیصر' is the subject 'Caesar.' The adverbial/compound 'همی‌پشت' means 'constantly behind/at the rear' or 'continually in support,' and 'راست' again means 'right' (the right side). Together the clause says Caesar kept ablution and was always on the right (i.e., in the proper/authorized position); word order places the possessed/held action before the subject as is common in poetry.
segments.s662-g65: چو + past verb (= چوبشنید style) here functions as a temporal conjunction 'when'. The verb بیاراستند is past plural 'they adorned/arranged', and قرطاس رومی is the direct object (a 'Roman' parchment/paper) with adjective after the noun, a typical older Persian word order.
segments.s662-g66: فرستاده را خواستند = 'they summoned/asked for the envoy' (را marks the definite object). The preposed item بدربر is an archaic/poetic locution here meaning essentially 'to the court / into the presence' (compare modern به دربار) and links to خواستند as the destination/setting of the summons.
segments.s662-g67: چو بشنید (here written چوبشنید) = 'when he heard'; دانا is the subject 'the wise (man)' placed after the verb, which is common in classical Persian. که introduces the content clause 'that', شد is past 'became/was made', and رای راست = 'a right/true counsel/decision'.
segments.s662-g68: بیامد + proper name بدر = 'Badr came' (verb before subject). پاسخ‌نامه خواست is a second clause: خواست is past 'requested/demanded' and پاسخ‌نامه is its object 'the reply-letter'. The two verbs are coordinated in sequence to report successive actions.
segments.s662-g69: ورا is the object pronoun 'to him' (preposed), ناسزا is an adjective/noun 'insult' qualifying خلعتی 'a robe (bestowed garment)', and ساختند is past plural 'they made' — together: 'they fashioned for him an insulting robe.'
segments.s662-g70: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from / with regard to / to' marking relation; بیگانه ایوان can be read as 'the iwan (hall) for the foreigner' (i.e. assigned to the stranger), and بپرداختند is a past plural verb 'they paid/handed over/attended to' — contextually 'they handed the iwan over to the foreigner'.
segments.s662-g71: بدو = 'to him', and گفت (here used as the past 'he said') is followed by the subject قیصر (verb–subject order). نه begins the negation of the reported speech; من چاکرم = 'I am a chākir/servant' (here negated: 'I am not a servant').
segments.s662-g72: نه ... کمترم is a negated comparative: از marks the standard of comparison ('than / compared with'), چین و هیتالیان are the groups compared (China and the Hitalians), and کمترم is first‑person predicate 'I am lesser / inferior' — with نه it yields 'I am not inferior to X.'
segments.s662-g73: This clause uses the poetic preposition ز + noun + verbal noun to express the act: ز مهتر سبک داشتن literally 'to have the chief light' → 'to treat the superior as light/insignificant.' داشتـن is the infinitival/nominal form 'to have' used here to form the action 'to consider/lighten.' ناسزاست is a present-state predicate formed on ناسزا meaning 'an insult,' so the whole clause reads 'treating a superior lightly is an insult.'
segments.s662-g74: وگر is a concessive/conditional 'but if / even if.' شاه تو is 'you, (though) king' with the pronoun تو for emphasis. بر جهان is the prepositional phrase 'over the world' (بر = over/on), and پادشاست is the present copular/predicative form 'is a sovereign/king.' Together: 'and even if you are king over the world.'
segments.s662-g75: بزرگ here is the predicate 'great.' آنک is a classical relative particle 'who/that,' introducing the clause او را بسی دشمنست 'there are many enemies to him.' را marks the person as theme/topic, بسی (classical) expresses plurality/intensity ('many' / 'much'), and دشمنست is a present predicate meaning 'are enemies / is hostile.' The overall structure is inverted for poetic emphasis: 'Great is he who has many enemies.'
segments.s662-g76: مرا here functions as 'for me' (the dative/experiencer); in the provided tokenization it is split, so را is shown separately as the object/topic marker attached to me. 'دشمن و دوست' is a coordinated noun phrase 'enemy and friend.' بردامنست is an archaic contraction of بر دامن است ('are on the hem/lie on the skirt'), a figurative expression meaning they cling to or are upon (my) hem. The clause means 'for me both enemy and friend are on the hem.'
segments.s662-g77: چه at the clause start is an exclamatory/interrogative 'why' here. داری is second-person present 'you have/keep.' بزرگی تو is the object 'your greatness.' از من دریغ is the idiom 'to withhold from me' (دریغ 'reluctance/withholding'); word order places the withheld thing before the 'from me' phrase. The sense: 'Why do you keep your greatness from me?'
segments.s662-g78: همی is the older continuous/habitual marker 'always/continually.' آفتاب is the object 'sun' used metaphorically for radiance/glory. اندر here is the prepositional 'in/with,' and آری is an archaic 2nd-person present of آوردن ('thou bringest'), so together 'you always bring.' بمیغ (بِ میغ) is an instrumental phrase 'with the sword' (میغ = blade). Grammatically: 'You always bring the sun (i.e. glory) with the sword.' Note: آری is an archaic verb form (2nd sing. present).
segments.s662-g79: نه negates the clause. از تابش او is the prepositional phrase 'by/from his radiance.' همی + شود forms a present/habitual verbal phrase 'is/does become' (here with کم 'less'), so همی کم شود = 'is not lessened/does not decrease.' Word order places the source phrase before the verb: 'It is not lessened by his shining.'
segments.s662-g80: وگر introduces a conditional 'and if / but if.' خون چکاند is a compact clause 'he spills/sheds blood' (چکاند here = 'sheds/drips'). برو is actually a contracted classical sequence (بر او = 'upon it / on it') in this poetic spelling; نم = 'moisture/dew'; شود 'becomes.' So the phrase says 'and if he sheds blood upon it, it does not become moisture' — meaning the radiance/thing is not dampened or stained even by blood. Note: some tokens are archaic/contracted (برو = بر او).
segments.s662-g81: "چو" introduces a temporal clause meaning "when." "کار" here means "affair/case," and "آیدم" is a (classical) third-person verb آید "comes" with the enclitic -م "to me" (i.e., "comes to me"). The main clause that follows, "شهریارم تویی," is declarative: شهریارم (شهریار + possessive -م = "my king") is the predicative noun and تویی ("you are") is the copula/predicate, so the whole reads "When the affair comes to me, you are my king."
segments.s662-g82: "همان" is a demonstrative pronoun meaning "that very/the same." The phrase "از پدر یادگارم" is a genitive phrase: از پدر "from my father" and یادگارم (یادگار + -م) "my keepsake/legacy." The sentence ends with تویی "you are," so the full sense is "You are that very keepsake from my father."
segments.s662-g83: سخن is the noun "speech/what you say." هرچ ("whatever") quantifies سخن. دیدی is a past tense of دیدن directed at the addressee—"you saw"—so "سخن هرچ دیدی" = "whatever speech (thing) you saw." بخوبی is an adverb "well/clearly," and بگوی here is the (classical) hortative/imperative form meaning "say/tell" (second-person), so the clause means "Tell well whatever you saw."
