segments.s662-g84: وزین is an adjective meaning "weighty/dignified" modifying پاسخ‌نامه "answer/response letter." زشتی is the noun "ugliness" and مجوی is a negative imperative/hortative "do not seek/look for." The clause therefore warns: "in this weighty response, do not seek ugliness." Grammatically, وزین پاسخ‌نامه is the topic and مجوی is the verb commanding the addressee not to hunt for vice in that reply.
segments.s662-g85: تنش is تن + possessive -ش "his person/his body" and را marks it as the definite object. بخلعت is a prepositional/instrumental phrase (بِ + خلعت) meaning "with the robe/of investiture." بیاراستند is a past plural verb "they adorned/they arrayed." Word order is object (تنش را) + adjunct (بخلعت) + verb (بیاراستند): "they adorned his person with the robe."
segments.s662-g86: ز is the poetic preposition "from." در functions here as "(the) court/door/gate" (poetic use for the royal court). باره in the idiom در باره means "concerning/about" (here read as a connective), and مرزبان is the object "marzban/frontier governor." خواستند is past plural "they requested/wanted." Together the clause says they requested (or sought) the marzban from the court: "from the palace they asked for the marzban."
segments.s662-g87: فرستاده is the subject "the messenger/envoy." برگشت and آمد are two coordinated past verbs "returned and came." دمان is a locative noun meaning "skirt/edge/outskirts" (the outskirts of a place) and here gives the place he arrived at: "the envoy returned and came to the outskirts."
segments.s662-g88: به منزل is a directional phrase: به + منزل "to the dwelling/house." زمانی here is temporal "for a while/for a time." نجستی is the past form of جستن in the sense "to seek/search" directed at the second person or third (classical morphology can vary); here it functions as a past-tense verb meaning "searched/sought." زمان is the noun "time/period." The clause reads roughly "he went to the dwelling and for a while searched (concerning) the time."
segments.s662-g665: خردمند is a predicative adjective meaning “wise.” شش (not broken into the word list) qualifies خردمند as “six‑fold/with six qualities.” بود is the past copula “was.” ما را پسر is an older/poetic expression meaning “a son of ours” (ما = we/us; را marks the definite/direct object or dative/possessive relation here). Word order places the predicate before the possessive phrase.
segments.s662-g666: This is a coordinated list of adjectival epithets describing the son. دل‌فروز is a compound in which دل “heart” + فروز (“bright/illuminating”) yields “heart‑brightening.” و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” بخشنده and دادگر are adjectives meaning “generous” and “just” respectively.
segments.s662-g667: تو را برگزیدم = “I chose you,” with را marking the definite object. که introduces a purposive/result clause here (“so that/that”). مهتر is the predicate noun “chief” or “leading man.” بدی is an archaic/poetic second‑person form of بودن (to be), functioning as an optative/subjunctive “may you be / you should be,” so the clause reads “that you be chief.”
segments.s662-g668: A continuation of the predicate specifying the kind of chief: خردمند and زیبای are coordinated adjectives (“wise and beautiful”). افسر here functions as a substantive meaning the helmet (an honorific headgear); it combines with these adjectives. بدی again is the archaic 2nd‑person copular form of بودن (see above) agreeing with the implicit subject “you.”
segments.s662-g669: بهشتاد is a noun/adjective meaning something like “paradisal” or “paradise” used as the subject. بر is the preposition “on/over/upon.” بود is the past copula “was.” پایِ قباد = “the foot of Qobad” is the locational complement; the whole phrase means “Paradise was at Qobad’s feet” (a poetic way to praise Qobad).
segments.s662-g670: که introduces a relative clause referring back to the preceding idea. در پادشاهی = “in (his) kingship/kingship” (در = in; پادشاهی = kingship/kingdom). مرا را here is split in the word list (م + را); together مرا = “me” as the object. کرد is the past tense of کردن “to make/do,” and یاد is “remembrance/memory”; کرد یاد = “caused (me) to remember” or “reminded me.”
segments.s662-g671: کنون من رسیدم = “now I have reached/arrived.” برسیدن + number uses به before the numeral: به هفتاد و چار = “to seventy and four,” i.e. “seventy‑four.” رسیدم is the past tense “I reached/attained.” The numeral is formed with هفتاد و چار (“seventy and four” = seventy‑four).
segments.s662-g672: تو را کردم (here written راکردم as a single token) = “I made you,” with را marking the object and کردم from کردن meaning “I made/did.” اندر = “in/within,” جهان = “the world,” and شهریار is the predicate noun “sovereign/king.” The phrase thus means “I made you sovereign in the world.”
segments.s662-g537: سخن هرچ او گفت پاسخ دهم places the object phrase before the verb, typical of poetic/older Persian: 'سخن هرچ او گفت' = 'whatever words he said'. 'پاسخ دهم' is a first‑person present/subjunctive form (دهم) meaning 'I give / I shall give' and functions here as the speaker's intention to answer. The whole segment therefore reads: 'I shall answer whatever he said.'
segments.s662-g538: بدین آرزو رای فرخ نهم: 'بدین' = ب + دین here functions as the prepositional/compound 'by/with this' (i.e. 'with this wish'); 'آرزو' is the noun 'wish/desire'. 'رای فرخ نهم' has رای ('opinion/plan/counsel') modified by فرخ ('fortunate') and نهم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive of نهادن 'to put/give' meaning 'I give/offer'. Together: 'with this wish I will give a fortunate counsel.'
segments.s662-g539: ز فرزند پرسید دانا سخن: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (marking the person addressed or source). The verb پرسید is past third‑person 'asked'. دانا ('the wise one') is an epithet applied to the asker; سخن is the object meaning 'words/question'. Word order is topicalized (object/agent before verb), so: 'The wise one asked (a) question of the child.'
segments.s662-g540: وزو بایدم پاسخ افگند بن: وزو is an archaic/fused form meaning 'from him/unto him' (وز + او) used here to indicate the addressee/recipient. بایدم = باید + م is first‑person 'I must/ought to'. پاسخ افگند uses پاسخ ('answer') with افگند (past of افکندن 'to cast/throw') used figuratively as 'to utter/offer' an answer; بن is a short particle/noun form appearing in classical usage (here concessive/closing particle or lexical relic). The segment thus means 'and to him I must give (offer) an answer.'
segments.s662-g541: به فرزند باشد پدر شاددل: به فرزند is a prepositional phrase 'on account of / for the child'. باشد is the literary present/subjunctive form of 'to be' used here as a present tense 'is/shall be'. پدر شاددل is the noun + adjective 'the father (is) joyful'. Word order (prepositional phrase before verb and predicate) is typical of classical poetry.
segments.s662-g542: ز غمها بدو دارد آزاد دل: ز غمها = 'from/because of sorrows' (here indicating that sorrows are the thing from which the heart is freed); بدو = 'for/to him' (the child as beneficiary); دارد is the present 'has/possesses'; آزاد دل is a compound predicate 'a free/untroubled heart'. Together: 'his heart is free of sorrows for him.'
segments.s662-g543: اگر مهربان باشد او بر پدر: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. مهربان is the predicate adjective 'kind/compassionate'. باشد is the literary present/subjunctive 'is/would be' used in the protasis. او is the subject pronoun 'he'. بر پدر uses the preposition بر ('toward/on') to indicate the target of kindness: 'toward his father.'
segments.s662-g544: به نیکی گراینده و دادگر: به نیکی = 'toward goodness' (به with an abstract noun), گراینده is a present‑participle/agentive meaning 'one who inclines/tends' (i.e. 'inclined to'), and دادگر is an adjectival/nominal predicate 'just' or 'righteous'. The coordination 'گراینده و دادگر' yields 'inclined to goodness and (being) just.'
segments.s662-g145: Linear subject–object–verb order in classical/poetic Persian: سپهدار (subject) + روزی‌دهان را (definite object marked by را) + بخواند (3rd person past narrative). The verb بخواند here is the poetic past used to mean 'called' or 'summoned' rather than 'read'.
segments.s662-g146: وزان introduces a prepositional idea 'and/from that (war)'; جنگ is the noun governed by it. چندی functions adverbially 'for a while', سخنها ('words, speeches') is the plural theme, and براند (poetic past 3rd sg with ب-) is used metaphorically here as 'drove forth' → 'uttered/spoke'.
segments.s662-g147: که introduces the explanatory subordinate clause 'that/because'. این کار is the clause subject 'this affair', با رنج بسیار is an instrumental/attendant phrase 'with great hardship', and گشت is the poetic past of شدن meaning 'became'.
segments.s662-g148: به آب و به کنده are parallel instrumental/prepositional phrases 'by water and by uprooting' (به repeated for emphasis). نشاید is an impersonal/poetic negative modal 'it is not fitting/possible', and گذشت (verbal noun/past sense) completes the idée 'to pass/accomplish' → 'cannot be passed/accomplished'.
segments.s662-g149: سپه را fronted with را marks the affected party ('the army'), followed by درم باید: in this impersonal/nominal construction باید means 'it is necessary / is needed', so 'the army needs money'. The conjunction و links the next noun 'دستگاه' (equipment/array).
segments.s662-g150: همان introduces the list as 'the same / namely', followed by a coordination of items (اسب, وخفتان, و رومی کلاه). وخفتان is an archaic/poetic equipment term (here a piece of riding gear), and رومی کلاه is a compound 'Roman (Byzantine) helmet'.
segments.s662-g151: سوی گنج is a prepositional phrase 'toward the treasury'. رفتند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they went'; the actors follow the verb (inverted order common in poetry), so روزی‌دهان is one of the going parties and agrees with the plural verb.
segments.s662-g152: A coordinated list of actors: دبیران 'secretaries' (plural), و 'and', گنجور here functions as the treasurer/treasury official connected to شاه جهان ('the king of the world') — the genitive relationship is implied in the poetic style. شاه جهان is a title 'king of the world'.
segments.s662-g9: گزین کرد is a compound past: گزین (root meaning 'choose') + کرد (past auxiliary 'did') = 'he chose'. The phrase that follows, ز ایران فرستاده‌ای, is the direct object (a messenger from Iran) introduced by the poetic preposition ز 'from'. Note the SOV tendency: the verbal kernel appears before the explicit noun phrase object here.
segments.s662-g10: This is a string of adjectival / descriptive phrases qualifying the messenger. جهاندیده and راد are epithets joined by و, and آزاده‌ای is an indefinite nominal-adjective ('a free/noble one') that completes the noun phrase. Adjectives and epithets follow the noun they modify in this poetic order.
segments.s662-g11: فرستاد is the simple past 'he sent/dispatched'. نزدیک is an adverb/prepositional adverb meaning 'near/close to' and combines with the following noun فرزند اوی ('his son') to give the destination/position. اوی is an archaic possessive 'his'.
segments.s662-g12: برشاخ is the prepositional phrase 'on/upon the branch' (بر + شاخ fused). سبز and برومند are consecutive adjectives ('green, flourishing') describing the branch; the possessive اوی ('his') attaches at the end. The whole phrase specifies the location where he was sent.
segments.s662-g13: سخن گفت is a light-verbal construction meaning 'he spoke' or 'he held speech'; سخن is the noun 'speech' and گفت is the verbal element. با او marks the interlocutor 'with him'. به چربی بسی is an adverbial phrase: به + noun (چربی 'oil/grease', metaphorically 'flattery') with بسی 'much' — 'with much oily/flattering talk.'
segments.s662-g14: کزین is a contraction of کز + این = 'from this'; بد qualifies the following noun رهایی ('release, freedom'), so 'this evil/reproach/ill'. نیابد is a present/subjunctive-form verb with negative prefix ن- meaning 'does not/finds not' or 'cannot obtain'. کسی ('anyone') falls under the negative to mean 'no one.' The clause states that no one can attain release from this misfortune.
segments.s662-g15: یکی is the indefinite subject 'a certain/someone'. نامه is the direct object 'a letter'. بنوشت is the past 'wrote' (classical/poetic past form equivalent to نوشت); note the given lemma/senses are irregular in the metadata, so the normal reading is past tense 'he wrote'. با سوگ و درد is a manner phrase 'with mourning and grief'.
segments.s662-g16: پر از is a fixed phrase 'full of'. آب‌دیده is a compound (literally 'water-seen') used poetically for tear-wet or tearful; دو رخساره 'two cheeks' (i.e. both cheeks) names the affected facial parts; زرد 'pale/wan' describes their color. Together: 'full of tears, his two cheeks pale.'
segments.s662-g601: تباهی (subject) = 'destruction/ruin'. که introduces a relative clause ('that/which'). گفتی here is the past-tense verb 'you said' (classical/poetic usage) referring back to what was called 'destruction'. ز is the poetic form of از ('from'), and گفتار = 'speech/utterance'. کیست (کی + ست) is the interrogative copula 'who/what is'. The phrase as a whole asks which destruction the speaker meant as coming from words.
segments.s662-g602: پرآزارتر is the comparative adjective formed from پرآزار + -تر, 'more troublesome/more tormenting'. It precedes the noun phrase it modifies (a normal Persian adjective order). درد آزار is a compound noun phrase ('pain/affliction' and 'harm/torment') and کیست again asks 'who/what is it'. The line rhetorically asks which of these is the greater torment.
segments.s662-g603: This is a coordinated list of epithets joined by و: 'سخن‌چین و دو روی و بیکار' = 'a tattletale, two‑faced, and idle'. These modifiers precede the head noun مرد, a common Persian order (modifiers before noun). دو روی is a compound meaning 'two‑faced'.
segments.s662-g604: دل هوشیاران is a genitive construction = 'the heart of the watchful/wise'. کند is the present/periodic third‑person verb 'makes' or 'fills'. پر ز means 'full of' (پر = 'full (of)'; ز = poetic از 'of/from'), and درد = 'pain/sorrow'. So the clause reads 'he fills the hearts of the wise with pain.'
segments.s662-g605: بپرسید can be read as an exhortation 'ask' (imperative/hortative) or as past 'asked' in classical narrative; here it functions as an address to inquire. دانا is the addressee 'wise one'. که introduces the embedded question 'why/with respect to what' (عیب از چه بیش = 'in what respect is the fault greater'). بیش = 'more/greater'.
segments.s662-g606: که باشد is a subordinate clause marker + subjunctive/optative copula 'that (he) be/is (may be)'. پشیمان = 'regretful/repentant'. ز گفتار خویش is a genitive phrase 'because of / about his own speech' — note خویش is split as خوی + ش for metrical/formal reasons but functions as the reflexive pronoun 'his own'. The clause asks whether the proper result is that one should regret his words.
segments.s662-g607: هرآنکس که = 'whoever'. راند here is an old/poetic past form meaning 'uttered/put forth' or 'drove forth' (transitive use with سخن). سخن بر گزاف = 'speech that is groundless/empty' (بر + گزاف indicates 'in a manner of absurdity/groundlessness'). The clause identifies the person who utters such talk.
segments.s662-g608: بود is the copula 'was/is' (here narrative/prescriptive 'is'). بر سر انجمن is an idiomatic locution 'before/at the head of the assembly' or 'in the gathering'. مرد لاف = 'a bragging man' (mord laaf = braggart). The whole line says such a person appears as a braggart at gatherings.
segments.s662-g361: چو here is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. The clause follows Persian word order: modifier (نزدیک شهر) + verbal phrase (اندر آمد). اندر آمد is a classical perfective 'came/entered'. ز راه is a prepositional phrase ('from/by the road') placed after the verb in poetic order.
segments.s662-g362: پذیره شدندش is a verb phrase in which شدند (past plural) marks the verbal action and the enclitic -ش = 'him' is the object (he who arrived). In classical usage پذیره (reception/received) with شدند yields 'received him' or 'he was received'; فراوان سپاه at the end is the plural subject 'a great host' that agrees with شدند.
segments.s662-g363: همه is the subject 'all/everyone'. پیش کسری is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of Khosrow' (پیش = before/in front of). پیاده شدند is past plural 'they became on foot' — i.e. they dismounted — with the verb at the end as usual.
segments.s662-g364: This is a coordinated predicate describing the same subject (همه). کمر بسته is an adjectival phrase literally 'waist-bound' (i.e. girded/ready), و links the second predicate, and دل گشاده شدند is 'their hearts became open'—گشاده used adjectivally and شدند (past plural) applies to both predicates.
segments.s662-g365: هر آنکس که ... is a free relative 'whoever/anyone who'. پیمود is the simple past 'traveled/covered' and با شاه‌راه indicates the route/instrument 'by the royal road' (با here = 'by/along').
segments.s662-g366: پیاده بشد combines the predicate 'on foot' with بشد (classical past/auxiliary 'became/it came to pass'), i.e. 'he went on foot' or 'dismounted'. تا here marks extent/destination 'as far as/to' (تا در بارگاه = up to the palace). The verb (بشد) is placed before the adverbial complement, as common in classical Persian.
segments.s662-g367: همه مهتران is the subject 'all the chiefs/nobles' (مهتران = great ones/leaders). خواندند is past plural 'they uttered/called out' and here takes آفرین as the direct object — they exclaimed 'آفرین' (an acclamation).
segments.s662-g368: آفرین بران شاه is the phrase 'bravo to that king' (بران = 'upon/for that'), followed by بیدار (an epithet 'alert/awake'). باداد is an archaic past/auxiliary meaning 'gave/granted' and ودین is 'and religion' or more narrowly 'the religion/faith' as the object. Word order places the acclamation and its target first, then a short comment about the king who 'gave/granted (the) religion'.
segments.s662-g425: This is a string of epithets describing a person: 'سر افراز' (lit. head‑high) = 'exalted/proud', 'بادانش' is the fused poetic form of 'با دانش' = 'with knowledge/learned', and 'خوب‌چهر' = 'fair‑faced'. The conjunction 'و' simply links the epithets; in Persian poetic style such adjective‑like phrases follow one another to build a compound description.
segments.s662-g426: 'بر آزادگان' is an indirect phrase meaning 'toward / to the free (nobles, freeborn)'; the verb/verb‑form is 'بگسترده' (past participle 'spread'), and 'مهر' is the object meaning 'kindness/favour'. The first 'بر' marks direction ('to/toward'), the second 'بر' before the participle works idiomatically with 'بگسترده' ('has spread over/on'); overall: 'has spread his favor toward the free.'
segments.s662-g427: بفرمود is an honorific past verb meaning 'he ordered/said' and takes 'کسری' (Kisra) as the subject. 'به کارآگهان' is the indirect object 'to the informed/aware ones' (here: his agents or informants). The preposition 'به' marks the recipients of the order.
segments.s662-g428: The particle 'که' introduces the reported or subordinate clause. 'جویند' is a present/subjunctive‑flavored form ('they should seek' / 'let them seek'), governed by the command; 'راز وی' = 'his secret' (راز = secret, وی = his); 'اندر نهان' = 'in/within hidden' = 'in secret'. The clause therefore means 'that they seek his secret in hiding.'
segments.s662-g429: نگه داشتندی is the compound verbal construction meaning 'they kept/held watch' (نگه = watch/keeping; داشتندی = past plural 'kept'). 'به‌روز و به شب' are temporal adverbials 'by day and by night' introduced by the preposition 'به' to indicate times.
segments.s662-g430: 'اگر' sets a conditional 'if/should'. 'داستان را' is the definite object 'the tale' (را marks the object). 'گشادی' is an action form from 'گشادن' = 'to open/reveal' (here second person: 'you should open/reveal'), and 'دو لب' ('two lips') is a figurative instrumental phrase meaning 'with your mouth / if you open your two lips to speak.'
segments.s662-g431: 'ز' here introduces the reason/cause ('because of' / 'from'). 'کاری' (literally 'a deed/action') is the noun governed by ز; 'که کردی' is a relative clause 'that you did'. The phrase 'بدی با بهی' coordinates the two qualities 'bad' and 'good' (an archaic pairing meaning 'whether bad or good'); syntactically it qualifies the preceding 'کار' so: 'the deed you did, bad or good.'
segments.s662-g432: رسیدی is the past form 'you reached/arrived' (here used transitively/locatively: 'you came to'). 'بشاه' = 'to the king' (the indirect object). 'جهان آگهی' is a noun phrase where 'جهان' (world) qualifies 'آگهی' (news/tidings/information) — i.e. 'tidings for the ruler of the world' or simply 'to the king of the world with tidings.'
segments.s662-g49: که introduces a subordinate clause meaning "that." امروز is an adverbial ('today'); قیصر is the subject and جوانست is a fused present-predicative ('جوان' + copula -ست) meaning "is young." و نو links a second predicate adjective (نو = "new/fresh") to the same subject; poetic word order allows the two predicates after the subject.
segments.s662-g50: به گوهر is a prepositional phrase meaning roughly "in/with (regard to) essence or gem-like quality." بدین is the preposition ب- attached to this (دین = poetic variant of 'این' = "these"), so بدین مرزها = "to/for these borders." پیشرو is a predicative noun 'forerunner/vanguard' that links back to قیصر (the copula is omitted), so the whole phrase says Caesar is, in essence, a forerunner to these frontiers.
segments.s662-g51: امسال sets the time "this year." با + مرد برنا is a simple prepositional phrase "with a young/robust man." مکاو is uncertain/obscure here (it may be a proper name or an archaic/poetic word); the phrase as a whole places an accompaniment or agent for the action in the following clause.
segments.s662-g52: به عنوان is a fixed phrase meaning "under the pretext/title (of)." بیشی here means something like "greater/supreme" (an adjective or nominalized quality). و با introduces additional accompaniment "and with ..."; باژ و ساو are listed in coordination and likely function as names or items grouped with the previous phrase. The clause is elliptical/poetic and expects the main verb in the surrounding lines.
segments.s662-g53: بهرپایمردی is a prepositional/nominal phrase meaning "for the sake of (or in praise of) steadfastness/firmness." و links the next noun; خودکامه‌ای is an adjectival/nominal form meaning "autocratic/self-willed one" (the -ای ending gives an indefinite/nominal sense). Together the phrase states the theme or purpose related to the action in the next segment.
segments.s662-g54: نبشتند is the past negative plural "they did not write" (classical negative prefix ن- with past stem). بر here marks the content/target ('on, concerning') and ناسزا نامه‌ای = "a letter of abuse/insult" (ناسزا "insult" + نامه‌ای indefinite "a letter"). Word order is object phrase after the verb, the usual classical arrangement.
segments.s662-g55: بعنوان here means "under the title/heading." ز (poetic 'from') introduces the sender/author: ز قیصر = "from Caesar." سرافراز "honored/triumphant" is an epithet placed before or after the name as a poetic epithet; روم specifies 'of Rome/Byzantium.' The phrase imitates a formal epistolary rubric "Under the title/from Caesar, triumphant of Rome."
segments.s662-g56: جهان سر به سر is a reduplicated/idiomatic expression meaning "the whole world, completely." هرچ emphasizes "everything/each thing," and جز روم "except Rome." شوم is the first‑person optative/subjunctive "may I become / I might be," expressing a wish or boast; together the clause expresses the wish to possess the entire world except Rome (elliptical, poetic syntax).
segments.s662-g417: دگر + کو is a fixed poetic interrogative ('where else/where now'); کو here functions like classical Persian 'where is/are (there)'. بدرویش is an old contracted/prepositional-looking form (here best read as 'به درویش' = 'to the dervish') placed before the preposition and adjective — a poetic word‑order inversion. بر is the preposition 'toward/to' governing مهربان, which is the predicative adjective 'kind'.
segments.s662-g418: بود is the past third‑person copula 'was' linking the subject implied by the question to the following predicate adjectives. راد is a predicative adjective 'generous'; و simply coordinates the following qualities. بی‑رنج is a compound adjective 'free from sorrow/without trouble' (carefree), and روشن‌روان is a poetic compound adjective 'bright‑souled / cheerful' describing the same person.
segments.s662-g419: The nominal پسر 'son' opens the phrase; the following items form an economical, somewhat elliptical description in archaic order. مر is an old dative pronoun 'to me' (or 'for me') and او را is the third‑person pronoun with the object marker را — together signaling possession/relationship. گرانمایه is an adjective 'precious/esteemed'. The numeral شش (six) follows to enumerate them; the exact syntactic links are condensed in this poetic line (elliptical copula implied).
segments.s662-g420: همه 'all' quantifies the previously mentioned sons. راد is again the adjective 'generous'; وبینادل and وشاه show the classical habit of writing the conjunction و attached to the following word (here 'and‑uncomplaining' and 'and‑king'). These coordinated descriptors list qualities: 'uncomplaining/without lament' (وبینادل) and a final compound where فش (literally 'press/oppress') completes the image (poetic, somewhat compressed phraseology).
segments.s662-g421: This is a piled list of abstract virtues linked by و ('and'). بمردی is an abstract/noun meaning 'manliness' or 'manly conduct' and here functions as one item in the series. فرهنگ 'culture/education', پرهیز 'restraint/forbearance' and رای 'counsel/decision' are parallel nouns; the repeated و simply coordinates these qualities into a single enumerated phrase.
segments.s662-g422: جوانان is the plural subject 'the young men/young people'. با is the preposition 'with' linking them to دانش ('learning/knowledge'), and the final دلگشای is an adjectival compound 'heart‑pleasing' or 'delightful', describing those young men; the order is typical of poetic compression (noun + با + attribute + adjective).
segments.s662-g423: از ایشان is an ablative/partitive phrase 'from/among them' introducing who is singled out. خردمند 'wise' and مهتر 'chief/elder' are coordinated predicates or appositions describing individuals taken from that group. بسال is an adverbial/time phrase (classical usage 'in the year' / 'each year' depending on context) positioned after the nouns in a typically terse poetic order.
segments.s662-g424: گرانمایه is an epithet 'esteemed/precious' attached to the proper name هرمزد (Hormozd/Ahura Mazda here likely a personal name). بد in this line does not behave as a straightforward negative adjective in modern usage and the phrase بی‌همال is a poetic compound 'without equal / unmatched'; together they form a short laudatory tag 'good/peerless' (the precise function of بد here is archaic/poetic).
segments.s662-g697: چو is a conditional 'if/when' introducing the subordinate clause; here it means 'if'. جهان را places 'world' as a definite/marked object (را) before the verb, a common Persian order (Obj–V). آباد داری is a present tense/actional expression 'you keep/maintain (prosperous)'; بداد here functions adverbially/nominally to indicate the means ('by/with justice') in a compressed, poetic form rather than the concrete noun sense given in the list.
segments.s662-g698: بود is the copula in a result clause and here carries a past/modal sense ('would be/was') as the outcome of the condition. تخت آباد is a nominal phrase 'the throne prosperous' (adjective after noun as in Persian). و links the two coordinated results; دهر 'age/world' is the subject of the second clause, and از تو شاد is a prepositional phrase 'glad because of you' showing cause ('از' = 'because of').
segments.s662-g699: چو introduces a temporal/general condition ('when/if'). نیکی نمایند is a subordinate clause with نیکی 'good deed/goodness' as object and نمایند a 3rd‑person plural verb form meaning 'they do/perform' (here 'people do good'); پاداش کن is an imperative 'give reward' (کن = 'do/give' in the imperative), with the recipient omitted as typical in Persian commands.
segments.s662-g700: ممان is a prohibitive/negative verb here meaning 'do not withhold' (negative imperative). تا introduces the purpose/result clause ('so that' / here functioning like 'lest' after a negative), شود is the light verb 'become/happen', and رنج نیکی کهن is a noun phrase 'the hardship/toil of long‑standing goodness' with کهن 'old/long‑standing' qualifying نیکی. The construction warns against withholding so that the long labours of goodness are not ruined.
segments.s662-g701: خردمند را places 'the wise person' as the definite/marked object (را) before the verb. شاد و نزدیک are predicative adjectives ('happy and near/close') and دار is the verb 'keep/hold' (imperative or prohibitive sense here: 'keep him...'); the object‑before‑verb order and coordinated adjectives are typical Persian syntactic patterns. Note that the provided lemma senses for دار in the word list are nominal, but here دار is verbal.
segments.s662-g702: This clause parallels the preceding one: جهان is the object, بر بداندیش is a prepositional phrase targeting 'against/for the ill‑minded' (بر meaning 'against/over' or 'toward'), تاریک is the adjectival complement 'dark', and دار is again the verb 'keep/make'. The sense: make the world dark (i.e. hard, obscure) for those of bad intent. As above, دار is verbal here and does not match the nominal senses listed.
segments.s662-g703: بهرکار is an adverbial/qualified term relating to 'work/affairs' and here functions like 'in business/for work, employ' (the poetic compression can render it 'in affairs' or 'use in work'). با مرد دانا سگال is a prepositional phrase 'with the wise man Sagal' specifying whom to keep or consult; the proper name سگال is the person to be relied upon.
segments.s662-g704: به رنج تن is a prepositional phrase 'at the cost of bodily toil' (به = 'by/with/at the cost of', رنج تن = 'the body's suffering'). از پادشاهی منال is a compact admonition 'do not make kingship your goal' — منال functions as 'goal/attainment' in the poetic phrasing, so the clause warns against seeking kingship (پادشاهی) as a goal if it requires bodily hardship.
segments.s662-g649: هم‌آواز here is a predicative adjective/adverb meaning 'in accord' or 'of one voice' and precedes the verb. شد is the simple past 3rd-person singular of شدن ('to become') used here to mean 'became' / 'came to be', so the whole clause reads 'the envoy came to be in agreement (with) the scribe'. با introduces the partner/instrument ('with').
segments.s662-g650: نبشتند is 3rd-person plural past ('they wrote'); despite initial ن it is not negative here (classical spelling). پس is an adverb 'then' linking to the previous action. نامه‌ای is an indefinite direct object ('a letter'), and بر + noun ('بر حریر') marks location/material: 'on/upon silk.' Word order is verb → object → locative phrase.
segments.s662-g651: دلارای here functions as a proper epithet/name ('beloved'/'Dularā') followed by عهدی ('of the covenant' / 'of the pledge'), forming a named phrase. ز is the poetic/prepositional 'from/of' linking that name to the following descriptive phrase; نوشین روان is a compound epithet ('nushīn' = 'sweet' modifying روان = 'soul, spirit'), i.e. 'sweet-souled.'
segments.s662-g652: به is the dative/prepositional marker 'to' (here indicating the addressee/recipient of the letter). هرمزد is a proper name. ناسالخورده is a compound adjective (نا- negative + سالخورده) meaning 'not-aged' → 'youthful', and جوان is an appositional reinforcing adjective 'young' or 'young man'.
segments.s662-g653: سرنامه ('opening/heading of the letter') is the locative/topic ('in the header' or 'at the start of the letter'). از + noun ('از دادگر') means 'about/of the just one', and کرد یاد is the fixed verbal phrase یاد کردن/کردن meaning 'to make mention of / to recall' (کرد here is the light verb 'did' that combines with یاد). The structure therefore means 'in the heading he mentioned/recalled the just ruler.'
segments.s662-g654: دگر here functions adverbially as 'again' or 'next' ('he then / furthermore'). گفت is the past verb 'said' (classical usage); کین is an archaic/demonstrative 'this/that' in this line (not the modern sense of 'vengeance'), and پند پور قباد is a genitive chain: پند 'advice' qualified by پور قباد 'the son of Qobad' (i.e. 'the advice of Qobad's son').
segments.s662-g655: بدان‌ای is the imperative 'know this' (بدان) plus the vocative marker ای ('O') attached to پسر 'son' — 'Know this, O son'. کین here is an archaic demonstrative 'this/that' pointing to the following clause. جهان is the subject 'the world', and بی‌وفاست is the predicate 'is faithless' (بی‌وفا + است; the copula is incorporated).
segments.s662-g656: پر از is the idiom 'full of' (پر + از). A series of nouns follows, joined by و: 'رنج و تیمار و درد' ('suffering / care / pain'), and the clause closes with بلاست (بلا + است) so that the whole predicate reads 'is full of suffering, care, pain, and calamity.' The copula 'است' is attached to the final noun as is typical in classical/poetic word order.
segments.s662-g489: کنون is an adverb meaning 'now' placed at the start. هرچ دانم is a relative-like construction: هرچ = 'whatever' and دانم = 'I know', so together 'whatever I know'. بپرسم is the 1st-person subjunctive/present used to express intent ('I shall ask' / 'I will ask'). ز introduces the topic 'about' (classical z = az).
segments.s662-g490: توپاسخ گزار is an address to the addressee: تو (you) + پاسخ گزار, where گزار here functions as a verbal/impositive 'report/relate' (imperative/agentive sense). آنچ = 'آن‌چه' 'that which/whatever'; آیدت is 'comes to you' (آید + enclitic ت); یاد = 'memory' so آنچ آیدت یاد = 'whatever comes to your mind'.
segments.s662-g491: ز here is 'of/among' (classical preposition); فرزند = 'child/son'. کو is an interrogative/relative particle (classical usage) linking to the question — here equivalent to 'which/who among'. بر + پدر ارجمند = 'toward/regarding the honored father' (بر = 'toward/on/in relation to'). Word order is classical: topic ('from the children') + relative particle + prepositional phrase.
segments.s662-g492: کدامست is the interrogative 'which is it' (کدام + است); شایسته is the predicate adjective 'worthy'; و = 'and'; بی‌گزند = 'without hurt/unscathed' (an adjective). Together they form the main question: 'which is worthy and unscathed?'.
segments.s662-g493: ببخشایش + دل is a prefixed/adjectival construction meaning 'a forgiving heart' (ب- + بخشایش acting adjectivally); سزاوار = 'deserving/fit'; کیست = 'who is' (کی + است) — so 'Who is deserving of a forgiving heart?'.
segments.s662-g494: که introduces the subordinate/explanatory clause 'that/so that'. بر درد او = 'over/for his pain' (بر = 'over/on', درد = 'sorrow/pain', او = 'his'). بر بباید گریست is a classical modal-verb phrase: بباید = 'one ought/must' and گریست = infinitive/verb 'to weep' (classical form), so 'that one ought to weep over his pain'.
segments.s662-g495: ز = 'by/from/because of' indicating cause; کردار نیکی is a genitive phrase 'deed(s) of goodness' (good deeds). پشیمان is a predicate/adjectival complement 'regretful/repentant'. کراست is an archaic/perfective auxiliary (classical contraction of 'کرد است' or similar) giving the resultive perfect: 'has made/has become' — here rendering 'has been made regretful' or 'has become regretful'.
segments.s662-g496: که = 'for/that' linking the explanation. دل is subject 'the heart'. بر پشیمانی او = 'to/for his repentance' (بر = 'regarding/with respect to', پشیمانی = 'repentance/regret', او = 'his'). گواست here functions as 'is witness' (classical shortened form of 'گواه است'), so 'for the heart is witness to his repentance.'
segments.s662-g473: چنین داد پاسخ is a poetic inversion of the more common modern order 'چنین پاسخ داد' — چنین 'thus' + داد 'gave' + پاسخ 'answer'. که introduces a result/relative clause ('that / whoever...'), and 'هرکس که داد' is a free relative clause ('whoever gave') with the verb داد (past of دادن) placed after the relative pronoun, as is frequent in Persian poetry.
segments.s662-g474: This clause gives the result: 'بداد از تن خود همو بود شاد' — بداد here is lexically difficult/ambiguous in the list and functions as the action/thing given (see translation); از تن خود is a prepositional phrase 'from/out of his own body/person', خود and همو emphasize 'himself', and بود شاد is a simple past copula + adjective 'was happy'. The clause therefore states the consequence for that person: he himself was glad.
segments.s662-g475: نگه کرد is a compound where نگه 'gaze/looking' combines with کرد 'did/made' to mean 'turned his gaze / looked'. The verb precedes the subject in this poetic line; پرسنده is the agent 'the questioner' and بوزرجمهر is the target of the look (either apposed name or the one being looked at).
segments.s662-g476: بدان is the prepositional 'to/at that' (written here as ب + دان in the segmentation); it points the gaze to the following epithets. پاکدل, مهتر, خوب‌چهر are a sequence of attributive epithets ('pure‑hearted', 'distinguished/elder/chief', 'fair‑faced') describing Bozorgmehr; there is no copula — they are in apposition to the person indicated by بدان.
segments.s662-g477: بدو گفت is 'he said to him' with the indirect object pronoun بدو placed before the verb گفت (past 'said'); کز introduces a content clause ('of/which') and گفتنی هرچ هست literally means 'whatever there is to be said' — گفتنی here = 'that which is to be said' and هست is the copula/auxiliary that closes the clause.
segments.s662-g478: بگویم is a first‑person form 'I will tell / I shall tell' (present-subjunctive/intentional use) introducing the speaker's offer. تو بشمر is an instruction to the addressee ('you count'), with بشمر as the verb; یکایک 'one by one' modifies بشمر and بدست 'by hand' is an adverbial phrase specifying the manner of counting.
segments.s662-g479: سراسر همه 'throughout / entirely all' intensifies پرسشم 'my questions' (پرسشم = پرسش + possessive ـم = 'my questions'). یادگیر here is an imperative/command 'learn / remember' (verb) directed at the addressee — the phrase orders the listener to commit all the questions to memory.
segments.s662-g480: به پاسخ همه is a prepositional phrase 'with / as to the answers to all' (i.e., 'in answering them all'); داد here functions as the notion of 'giving/doing' (the act of answering) and بنیاد گیر is a verbal phrase 'take as foundation' (بنیاد 'foundation' + گیر 'take/seize' — گیر used transitively: 'make X your basis'). The whole clause advises: make answering all those questions your firm foundation.
segments.s662-g449: کنون is an adverb placed sentence-initially meaning 'now'. موبدان و ردان is a coordinated noun phrase (موبدان 'mōbedān' + و + ردان) followed by the object marker را; the verb بخواه is a 2nd-person singular/plural imperative of خواستن placed at the end, giving the command 'summon/ask for'. The word-order (Adverb — Object — Verb) and the enclitic را marking the definite object are typical of classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s662-g450: کسی کو is a classical relative expression 'someone who' (کو here functioning as a relative particle). کند is a present-tense 3rd-person singular verb ('does / turns') used to introduce the relative clause. سوی دانش نگاه is a prepositional phrase 'toward knowledge' plus نگاه 'gaze/look' — together 'turns his gaze toward knowledge'. The clause modifies whom to summon: 'someone who directs his gaze to learning.'
segments.s662-g451: بخوانیدش is the plural imperative بخوانید ('call' / 'bring') with the enclitic ـش 'him/it' attached, so 'call him' or 'bring him'. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. آزمایش is a noun meaning 'test' and کنید is the 2nd-person plural imperative of کردن 'do' — together 'and test (him)'. The sequence is a pair of imperatives addressing the assembled: 'Call him and test him.'
segments.s662-g452: هنر بر هنر بر فزایش کنید is an emphatic, poetic imperative: هنر ('skill/art') repeated with the preposition بر 'upon' gives the sense 'skill upon skill', and فزایش کنید is the plural imperative 'increase (it)'. The doubled بر adds rhetorical piling-up: literally 'cause an increase of skill upon skill' — 'let skill be increased again and again.'
segments.s662-g453: شدند is the simple past 3rd-person plural of شدن 'they became / they were', here functioning as 'they came to be' or 'they formed'. اندران is a poetic locative 'among them' (اندر + ان). موبدان انجمن ('the mobeds' + 'assembly') follows: together the clause means 'the mobeds formed an assembly among them.' The verb preceding the locative phrase is a common poetic inversion.
segments.s662-g454: ز هر در is a poetic expression 'from every door/quarter' (ز = from; هر = every; در = door/gate, used idiomatically for 'place/quarter'). پژوهنده is an agent noun 'an inquirer / seeker (of knowledge)'. و رای زن pairs another agentive phrase: رای 'counsel/opinion' plus زن forming an agentive nominal (one who utters or gives counsel), so together 'an inquirer and a counselor.' The clause describes people coming from every quarter to the assembly.
segments.s662-g455: جهانجوی is an epithet 'world-seeking' or 'ambitious' placed before the name it modifies. هرمزد is the personal name; را is the definite object marker. خواندند is the past 3rd-person plural 'they called/summoned' (here 'they summoned Hormozd'). The epithet + name + را + خواندند sequence is a normal ornamental way to report a summons.
segments.s662-g456: بر is the preposition 'upon/on/to' indicating placement; نامدارنش is a possessive/attributive form meaning 'their renowned/famed ones' or 'the renowned (of theirs)' and functions as the locus where the summoned man is placed; بنشاندند is the past 3rd-person plural causative 'they seated/installed (him)'. Together: 'they set him among their renowned (men).' Note the archaic possessive suffix and the causative verb بنشاندند.
segments.s662-g545: دگر is a transition word here meaning “another” (introducing a new point). آنک is the relative “that/which,” introducing a clause describing what follows. بر جای is a prepositional phrase “in its place / appropriately” (بر precedes جای as in classical Persian), and بخشایست is the noun بخشایش (forgiveness/pardon) plus the archaic copula -ست = “is,” so the segment says “that which in its place is pardon.”
segments.s662-g546: برو here is an archaic adversative/contrastive particle (introducing a counterpoint: “but/and as for”). چشم را is the object with the definite marker را (literally “the eye”), and جای پالایشست places the predicate at the end: جای = “place/role” and پالایشست = پالایش (purification/refinement) + -ست (“is”), so “as for the eye, its place is purification.”
segments.s662-g547: بزرگی here denotes “a great one / greatness” used as the subject. که is the relative pronoun “who/that,” and بختش is the possessed noun “his fortune/fate.” پراگنده is a predicate adjective “scattered,” followed by گشت (from گشتن) = “became/turned,” so literally “a great one whose fortune became scattered.”
segments.s662-g548: به پیش یکی is a prepositional phrase meaning “before/in the presence of a certain one” (به + پیش + one). ناسزا بنده گشت is an idiomatic sequence: ناسزا (“abuse/insult”) together with بنده (“servant/slave” used as a predicate noun) and گشت (“became”), giving the sense “became an object/target of insult” or “became an abused/servile figure.” Word order follows classical Persian: circumstance (به پیش یکی) before the predicate (ناسزا بنده گشت).
segments.s662-g549: ز کار وی is a causal/locative phrase “from/because of his deed” (ز = poetic “from/because of,” کار وی = “his act”). ار is the conditional “if.” خون خروشی is a compound noun (“blood‑roaring,” i.e. bloodshed/violent bloodletting), and رواست = روا + ست is the predicative “is fitting/permissible,” so this clause reads “if, because of his deed, bloodshed is permissible/fitting.”
segments.s662-g550: که introduces an explanatory clause “that/because.” ناپارسایی is the noun “impiety / moral unworthiness.” برو here is an archaic contraction meaning roughly “بر او” (= “against him / toward him”) used as a prepositional phrase; it is not one of the modern senses listed. پادشاست is the older orthography of پادشاه است (pādšāh ast) or can be read as referring to the sovereign/reign; the phrase therefore links the permissibility of bloodshed to the fact that impiety against him concerns the sovereign (i.e. is an offence to the king), giving the explanatory sense “for impiety against him concerns the sovereign.”
segments.s662-g551: دگر here is a discourse marker “again/another point.” هر که is the indefinite relative “whoever.” با مردم is the prepositional phrase “toward/with people,” and ناسپاس is the adjectival predicate “ungrateful” describing that person (classical placement of the adjective after the noun phrase).
segments.s662-g552: کند is the present/past general-form verb “does/makes” (here causing a result). نیکویی is the object “goodness/virtue.” ماند is the verb “remains / is left,” so together کند ماند expresses “causes to remain” or “leaves (something) in a state.” اندر هراس is an adverbial phrase “in fear / in dread.” Word order follows classical Persian: verb(s) follow the subject and the object and final adverbial phrase.
segments.s662-g401: The first 'زمان' topicalizes/time-adverbializes the idea ('for a time'). 'خواهم' is the 1st-person present/future auxiliary ('I will/want') used with the following verbal phrase. 'ازکرد' is an archaic poetic form from از کردن meaning 'to remove/put aside' (here: 'set aside'); it combines with 'خواهم' to mean 'I will set aside'. 'گار زمان' is a genitival phrase 'the work/affairs of Time' (گار = work/deed, followed by زمان = time) and is the object of 'ازکرد'.
segments.s662-g402: 'که' introduces a subordinate/purpose clause ('so that'). 'چندی' means 'for a while' (duration). 'بماند' is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive ('may remain' / 'might stay') expressing desired/resultant state. 'دلم' = 'my heart' (دل + enclitic -م). The separate particle 'م' listed in the text is the reduced/copular element (from هست) linking the subject to the predicate. 'شادمان' is the predicate adjective 'happy/joyful'.
segments.s662-g403: 'که' again introduces or continues a clause ('that' / 'for'). 'این' is the demonstrative 'these/the foregoing'. 'داستانها' is the plural 'tales/stories'. 'و' = 'and'. 'چندین' = 'several/many'. 'سخن' here means 'words/sayings/tales' (a poetic term for utterances or stories) and pairs with 'چندین' to mean 'many a saying/word'.
segments.s662-g404: 'گذشته' is a past participle/adjective 'having passed/gone'. 'برو' is a poetic attachment of بر (lit. 'over') used with 'گذشته' to form 'having passed over/through'. 'سال' = 'years' (the time span). 'و' = 'and'. 'گشته' is the perfect/participial form 'become/has turned', and 'کهن' is the adjective 'ancient/old' — together 'and have become old/ancient'. The phrase is a compact past-result description with inversion common in poetry.
segments.s662-g405: The preposition 'ز' (poetic form of از) = 'from'. 'هنگام' = 'the time/season'. 'کی' here functions as part of the dynastic title (Kay/Kei) and with 'شاه' yields 'کی‌شاه' = 'Kay‑shah' (a lineage/title), rather than the interrogative 'when/who'. 'تا' = 'until/up to', and 'یزدگرد' is the proper name Yazdegerd. The whole phrase marks the chronological span 'from the time of the Kay‑shahs to Yazdegerd'.
segments.s662-g406: 'ز' again = 'from/of'. 'لفظ من' = 'my utterance/word' (لفظ = utterance/word + من = my). 'آمد' is the past 'came' used with the following predicate to mean 'have come to be / have turned out'. 'پراگنده' = 'scattered/dispersed'. 'گرد' here is an archaic/verbal element (from گردیدن/گشتن) forming the verbal predicate 'became/scattered' — so 'from my words came (i.e. have) been scattered'.
segments.s662-g407: 'بپیوندم' is 1st-person singular present/subjunctive (from پیوستن) used here with a future sense 'I will join / attach myself'. 'و' = 'and'. 'باغ' is the noun 'garden' followed by the adjective 'بی‌خو' ('without sleep' = sleepless). 'کنم' is 1st-person singular present/subjunctive of کردن used causatively 'I will make' — together 'I will join and make the garden sleepless' (a poetic way of saying he will keep the court/gathering awake or enliven the place).
segments.s662-g408: 'سخنهای' is the plural genitive/accusative 'the words/tales'. 'شاهنشهان' is the plural/genitive form referring to 'shahanshahs' (emperors); in older/poetic usage this is the genitive of شاهنشاه. 'نو' = 'new'. 'کنم' again is 1st‑person singular 'I will make' — together 'I will make (or renew) the words/tales of the emperors anew.'

sentences.s662-s177: From there an army came toward Ctesiphon; its rear and its front were inside.
sentences.s662-s178: All, entirely prosperous with silver and gold, in golden garments and in golden belts.
sentences.s662-s179: Because of so many silken standards of the chiefs, you said the air had become all silk.
sentences.s662-s180: You said that in court and field they had become golden, and that the belts, from jewels, had become like Parvin.
sentences.s662-s105: The messenger said, "I will not endure this hardship, since you have shortened my way to the treasure."
sentences.s662-s106: He came to the wise man at night, and from that the shoemaker also opened his lips.
sentences.s662-s107: Bozorgmehr became glad before the king; thereupon, at the king's request, he uncovered his face.
sentences.s662-s108: He said thus afterward: 'May I not give praise to God unless I am pure and God‑knowing.'
sentences.s662-s385: Many wept over that letter; after the lapse of one year he lived again.
sentences.s662-s386: He departed, and these words remained as a memorial; keep this memorial, you, as a warning.
sentences.s662-s387: Now, after this, I will adorn the crown of Ahura Mazda, O king, and at the appointed time place (it / him).
sentences.s662-s205: Since my heart cannot bear suffering, if I were to pass away from this sponge‑like dwelling.
sentences.s662-s206: What can a clear‑souled man say now about the sovereign’s sweet‑spirited counsel?
sentences.s662-s207: When years came to seventy and four, the king became pensive about death.
sentences.s662-s208: A worldly wayfarer sought to become a village headman — one who would be the first to put on the robe given.
sentences.s662-s161: We are wholly the dust of your toil; we are all the guardians of your treasure.
sentences.s662-s162: May the king become very pleased with us; may we not be unsuccessful and ill‑fated.
sentences.s662-s163: Because of the hardship the shahanshah endured here, not all the Romans have that wisdom.
sentences.s662-s164: We fill ten cowhides with dinars and bring them into the treasury from the gate and the like.
sentences.s662-s217: At that time the king said to Bozorgmehr, "I have a secret kept hidden."
sentences.s662-s218: When seventy years had passed, his head and hair, once dark, turned like camphor.
sentences.s662-s219: When I pass from this spongy dwelling, the world will require one chief (a headman).
sentences.s662-s220: — one who shows generosity to the dervish, to strangers, and to his own people.
sentences.s662-s85: In these towns there is a skilful roaming collector who takes more than a dirham from anyone who has it in hand.
sentences.s662-s86: From merchants and from peasants, if you ask for a loan of a dirham they will not become sullen.
sentences.s662-s87: On this matter the king agreed, and the wise man of Iran recited the tale.
sentences.s662-s88: He sent for Bozorgmehr, the wise, joyful‑hearted and fair‑faced man.
sentences.s662-s165: He rules in small and great matters; he will accept (it) from us, although that is an insult.
sentences.s662-s166: He answered thus: because the treasure was gained by work, it ought to be put in order with toil.
sentences.s662-s167: All the Romans went before the mobed, clamoring, and under an unlucky star they were afflicted.
sentences.s662-s168: They spoke abundantly at every gate; they drove away all of Caesar's secrets.
sentences.s662-s289: Another asked, “What is difficult? Who is the harmless one whose heart is full of song?”
sentences.s662-s290: When evil was, and an evil-maker sat with him; there was a certain life like a کبست.
sentences.s662-s291: Another who says, “Who is a true witness?” — for life and wisdom are برگوا برگوا (an obscure/echoing phrase).
sentences.s662-s292: I have seen no witness better than experience; a witness is a speaker and a ruler.
sentences.s662-s369: Inscribe upon my palace my greatness, my treasure, and my host (army).
sentences.s662-s370: Scatter (or provide) abundantly every kind — and likewise bring things of colour, scent, and of scattered adornment.
sentences.s662-s371: Anoint the body with camphor to make it rich; from zamshak (a fragrant plant) make an afsar (headpiece/crest) over a rag.
sentences.s662-s372: Bring five pieces of rich gold-woven brocade; bring from the treasury things the eye has not yet seen.
sentences.s662-s285: If he is wealthy, cover his tent; if he is a dervish, strive with him.
sentences.s662-s286: Whoever is more humble and more generous, the hearts of his friends are the more joyful toward him.
sentences.s662-s287: Another (one) asks: 'Who is the enemy?' — the one from whom the heart is always in pain and is a scourge.
sentences.s662-s288: When his tongue is insolent, his speech turns bad; from his words an enemy comes — and that is deserved.
sentences.s662-s321: O you old, decrepit, unrepentant man — take wisdom and turn away from feasting and joy.
sentences.s662-s322: The world was renewed when you obtained the goblet; because of your repentance you made the soul shine.
sentences.s662-s323: What did that aged poet say when he recalled the soul's sweet counsel?
sentences.s662-s324: When the words of Ahura Mazda were seen thus, a certain new mobed then uttered a fresh speech.
sentences.s662-s141: He had presented thirty thousand dinars as an offering to the king from each person.
sentences.s662-s142: When they saw the color of the king's face, they left trembling and twisting like serpents.
sentences.s662-s143: When the shah saw them, he greeted/entertained them, and according to custom appointed them to one position.
sentences.s662-s144: Thus said the leading speaker: 'O king, the Caesar is young and new.'
sentences.s662-s101: The shoemaker said to him, 'O fair‑faced one, tell Bozorgmehr, even if it costs you hardship.'
sentences.s662-s102: For in this age I am but a child, and I do not hold his trade cheap in my heart.
